title: Annotation handoff & backfill playpen
preview:
Purpose:
Quick working notes for shipping a fast backfill/handoff flow to get legacy unlabeled data into the new adjudication queue. This is a scratchpad — ideas, tiny scripts, and TODOs. Not final.

Context:
- We have ~1.2M legacy rows with partial metadata (session_id, raw_text, source).
- Existing labeling UI expects canonical_id and normalized text; legacy rows frequently miss canonical_id and contain noisy punctuation, XML fragments, or truncated sentences.
- Need a low-friction backfill that: (1) dedupes obvious duplicates, (2) canonicalizes IDs where possible, (3) surfaces a small, high-signal subset for human adjudication, and (4) routes remainder to a cheaper microtask path.

Goals (metrics to aim for):
- Human adjudication queue: 10–15k highest-uncertainty items per week (manageable throughput for two contractors).
- Automated/cheap microtasks: 60–75% of legacy pool after simple rules + model scoring.
- Duplicate reduction: remove >= 30% of exact/text-normalized duplicates before any human work.
- Average label time (human): <= 45s per item via tightened UI and short-context presentation.

High-level pipeline (sketch):
1) Lightweight normalization pass (batch job, 1h window):
   - Lowercase, normalize whitespace, collapse repeated punctuation, strip common boilerplate ("<div>", "Read more:").
   - Small emoji strip/convert step: remove trailing emoji-only signatures.
   - Output: normalized_text, norm_hash.

2) Exact & soft dedupe stage (SQL + embedding):
   - Exact dedupe by norm_hash.
   - Soft dedupe: generate 384-dim embedding (existing embed model v2). Compare cos_sim > 0.93 => mark as near-duplicate.
   - Heuristic: if near-duplicate cluster size > 5 and 80% of cluster have label X in older dataset, auto-assign label X with provenance tag.

3) ID reconciliation attempt (best-effort):
   - Try canonical_id mapping via three lookups (fast): document_id table, session->user mapping, and fuzzy title match.
   - If mapping ambiguous (>1 candidate above threshold), mark for manual ID-handoff microtask (separate small flow).

4) Model scoring + triage rules (rank by uncertainty/impact):
   - Run lightweight classifier for label candidates (use small distilled model to save cost).
   - Compute uncertainty = max_entropy(label_probs).
   - Score = uncertainty * importance_weight(source, recency) + dedupe_penalty.
   - Top N uncertain items => human adjudication queue. Next band => microtasks (multi-choice with 3 options), remainder => auto-label + review-sample.

Microtask design notes:
- Keep prompt short: show canonical context (one previous turn if exists), highlight token spans with highest model attention score (precomputed).
- 3-option tasks (A / B / Abstain). Abstain routes to adjudication.
- Small batch size for workers: present 8 items at once so they can maintain context and pace.
- Qualification test: 90% accuracy on 20 golden items to continue.

Adjudication UX improvements (to hit 45s goal):
- Strip header chrome, prefill metadata, show quick-action hotkeys (1/2/3 for choices, r for reopen, c for comment).
- Show provenance trail: show up to 3 previous labels assigned, with confidence and model version.
- Inline quick-merge for near-duplicates (merge cluster) so adjudicator can resolve multiple items in one go.

Corner cases & heuristics (notes to self):
- Truncated sentences often end with mid-token hyphen or "...". If tokenization shows a very short last token (<3 chars) and endswith('-') or '...', nudge to microtask for recovery.
- XML-ish fragments: if text contains '<' followed by letters and '>' and length < 300, try an XML-strip; if resulting sentence is empty, mark as 'source-noise' and route to discard-sample.
- Numeric-heavy rows: if >60% characters are digits/punctuation, don't send to standard UI — use numeric-label microflow.
- Multilingual snippets: detect lang via fastlang; if low confidence (<0.6) and length > 20 characters, route to bilingual microtask.

Operational/runbook snippets (for SRE handoff):
- Backfill workers run as k8s jobs in batch namespace (labeler-backfill-v2).
- Pod resources: cpu 2, mem 6Gi, gpu none. Each pod processes ~25k rows / hour. Scale to 20 pods for parallel.
- Logs: stdout structured JSON with keys: row_id, norm_hash, cluster_id, action (auto-label|microtask|adjudicate).
- Failure mode: if more than 3 crashes in 10m, mark job as flaky -> pause scale up, create ticket ENG-xxxx and attach last 50 log lines.

Quick SQL snippets (ad hoc):
- Normalization dedupe sample:
  WITH norm AS (SELECT id, LOWER(REGEXP_REPLACE(text, '\\s+', ' ', 'g')) AS norm_text FROM legacy_table)
  SELECT COUNT(*) FROM (SELECT norm_text, COUNT(*) c FROM norm GROUP BY norm_text HAVING c > 1) t;

- Embedding candidate pull:
  SELECT id, text FROM legacy_table WHERE created_at > '2023-01-01' ORDER BY RANDOM() LIMIT 10000;

Edge decisions to write down (open):
- Auto-assign on high-consensus clusters: threshold? 0.8 cluster purity sounds reasonable but need product buy-in.
- How large should adjudication weekly cap be? Start small (10k) and iterate.
- Should we version-backfill outputs separately so model training can ignore auto-assigned labels? yes: add label_source = backfill_v1_auto.

Mini scripts (psuedocode) to copy into labeler-sidecar PR later:
- dedupe_clusterer.py: group by norm_hash then run approximate nearest neighbors on embeddings (faiss index) and emit cluster_id TSV.
- triage_ranker.py: load classifier, compute entropy score, output score CSV for scheduler.

Deployment checklist for handoff iteration 0:
- [ ] Write small normalization lambda and run on 1k test slice.
- [ ] Build embedding job and check near-duplicate rate.
- [ ] Create microtask template and run closed pilot with 50 golden items.
- [ ] Instrument adjudication UI metrics (time_per_item, click_hotkey_rate).
- [ ] Add provenance flags and stable label_source tags.

Open questions / follow-ups:
- Lina: can we get additional contractor capacity for adjudication starting Jan? (ask recruiting)
- Product: what is acceptable auto-assign risk threshold? (needs OK from Sam)
- Research: try 2x smaller embed dim — cost vs dedupe recall tradeoff.

Notes to myself: keep this doc informal. When things stabilize, move a cleaned / ops-ready version to shared_drives/company/misc or product/data-curation. For now, quick iteration + cheap pilots.

Timestamped micro-entries (scratch):
- 2025-11-05 09:12 — ran normalization on slice-1 (10k): dedupe removed 28% rows, good.
- 2025-11-07 16:04 — embedding clusterer prototype: FAISS PQ recall ~0.88 on near-duplicate golden set.
- 2025-11-11 11:50 — microtask UI mock looks clean; hotkeys working in prototype.

End of playpen notes.

Comments: feel free to copy any SQL or tiny scripts to GH PR branch labeler-sidecar/backfill-experiment and reference ENG-2143.

title: CapEx commitment review 1:1 - prep scratchpad
preview:
Meeting: 1:1 prep with Procurement lead (Lina) — purpose: reconcile open capex commitments vs approved spend, clear odd approvals, align on near-term PO cadence.

Context notes (tl;dr):
- Q4 capex commitments creeping above plan; forecast vs committed gap ~ $420k. Need to decide which POs we can push or re-negotiate.
- A handful of vendor invoices are in flight but missing SOW sign-off (see FIN-2789).
- Dedicated infra appliance purchase (EdgeCluster-3) has flexible ship date but sticky vendor cancellation fee ($45k) if we delay past 2025-12-01.

Agenda (30 min):
1) Quick run of numbers (5m) — highlight top 5 commitments by $ (EdgeCluster-3, GPU-resale, Monitoring-lic, Rack+Install, Security-audit).
2) Items to question for deferral/cut (10m) — list candidate POs + risk note.
3) Procurement approval queue sanity check (5m) — any >$100k stuck on legal/KMS?
4) Actioning: owners & dates (10m) — who asks vendor for date push, who re-issues revised SOW, who escalates to EngPM.

Key figures (quick):
- Total committed capex (open POs): $1,820,000
- Approved in plan for remainder of year: $1,400,000
- Delta: +$420,000 (need containment or reclassification to opex)
- Immediate liquidity headroom for capex: $300k (finance reserve)
- Cancellation fee thresholds: EdgeCluster-3 = $45k (deadline 2025-12-01)

Candidates for deferral (notes):
- EdgeCluster-3 (PO #ECL-3321) — medium risk to timeline, vendor willing to push 30d for nominal fee. Ask: negotiate full waiver? Owner: Lina.
- GPU-resale contract (Vendor: FerroAI, PO #FER-1102) — high spend, tied to Dedicated capacity ramp. Ops says can trim 10% of units, ask Eng for updated perf targets. Owner: Ethan.
- Monitoring licensed modules (PO #MON-771) — license start tied to install date; can delay start without penalty if invoice not processed. Low risk. Owner: Aisha (follow up to AP).

Procurement approvals — red flags:
- FIN-2789: SOW missing signature from Legal for vendor 'SecureLattice' (security-audit). If legal signs late, invoice could clear next month and blow the run-rate. Need legal ETA.
- PO approvals stuck on KMS onboarding (KMS-PLAT-5). If not resolved, hardware encryption key rotation will delay deployments, may force reclassification to cloud spend.

Unresolved questions to raise in 1:1:
- Which two POs are highest priority to keep on current schedule (oppo cost if delayed)?
- Is there room to reclassify portion of EdgeCluster-3 to opex (rental/lease)? Any vendor options?
- Can procurement batch smaller POs to reduce AP processing overhead this quarter? (saves headcount + latency)

Talking points / scripts (short):
- "I want to focus on where we can buy time without adding cost — vendor-negotiable items first."
- "If legal can't sign SecureLattice this week, we should pre-authorize partial testing to avoid infra idle time — is that acceptable?"
- "For GPU units: if engineering trims 10% and we defer 2 racks, does capacity still hit our customer SLAs?"

Immediate action items (post-1:1):
- Lina: confirm vendor push for EdgeCluster-3; negotiate fee waiver or extend ship date; due 2025-11-14.
- Ethan: get EngPM thumbs on GPU-resale trim proposal; reply with risk assessment by 2025-11-12.
- Aisha (me): hold off AP on Monitoring module invoice, confirm invoice hold code with AP (ticket AP-4590); due 2025-11-11.
- Legal: expedite SecureLattice review; request prioritized review and provide redline by 2025-11-13 (owner: Legal Ops).
- Finance ops: model scenario A/B (defer EdgeCluster vs accelerate opex) and show P&L impact for monthly run-rate by 2025-11-15.

Follow-ups to capture in meeting notes:
- Confirm who will create small-ticket bundling process for AP.
- Capture vendor contact + escalation path for each of top-5 PO items.
- Flag any procurement items that need executive sign-off > $500k.

Notes-to-self:
- Bring up 'reclassification to opex' approach only if we have signed vendor confirmation on lease options. Avoid flagging as primary plan.
- Keep the tone collaborative; procurement likely has limited levers — ask about quick wins (batching, invoice hold flags) first.

Quick links to reference in meeting:
- Capex commitments sheet: https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1a2b3c-capex-commitments (tab: open_pos)
- JIRA ticket (procurement queue): FIN-2789
- Confluence summary of Q4 capex plan: /pages/viewpage.action?pageId=98765432

End scratchpad — convert any agreed decisions into PO-level notes after meeting and update capex sheet.

title: Anchored session credit stitchpad
preview:
Purpose:
Quick personal scratchpad for an anchored session-credit model that blends PAYG token metering with light session commitments. Aim: reduce perceived billing friction for interactive apps (chat UX) while preserving unit-econ clarity and limiting subsidy exposure.

Context / motivation:
- Lots of product teams want a simple UX: "buy session credits" rather than thinking about tokens. But we need to map credits back to tokens and GPU seconds to understand cost.
- Want a middle ground between flat subs and pure payg: small upfront anchors (cheap) that cover a guaranteed baseline of concurrency and warm KV caches, plus per-token taper after anchor is consumed.

Key primitives (names I use here):
- Anchor: upfront small credit (micro-commit) purchased on first significant use. Covers warmups, short prefixes, and a buffer for bursts.
- Session credit: unit consumed per user session start; convertible to N tokens at nominal efficiency.
- Burst reserve: additional temp credits drawn when sustained throughput spikes above baseline; bill at higher marginal rate.
- Decay window: how long an anchor remains attached to a user (sliding TTL).

Design constraints / heuristics:
- Keep math simple on billing page: 1 session credit ~ "covers first X tokens & warm cache"; then tokens billed at PAYG rate after.
- Anchors should pay for KV-cache warm percentile (say first 90s of conversation activity) because warm-up cost is heavy.
- Avoid per-utterance micro-billing (UX friction). Prefer per-session + fallback to tokens.
- Give developers tools to tune anchor size by intended UX (low-latency stream vs high-quality long-form).

Rough unit-econ back-of-envelope (example numbers):
Assumptions:
- GPU cost to us (amortized): $2.00 / GPU-hour (reserved infra mix).
- Average throughput for interactive model variant: 25 tokens/sec per GPU (steady-state).
- Effective tokens/hour per GPU: 25 * 3600 = 90,000 tokens/hr.
- Implied cost per token = $2.00 / 90,000 = $0.0000222/token (~0.00222 cents/token).
- Add infra overhead + sw/service margin, round to internal baseline unit cost = $0.00004/token.

Session anchoring math:
- Typical early-session behavior: first 60s of back-and-forth dominates warm cost and has high per-token cost due to cold KV and startup. If average burst during that minute is 7 tokens/s -> 420 tokens.
- At internal cost $0.00004/token, that 420 tokens = $0.0168 of infra cost. Round up to $0.02 to cover extra overhead and logging.
- UX-friendly anchor price to customer: anchor retail price = $0.10 (psychologically round). That leaves room for discounts, margins, and platform fees.
- Map: 1 anchor credit => covers first 400 tokens (approx) + KV warm. After 400 tokens, continue at PAYG consumer price.

Customer-facing pricing mapping (example):
- 1 anchor credit = $0.10 (covers first 400 tokens)
- PAYG token add-on = $0.00025/token (retail) after anchor consumed
- Burst surcharge (if > 4x baseline sustained > 30s) = +30% on token rate for that span (discourages unbounded burst abuse)

Why these numbers?
- Retail token price is deliberately 6x internal baseline to get sensible gross margin and leave room for commissions/discounting.
- Anchor is priced where a reasonable fraction of short sessions (<=400 tokens) feels "free" relative to user expectation but still accumulates revenue.

Behavioral mechanics and TTLs:
- Anchor attaches to a user identifier for 14 days idle TTL. If the user returns within 14 days, next session consumes anchor leftover first.
- Session boundaries: either explicit client-side session start event or 30 minutes inactivity cutoff.
- Anchor top-ups: customer can buy bundles of anchors (10 anchors for $0.85) -> encourages commit.

Edge cases and fraud protections:
- Shared API keys: anchors are per-key and per-user metadata where available. If API key used at high concurrency from many IPs, throttle anchor redemption and route to PAYG.
- Cache-warm abuse: detect too-frequent tiny sessions (5s chats) from same key; degrade anchor eligibility after threshold.

Conversion and reporting needs:
- Billing UI should show: anchors bought, anchors consumed (with token equivalence), tokens billed PAYG, surge charges.
- Expose to customers per-route counters so they can map anchors to features (chat vs embeddings).

Alternate variants (notes):
- Time-anchored approach: anchor covers first N seconds of compute (instead of tokens). Maps more directly to GPU-seconds cost but is harder to communicate to customers.
- Concurrency-anchored: anchors give a small sustained concurrency reservation for 1 minute to preserve latency SLOs (sell as "fast-response credit").
- Sliding anchor: anchors drip credits over 24h; good for low-frequency users.

Quick sensitivity checks (how fragile is unit econ):
- If internal GPU cost rises 2x -> internal cost per token ~ $0.00008 -> anchor internal cost ~ $0.04 -> need to raise anchor price or shrink covered tokens.
- If average session length increases to 2k tokens, anchors will be consumed less often relative to session length; retail anchor subsidizes many sessions but contributes small fraction of revenue -> measure ratio anchors_consume_tokens / tokens_billed.

Open questions / TODOs:
- Should anchors expire more quickly for trial-tier customers? probably yes (reduce abuse).
- Exact mapping of tokens->anchor: flat token bucket vs proportional depletion (I prefer flat bucket for UX).
- Integrate with quota system: anchors reduce burst protection triggers temporarily.
- Simulate cohort economics for 6 months: need sample of session length distribution from prod (pull from analytics).
- Decide whether anchor purchase is user-level or org-level (org-level better for seat-sharing customers).

Small implementation notes for infra team: 
- On request path, check anchor state first; if anchor has >= remaining_tokens_threshold, mark request as "anchor-covered" and route to cheaper warming node pool.
- Instrument events: ANCHOR_CONSUMED, ANCHOR_TOPUP, ANCHOR_EXPIRED with token counts.
- Add guard rails to ensure anchor can't be used to cover long-running streaming generation indefinitely (cap per-output > 2k tokens shifts to PAYG).

Closing thought:
Anchors look promising as a low-friction mental model for customers building chat features. Key is to keep customer messaging simple ("each session starts with one free-ish credit") while maintaining discoverable metrics so teams can reconcile usage. Need to run sensitivity sims before committing to pricing buckets.

- Want a middle ground between flat subs and pure payg: small upfront anchors (cheap) that cover a guaranteed baseline of concurrency and warm KV caches, plus per-token taper after anchor is consumed.

Key primitives (names I use here):
- Anchor: upfront small credit (micro-commit) purchased on first significant use. Covers warmups, short prefixes, and a buffer for bursts.
- Session credit: unit consumed per user session start; convertible to N tokens at nominal efficiency.
- Burst reserve: additional temp credits drawn when sustained throughput spikes above baseline; bill at higher marginal rate.
- Decay window: how long an anchor remains attached to a user (sliding TTL).

Design constraints / heuristics:
- Keep math simple on billing page: 1 session credit ~ "covers first X tokens & warm cache"; then tokens billed at PAYG rate after.
- Anchors should pay for KV-cache warm percentile (say first 90s of conversation activity) because warm-up cost is heavy.
- Avoid per-utterance micro-billing (UX friction). Prefer per-session + fallback to tokens.
- Give developers tools to tune anchor size by intended UX (low-latency stream vs high-quality long-form).

Rough unit-econ back-of-envelope (example numbers):
Assumptions:
- GPU cost to us (amortized): $2.00 / GPU-hour (reserved infra mix).
- Average throughput for interactive model variant: 25 tokens/sec per GPU (steady-state).
- Effective tokens/hour per GPU: 25 * 3600 = 90,000 tokens/hr.
- Implied cost per token = $2.00 / 90,000 = $0.0000222/token (~0.00222 cents/token).
- Add infra overhead + sw/service margin, round to internal baseline unit cost = $0.00004/token.

Session anchoring math:
- Typical early-session behavior: first 60s of back-and-forth dominates warm cost and has high per-token cost due to cold KV and startup. If average burst during that minute is 7 tokens/s -> 420 tokens.
- At internal cost $0.00004/token, that 420 tokens = $0.0168 of infra cost. Round up to $0.02 to cover extra overhead and logging.
- UX-friendly anchor price to customer: anchor retail price = $0.10 (psychologically round). That leaves room for discounts, margins, and platform fees.
- Map: 1 anchor credit => covers first 400 tokens (approx) + KV warm. After 400 tokens, continue at PAYG consumer price.

Customer-facing pricing mapping (example):
- 1 anchor credit = $0.10 (covers first 400 tokens)
- PAYG token add-on = $0.00025/token (retail) after anchor consumed
- Burst surcharge (if > 4x baseline sustained > 30s) = +30% on token rate for that span (discourages unbounded burst abuse)

Why these numbers?
- Retail token price is deliberately 6x internal baseline to get sensible gross margin and leave room for commissions/discounting.
- Anchor is priced where a reasonable fraction of short sessions (<=400 tokens) feels "free" relative to user expectation but still accumulates revenue.

Behavioral mechanics and TTLs:
- Anchor attaches to a user identifier for 14 days idle TTL. If the user returns within 14 days, next session consumes anchor leftover first.
- Session boundaries: either explicit client-side session start event or 30 minutes inactivity cutoff.
- Anchor top-ups: customer can buy bundles of anchors (10 anchors for $0.85) -> encourages commit.

Edge cases and fraud protections:
- Shared API keys: anchors are per-key and per-user metadata where available. If API key used at high concurrency from many IPs, throttle anchor redemption and route to PAYG.
- Cache-warm abuse: detect too-frequent tiny sessions (5s chats) from same key; degrade anchor eligibility after threshold.

Conversion and reporting needs:
- Billing UI should show: anchors bought, anchors consumed (with token equivalence), tokens billed PAYG, surge charges.
- Expose to customers per-route counters so they can map anchors to features (chat vs embeddings).

Alternate variants (notes):
- Time-anchored approach: anchor covers first N seconds of compute (instead of tokens). Maps more directly to GPU-seconds cost but is harder to communicate to customers.
- Concurrency-anchored: anchors give a small sustained concurrency reservation for 1 minute to preserve latency SLOs (sell as "fast-response credit").
- Sliding anchor: anchors drip credits over 24h; good for low-frequency users.

Quick sensitivity checks (how fragile is unit econ):
- If internal GPU cost rises 2x -> internal cost per token ~ $0.00008 -> anchor internal cost ~ $0.04 -> need to raise anchor price or shrink covered tokens.
- If average session length increases to 2k tokens, anchors will be consumed less often relative to session length; retail anchor subsidizes many sessions but contributes small fraction of revenue -> measure ratio anchors_consume_tokens / tokens_billed.

Open questions / TODOs:
- Should anchors expire more quickly for trial-tier customers? probably yes (reduce abuse).
- Exact mapping of tokens->anchor: flat token bucket vs proportional depletion (I prefer flat bucket for UX).
- Integrate with quota system: anchors reduce burst protection triggers temporarily.
- Simulate cohort economics for 6 months: need sample of session length distribution from prod (pull from analytics).
- Decide whether anchor purchase is user-level or org-level (org-level better for seat-sharing customers).

Small implementation notes for infra team: 
- On request path, check anchor state first; if anchor has >= remaining_tokens_threshold, mark request as "anchor-covered" and route to cheaper warming node pool.
- Instrument events: ANCHOR_CONSUMED, ANCHOR_TOPUP, ANCHOR_EXPIRED with token counts.
- Add guard rails to ensure anchor can't be used to cover long-running streaming generation indefinitely (cap per-output > 2k tokens shifts to PAYG).

Closing thought:
Anchors look promising as a low-friction mental model for customers building chat features. Key is to keep customer messaging simple ("each session starts with one free-ish credit") while maintaining discoverable metrics so teams can reconcile usage. Need to run sensitivity sims before committing to pricing buckets.

title: cbatch oscillation debug log
preview:
Objective:
Short session notes capturing commands tried, raw snippets, and working hypotheses for the intermittent throughput oscillations observed on node pool-rt-3 when continuous_batching is enabled. Goal is to preserve breadcrumbs for follow-up and handoff to SRE/infra.

Summary of symptom:
- Starting 2025-10-29 we saw periodic ~40-70% drop in tokens/s for baseline text-generation workloads (seq_len=512, concurrency=40). Drops last 2–6 minutes then recover. Not correlated with GPU utilization spikes (GPU stays ~60–75%).
- Regression appears only when continuous_batching=true and kv_prefetch=true on the same runtime revision (serving-runtime:v2.14.1). No regression when we disable continuous_batching or pin to previous runtime v2.13.8.

Quick reproducer (local-ish):
1) Launch small cluster: kubectl apply -f test-cluster.yaml (k8s job using model-opt-7b)
2) Load generator: locust or simple python loop (attached below) hitting /v1/generate with concurrency=40 and prompt size ~200 tokens.

Commands & logs I collected during incident:
- List pods and annotate: kubectl get pods -n serving -l app=runtime -o wide
- Describe pod at time of drop: kubectl describe pod runtime-rt-3-7d6f6 (no OOM, no node drain events)
- Tail runtime logs: kubectl logs runtime-rt-3-7d6f6 -c runtime --since=15m | egrep "batch|kv|prefetch|latency|throughput" -i > /tmp/rt3-tail.log
- Example log snippet (timestamp truncated):
  [2025-10-29 14:12:03] INFO batcher: batch_window_ms=6 current_batch_size=1 dispatch_count=1
  [2025-10-29 14:12:03] INFO kv_cache: prefetch miss for key shard-12
  [2025-10-29 14:12:03] WARN runtime: small batch ramp fallback triggered; estimated_tokens_per_sec=1200
  [2025-10-29 14:12:04] INFO batcher: batch_window_ms=40 current_batch_size=32 dispatch_count=1
  [2025-10-29 14:12:20] INFO metrics: tokens_sec=450 (baseline ~1500)
  [2025-10-29 14:14:05] INFO batcher: batch_window_ms=6 current_batch_size=2 dispatch_count=1
  [2025-10-29 14:14:05] INFO metrics: tokens_sec=1520

- Node-level telemetry around drop: no host CPU pressure, network tx/rx steady. GPU memory usage steady. NVLink counters normal.

Commands I ran to toggle config in the runtime configmap:
- kubectl edit configmap runtime-config -n serving
  toggled: continuous_batching: true -> false
  observed: throughput immediately stabilizes to baseline within ~30s after hot-path pick up

A/B toggles and results (raw):
- continuous_batching=true, kv_prefetch=true, runtime=v2.14.1  => oscillation seen in 9/12 runs (avg tokens/s drop to 38% of baseline, duration 2-6m)
- continuous_batching=false, kv_prefetch=true, runtime=v2.14.1 => no oscillation in 12/12 runs (stable tokens/s ~ baseline)
- continuous_batching=true, kv_prefetch=false, runtime=v2.14.1 => oscillation seen 2/12 runs (much reduced)
- continuous_batching=true, kv_prefetch=true, runtime=v2.13.8 => no oscillation in 10/10 runs

Hypotheses (ranked):
1) Race between continuous batcher window shrinkage and KV cache prefetch: when batcher collapses to tiny windows (6ms) it dispatches small batches causing upstream kernel selection to pick low-utilization kernels and prefetch logic to thrash, producing lower per-token throughput until profiler decides to switch back. Recovery occurs after cache warms and batcher regains larger window.
2) Heisenbug in kernel selection path triggered by payload distribution spike: runtime v2.14.1 introduced a fallback threshold for small-batch kernels that, combined with our model's kv-prefetch latencies, moves requests onto a slower code path until a hysteresis timer expires.
3) GC/JS memory fragmentation in the worker process that correlates with small batches causing scheduler to yield more often (less likely given stable mem numbers).
4) Networking/API backpressure: not supported by host telemetry but still possible due to per-connection backoffs interacting with continuous_batching burst shaping.

What I tried and the rationale:
- Increase batch_window_ms min from 6ms to 20ms in config (prod-simulation) -> oscillations disappear but tail latency increases slightly. Rationale: increase minimum batch accumulation so we avoid tiny batches that switch kernels.
- Disabled kv_prefetch -> oscillation largely disappears. Rationale: eliminate possible thrash from cache misses/prefetch threads causing contention in process.
- Added more aggressive batching metrics: track batch size histogram, per-batch kernel_id, kernel_switch_count in logs. This helped see that kernel_switch_count spikes right before throughput drops.
- Reproduced under controlled RNG for request arrival times (poisson vs bursty) -> oscillation more likely under bursty arrival (client-side burstiness).
- Reverted to runtime v2.13.8 (canary) with same config -> no oscillation. Suggests a behavioral change introduced in v2.14.1.

Important log traces saved:
- /tmp/rt3-tail.log (filtered)
- /var/log/serving/batcher-kernels-2025-10-29.log (complete kernel switch timeline)
- perf trace tsc counters captured (attached to ticket INF-3246)

Open questions / next steps:
- Is the kernel selection hysteresis parameter newly added in v2.14.1? Who owns that change? (Assign to Rafiq to triage PR 981).
- Can we add a short-circuit: if batch_size drops below X and tokens/sec falls Y% vs baseline, skip the slow kernel path for N seconds? (quick mitigation).
- Add an experiment: force KV prefetch threadpool size=1 vs 4 and observe. If prefetch concurrency is thrashing L2/L3 caches we should see improvement.
- Add flamegraphs for runtime process during a drop window to check for scheduler stalls. Priya to run perf + flame map during next repro window.

Immediate mitigations suggested for prod until root cause identified:
- Set continuous_batching.min_window_ms=20 for affected node pools (fast rollback and low-risk change).
- Or disable continuous_batching on 2/3 node pools as staged rollback.

Notes to self (breadcrumbs):
- Save command for live toggles: kubectl patch configmap runtime-config -n serving --patch '{"data":{"continuous_batching.min_window_ms":"20"}}'
- Quick test runner I used: python3 tools/simple-loadgen.py --qps 300 --concurrency 40 --seq-len 512 --duration 15m --target http://runtime-rt-3.internal/v1/generate
- Snippet to collect kernel switch counts: grep kernel_switch /var/log/serving/batcher-kernels-*.log | awk -F"|" '{print $1,$4,$6}' > /tmp/kernel-switch-counts.txt

Unresolved: which exact commit in v2.14.1 introduced the hysteresis or kernel fallback threshold change. PR 981 touched kernel selector utilities but needs confirmation.

Appendix - quick raw perf numbers from experiment run on 2025-10-30 (single node):
- baseline (cbatch disabled): tokens/s median=1540 p95=1720
- v2.14.1 cbatch=true kv_prefetch=true: tokens/s median=1380 p95=1670, but during drop median=520 (40% of baseline)
- v2.14.1 cbatch=true kv_prefetch=false: tokens/s median=1500 p95=1700

TODOs assigned:
- Rafiq: confirm PR 981 behavioral changes and link commit -> (by 2025-11-07)
- Priya: collect flamegraphs during next repro window and attach (by 2025-11-06)
- Maya: run kv_prefetch threadpool sizing experiment and report perf (by 2025-11-08)
- Ethan (me): prepare a short mitigation PR to raise min_window_ms default and include test harness reproducer (by 2025-11-10)

If handing off: attach /tmp/rt3-tail.log, kernel-switch-counts.txt, perf trace, and link INF-3246. Leave this file as living scratchpad for the incident.

End of notes.

title: Cadence-based prompt sandbox
preview:
Purpose:
This is a personal sandbox to explore the idea of 'cadence' in prompting — i.e., the rhythm and timing of instructions, few-shot injections, and turn-level scaffolds across a multi-turn or streaming interaction. Goal is practical: identify brittle cases where changing the tempo (pace, density of examples, distance between examples and query) materially changes outputs and to capture simple mitigation patterns.

Quick hypothesis notes (working):
- H1: Denser few-shot injections immediately before a query increase stylistic conformity but can reduce creativity and cause token inflation.
- H2: Interleaving short clarifying instructions between examples (mini-prompts) improves factual recall on long-context tasks by anchoring the model's attention.
- H3: A fixed cadence (e.g., example -> 2-line instruction -> example -> question) yields more consistent output distribution than ad-hoc placement.

Experiment scaffolding (how I run):
- Model: hosted-redwood/gptx-small-8k (toy) and local quantized variants for throughput checks.
- Metric buckets: style-consistency (BLEU-ish), factual-precision (QA accuracy on gold set), token-cost (tokens per response), latency impact (sec/turn), instability-index (JS divergence across 10 seeds).
- Control variables: same few-shot exemplars, same system instruction, same temperature schedule. Only change is placement/density of examples and spacer phrasing.

Cadence patterns to test (naming shorthand):
1) Burst: 3 examples in a row immediately before the query (no interleaving).
2) Drip: single example, query, single example, query (alternating).
3) Anchor: example + anchor-line ("Recall: keep to fact") repeated, then query.
4) Negative-space: examples separated by long neutral filler paragraphs before query.
5) Rolling-window: sliding window of last 2 turns used as few-shot.

Sample prompt templates (literal text to copy into runs):
- Burst template:
SYSTEM: You are a helpful assistant.
EX1: [Example 1 text]
EX2: [Example 2 text]
EX3: [Example 3 text]
USER: [User query]

- Anchor template:
SYSTEM: You are concise and factual.
EX1: [Example 1]
ANCHOR: "Keep responses concise and cite when asked."
EX2: [Example 2]
ANCHOR: "Keep responses concise and cite when asked."
USER: [User query]

Notes on failure modes observed so far (short journal):
- Case: Burst -> hallucination drift when examples are stylistically different from gold answers.
  - Observation: The model tends to overfit to the nearest example's style, especially with high temperature.
  - Mitigation tried: insert short instruction anchor after examples; reduced hallucination by ~18% on small test set.

- Case: Drip -> increased incoherence in longer multi-turn tasks (3+ turns).
  - Observation: Drip keeps earlier examples in context but the model loses global cohesion; pronoun references break.
  - Mitigation: add explicit turn markers and short recap prompts every 3 turns.

- Case: Negative-space -> lower token cost but sometimes misses edge-case instructions embedded in an example.
  - Observation: The model may treat distant examples as lower priority; works for high-similarity queries, fails for nuanced ones.
  - Mitigation: use a brief example summary line close to the query (1 sentence).

Micro-experiments to run (to-do list):
- [ ] A/B test Anchor vs Burst on 200-item QA subset (factual precision + token cost).
- [ ] Run instability-index across 10 seeds for Rolling-window pattern.
- [ ] Test cadence effects with streaming (token-by-token) outputs: does cadence change early token distribution?
- [ ] Try cadence + dynamic temperature scheduling (lower temp on anchor lines).

Operational notes / how I log runs:
- Use local harness: /infra/quick-runs/cadence-harness (link to be added to RED issue).
- Naming convention: cadence.{pattern}.{date}.{seed} e.g., cadence.anchor.2025-09-21.s03
- Keep a csv per run: pattern, model, temp, context-length, tokens_out, accuracy, notes.

Prompt snippets & micro-recipes (copyable):
- Quick recap marker: "[RECAP: summarize the conversation in one sentence]" placed every 3 turns. Works well with Drip.
- Minimal anchor: "Response should be <=120 tokens; prefer concise factual sentences"
- Sparse few-shot: include 2-line exemplar with explicit labels (Q: / A:) and a trailing note "This example emphasizes concise structure."

Edge-case thought experiments (brainstorm):
- Time-decayed weighting: if examples are farther back (in tokens or turns), append "(older)" tag to them in the prompt so the model can deprioritize.
- Cadence-aware reranking: generate N candidates and rerank by a scorer trained to prefer cadence fidelity (i.e., style + factual match).

Questions to resolve (notes to self):
- What is the smallest anchor phrasing that yields majority of benefit? (1-3 words vs sentence)
- Does cadence interact with model size in predictable ways (bigger models more robust to negative-space?)
- How much unit-cost tradeoff is acceptable for high-consistency apps? Need guardrails for SLAs.

Slack / tickets / refs to follow up:
- Slack: #prompt-lab (pinned message re: cadence experiments).
- Ticket: RED-4567 (tracking anchor template rollout).
- PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/prompt-profiles/pull/834 (adds 'anchor' template to library)

Quick wins (for immediate product experiments):
- Add a one-sentence anchor to existing Burst templates in the hosted API as an opt-in field and measure error rates for top 10 customers using few-shot.
- Expose a "cadence hint" boolean in SDK that appends a short anchor when true (low friction).

Loose end housekeeping (cleanup before sharing):
- [ ] Convert successful patterns into standardized template objects (name, pattern, anchor_text, recommended_token_budget).
- [ ] Add small example corpus for public reproducibility (20 items).
- [ ] Draft a short internal note summarizing wins for product PR.

Random notes to self (style + voice experiments):
- Try a 2-line anchor that is procedural: "1) Answer directly. 2) Give one example if relevant."
- Test tempo where anchor lines are phrased as questions ("Do this: be concise") vs commands ("Be concise.") — preliminary sense: commands stronger but slightly more rigid.

End of sandbox dump.
If I pick one next experiment: run A/B anchor vs no-anchor on 200 QA items and log token delta + accuracy.

-- Priya
(quick personal note: leave TODOs in this doc, it's messy by design)

  - Observation: The model tends to overfit to the nearest example's style, especially with high temperature.
  - Mitigation tried: insert short instruction anchor after examples; reduced hallucination by ~18% on small test set.

- Case: Drip -> increased incoherence in longer multi-turn tasks (3+ turns).
  - Observation: Drip keeps earlier examples in context but the model loses global cohesion; pronoun references break.
  - Mitigation: add explicit turn markers and short recap prompts every 3 turns.

- Case: Negative-space -> lower token cost but sometimes misses edge-case instructions embedded in an example.
  - Observation: The model may treat distant examples as lower priority; works for high-similarity queries, fails for nuanced ones.
  - Mitigation: use a brief example summary line close to the query (1 sentence).

Micro-experiments to run (to-do list):
- [ ] A/B test Anchor vs Burst on 200-item QA subset (factual precision + token cost).
- [ ] Run instability-index across 10 seeds for Rolling-window pattern.
- [ ] Test cadence effects with streaming (token-by-token) outputs: does cadence change early token distribution?
- [ ] Try cadence + dynamic temperature scheduling (lower temp on anchor lines).

Operational notes / how I log runs:
- Use local harness: /infra/quick-runs/cadence-harness (link to be added to RED issue).
- Naming convention: cadence.{pattern}.{date}.{seed} e.g., cadence.anchor.2025-09-21.s03
- Keep a csv per run: pattern, model, temp, context-length, tokens_out, accuracy, notes.

Prompt snippets & micro-recipes (copyable):
- Quick recap marker: "[RECAP: summarize the conversation in one sentence]" placed every 3 turns. Works well with Drip.
- Minimal anchor: "Response should be <=120 tokens; prefer concise factual sentences"
- Sparse few-shot: include 2-line exemplar with explicit labels (Q: / A:) and a trailing note "This example emphasizes concise structure."

Edge-case thought experiments (brainstorm):
- Time-decayed weighting: if examples are farther back (in tokens or turns), append "(older)" tag to them in the prompt so the model can deprioritize.
- Cadence-aware reranking: generate N candidates and rerank by a scorer trained to prefer cadence fidelity (i.e., style + factual match).

Questions to resolve (notes to self):
- What is the smallest anchor phrasing that yields majority of benefit? (1-3 words vs sentence)
- Does cadence interact with model size in predictable ways (bigger models more robust to negative-space?)
- How much unit-cost tradeoff is acceptable for high-consistency apps? Need guardrails for SLAs.

Slack / tickets / refs to follow up:
- Slack: #prompt-lab (pinned message re: cadence experiments).
- Ticket: RED-4567 (tracking anchor template rollout).
- PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/prompt-profiles/pull/834 (adds 'anchor' template to library)

Quick wins (for immediate product experiments):
- Add a one-sentence anchor to existing Burst templates in the hosted API as an opt-in field and measure error rates for top 10 customers using few-shot.
- Expose a "cadence hint" boolean in SDK that appends a short anchor when true (low friction).

Loose end housekeeping (cleanup before sharing):
- [ ] Convert successful patterns into standardized template objects (name, pattern, anchor_text, recommended_token_budget).
- [ ] Add small example corpus for public reproducibility (20 items).
- [ ] Draft a short internal note summarizing wins for product PR.

Random notes to self (style + voice experiments):
- Try a 2-line anchor that is procedural: "1) Answer directly. 2) Give one example if relevant."
- Test tempo where anchor lines are phrased as questions ("Do this: be concise") vs commands ("Be concise.") — preliminary sense: commands stronger but slightly more rigid.

End of sandbox dump.
If I pick one next experiment: run A/B anchor vs no-anchor on 200 QA items and log token delta + accuracy.

-- Priya
(quick personal note: leave TODOs in this doc, it's messy by design)

title: microrefactor-mapping-and-pr-followups
preview:
Quick context:
- Working notes for a set of small, targeted refactors that came out of PR #4876 review (routing + KV-cache edge-case fixes). Not a grand design doc, more of a map + prioritized TODOs for next few sprints.
- Goal: reduce cognitive overhead in request routing, make fallback logic explicit, and remove two hotspots in request path that are repeatedly causing timeouts for long-seq workloads.
- Constraints: minimal API surface change, keep canary rolloutable, preserve current telemetry hooks.

High-level summary (one-liner):
- Break routing logic into three deterministic layers: policy resolution, model-compatibility, and fallback orchestration. Move heuristics out of hot path and into a small cache-backed decision service.

Why this matters (observed pain):
- Multiple incidents where fallback routing reselected a larger model causing latency spikes (seen in prod incidents 2025-08).
- Codepaths for "isCompatible" check are duplicated across gateway and dedicated router module. Hard to reason about and easy to miss when adding quantized variants.
- KV-cache eviction is tied to request lifecycle in a way that creates transient cache churn under bursty loads.

Quick design map (sketch):
- Layer A: PolicyResolver (cold, infrequent). Responsible for evaluating user-configured policies (latency budget, cost tier, tenant constraints) and producing a ranked list of candidate model variants. Cached per-tenant+route with TTL (30s).
- Layer B: CompatibilityService (hot, simple). Lightweight stateless checks: model architecture, tokenizer compatibility, max-len, supported-quantization. Implemented as in-process inline checks for performance with an eventual plan to move to a narrow gRPC service if complexity grows.
- Layer C: FallbackOrchestrator (hot). Executes fallback strategy deterministically: try primary, then cached prefix-compat fallback, then region fallback, then degrade to lower-cost model. Each step emits a structured trace event.

Immediate actions (PR-sized chunks):
1) Extract isCompatible helpers into a single file and add unit tests (small, low risk).
   - TODO: ensure tests cover quantized vs fp16 vs bf16 mismatch cases.
   - Owner: Noor. Relates to PR 4876.
2) Introduce PolicyResolver stub with in-memory per-tenant cache (TTL 30s). Wire it into the router but return current behavior by default (feature-flag off).
   - Measure: extra CPU overhead under 99p.
   - Owner: Ethan.
3) Move fallback orchestration into a single function with clear step boundaries and structured tracing (no policy decision changes initially).
   - This consolidates duplication and makes next steps safer.
   - Owner: Maya.
4) Add an experiment flag to route through CompatibilityService for a subset of tenants (A/B), collect metrics for failed compat checks and latency delta.
   - Data collection: count compatibility mismatches, time spent in check, downstream latency.

Review Checklist (what I looked for in PRs):
- API compatibility: no changes to public REST endpoints, only internal routing logic.
- Telemetry: each fallback step logs trace/span + fallback_reason tag.
- Tests: unit tests for each isCompatible branch, and an integration test simulating quantized fallback.
- Performance: microbenchmark for hot path (<5% p99 regression target).
- Canary plan: config flag + rollout to 5% of traffic, monitor token latency and error rate.

Hotspots / suspicious code in current tree (notes to self):
- serving/runtime/router.go: checkVariantCompatibility duplicated 3x (lines ~230-420). Replace with single helper.
- serving/runtime/kv_cache.go: eviction callback holds global lock while calling request-specific cleanup. Potential for lock convoys under bursts. Consider using channel-based async cleanup to avoid holding lock.
- console/rollouts/config.go: feature flags are checked in two different codepaths; unify into central feature flag accessor to avoid mismatched defaulting.

Refactor sequencing (safe order):
- Phase 1 (low risk): extract helpers + tests, add trace events (should be pure refactor).
- Phase 2 (experiment): introduce PolicyResolver stub, feature flag it. Observe.
- Phase 3 (medium risk): change fallback orchestration to use PolicyResolver outputs. Canary.
- Phase 4 (optional): move Compatibility checks to a narrow in-process service if measured cost grows; otherwise keep inline.

Test plan / validation scenarios to cover in CI:
- Unit: quantized-vs-fp mismatch -> compatibility false.
- Integration: route request with long sequence and ensure fallback to quantized variant preserves correctness (token-level equivalence tests / acceptance).
- Load test: simulate bursty traffic and confirm no new lock contention in KV cache path.
- Chaos: simulate region fallback target unreachable and verify orchestrator falls back to next step and logs structured error.

Metrics to monitor during canary: (metric_name : alert_condition)
- route.request.latency.p99 : > baseline + 10% -> rollback
- route.fallback.count : unexpected increase > x5 baseline -> investigate
- compatibility.check.fail_rate : > 1% for canary cohort -> investigate config mismatch
- kv_cache.eviction_rate : > baseline + 20% -> potential regression

Follow-ups / non-blocking tech-debt ideas (parked):
- Consolidate feature flag checks into a lightweight middleware.
- Move eviction cleanup to a background worker with batching.
- Add a small decision simulator harness to test policy permutations offline for edge-case reasoning.

Open questions / things to decide before Phase 3:
- TTL for PolicyResolver caching: 30s seemed reasonable for rapid reconfig, but could be 60s for fewer lookups. Tradeoffs?
- What is safe size for in-process CompatibilityService lookup table? Want O(1) checks with a few hundred model variants. Memory budget?
- Should we introduce an explicit compatibility schema version to allow rolling changes without coupling code versions?

Work items (short checklist for next 2 weeks):
- [ ] open small PR to extract helpers + add unit tests (target: 2 days)
- [ ] add structured trace events to fallback steps + update console instrumentation (target: 3 days)
- [ ] stub PolicyResolver + feature flag wiring (target: 5 days)
- [ ] write integration test covering quantized fallback (target: 3 days)
- [ ] schedule 5% canary and define runbook (target: follow-on with SRE)

Notes to self (random):
- Keep change sets small and reviewable. If possible split telemetry additions from behavior changes.
- Talk to SRE about safe canary windows and emergency rollback steps (Slack: #infra-sre).
- Add a short confluence blurb summarizing the deterministic fallback states for PM/SE alignment.

Slack snippets to send when canary starts:
- "Starting microrefactor fallback canary (PR 4876). 5% traffic to PolicyResolver-backed routing. Watch route.request.latency & fallback.count. Ping me if you see >10% p99 reg."

End of notes.

(Will iterate this doc as PRs land and we get telemetry.)

title: Tokenization Lattice + Mapper — scratchpad
preview:
Purpose:
This is a personal scratchpad exploring an API shape and runtime behaviour for a tokenization lattice + mapper service. Goal: produce a lightweight contract that lets the client and multiple tokenizer implementations interoperate for streaming generation, partial offsets, alignment with original bytes, and robust fallback when tokenizer variants disagree.

Background / motivation:
- We need consistent token -> byte/char offsets for: eval alignment, user-visible cursor highlighting, and incremental cache resumption.
- Different tokenizers (BPE, unigram, byte-level) used across model family variants and quantized runtime can disagree on token boundaries.
- In streaming scenarios, we often emit tokens before client has the whole completion; clients need to map tokens back to original input text reliably for highlighting and incremental diffing.

Design goals / constraints:
- Low-latency: mapping must not add extra roundtrips for each token in steady state.
- Incremental: support partial sequences and final reconciliation when true tokenization is confirmed.
- Deterministic fallback: if two tokenizers disagree, use a deterministic and explainable tie-breaker (e.g., canonical tokenizer order + token-level provenance).
- Small wire format: messages should be compact and friendly to streaming transport (HTTP/2 chunking, SSE, or WebSocket fragments).
- Backwards-compatible: clients should be tolerant of missing fields (e.g., older clients don't need provenance).

API sketch (streaming-friendly):
- Service: /v1/tokenizer-mapper/stream  (long-lived duplex)
- Initial handshake (client->server):
  {
    \"type\": \"handshake\",
    \"request_id\": \"uuid\",
    \"model\": \"rwkv-8b-v2\",
    \"preferred_tokenizers\": [\"redwood-bpe-v1\", \"byte-level-v2\"],
    \"text_fragment\": \"partial user prompt or new text chunk\",
    \"origin_offset\": 1024  // byte offset in user document if streaming a slice
  }

- Server responses are short lines (SSE-style) containing JSON objects:
  - token: emitted when model generates one token (or model-side tokenization)
  - reconcile: emitted when server can provide final byte offsets for prior tokens (might be delayed)
  - error / warning: signal misalignment or fallback decisions

Examples (single-line entries for stream):
- token (optimistic):
  {\"type\":\"token\",\"request_id\":\"uuid\",\"seq\":12,\"token_text\":\"ing\",\"token_id\":5021,\"provenance\":\"redwood-bpe-v1\",\"partial\":true}

- reconcile (later):
  {\"type\":\"reconcile\",\"request_id\":\"uuid\",\"seq\":12,\"token_id\":5021,\"byte_start\":1532,\"byte_end\":1535,\"char_start\":412,\"char_end\":415,\"canonical\":true}

Notes on fields:
- seq: monotonic token sequence per request (helps client reorder/fragments)
- token_id: tokenizer-specific id (clients may ignore)
- partial: true if emitted before final mapping available (client display may show ephemeral tag)
- provenance: the tokenizer that produced the token text (optional)
- reconcile.canonical: whether the mapping is final per canonical tokenizer (server chooses canonical order at handshake)

Lattice model (informal):
- Server maintains a tiny lattice structure for the prefix window W (default 2048 chars) with multiple tokenizer paths. Each emitted token has one or more candidate alignments: [ {tokenizer: X, byte_start, byte_end, score}, ... ]
- Emission heuristic: pick highest-prob candidate that doesn't conflict with already-consumed bytes. If ambiguous, prefer the tokenizer earlier in preferred_tokenizers. Mark as partial and emit.
- Reconciliation: within N ms or when next whitespace/commit boundary detected, compute final canonical path across lattice using dynamic programming that maximizes token-level agreement with model logits and preferred_tokenizers costs. Emit reconcile events for affected seq positions.

ASCII pipeline diagram:

Client ---> [handshake] ---> Tokenizer-Mapper (handshake stores preferred order)
   |                            |
   |---- text fragments ---->   |--- split to candidate tokenizations --> [lattice builder]
   |                            |--- model generation stream -> tokens (ids)
   |                            |--- align ids to lattice -> emit partial tokens
   |                            |--- DP reconcile stage -> emit reconcile messages

Edge cases / examples:
1) Emoji or combined grapheme clusters. Tokenizer A treats emoji sequence as two tokens; Tokenizer B as one. We must provide stable char offsets. Use char-index mapping (UTF-8 aware) in reconcile events.
2) Client sends offsets based on original doc slice. Handshake includes origin_offset so server returns absolute byte offsets.
3) Model-generated substitutions: Model outputs token text that doesn't map cleanly to source fragment (e.g., hallucinated text). Reconcile should set byte_start == -1 when no mapping exists and include a reason: \"generated\".

Compact wire optimization (binary-friendly alternative):
- Short-forms for common messages: first byte = type code, then varint fields: request_id hash (64-bit), seq (varint), token_id (varint), flags (bits for partial/canonical), offsets (varint delta encoded).
- Keep JSON as default for debuggability; allow protocol negotiation during handshake.

Developer ergonomics / client lib responsibilities:
- Provide helpers to: 1) merge token stream + reconcile events into a stable token list, 2) map token seq to original char range (for UI highlights), 3) gracefully show ephemeral tokens (partial) and update them as reconcile arrives.

Open questions / TODOs:
- Backpressure: when client is slow to ACK, do we hold lattice memory per request? Limit memory per-request and garbage collect reconciled atoms after window W.
- Multi-model fallbacks: if model switches (e.g., small to large) during streaming, we need cross-model reconciliation policy. Perhaps treat model as another provenance and prefer canonical tokenizer for final mapping.
- Security: avoid leaking absolute byte offsets for PII-sensitive streams unless client asked for absolute mapping. Add handshake flag: \"return_absolute_offsets\": true|false.
- Failure modes: how to surface when reconcile can't find a consistent mapping? Proposed field in reconcile: \"status\": \"ok\"|\"partial\"|\"unmapped\" with human-readable \"note\".

Sketch of client-side reconstructor (pseudocode):
- state.tokens = []
- on token event: append {seq, token_text, token_id, provenance, partial}
- on reconcile event: find state.tokens entry with seq; update offsets, set canonical flag; if unmapped => mark generated
- UI: if partial && not reconciled -> show translucent; on reconcile replace text spans using byte->char mapping

Quick experiments to run:
- fuzz: feed emoji-heavy strings with 3 tokenizers and see reconcile flapping counts for window sizes 512/2048/8192 chars.
- latency tradeoff: measure extra reconcile delay when window grows; target median extra latency < 6ms per 512-char increase.
- incompatibility test: model produces token id that tokenizer can't map -> server should emit reconcile with status=unmapped immediately.

References / related notes:
- need to crosslink later to runtime \"prefix-kv-caching\" doc since byte offsets affect cache keys.
- link to tokenization proto (proto file in github repo) for canonical id mappings.

Personal next steps (checklist):
- [ ] implement minimal server that returns simple token + reconcile sequence for a sample prompt.
- [ ] write client helper to merge streams and unit-test highlight correctness.
- [ ] add opt-in handshake flag for absolute offsets and wire-format negotiation.
- [ ] propose field names to API review and lock proto v0.1

Notes to self: keep it small and recoverable. This should remain tolerant (backwards compatible) — older clients may simply render token_texts and ignore offsets.

End of scratchpad.

- fuzz: feed emoji-heavy strings with 3 tokenizers and see reconcile flapping counts for window sizes 512/2048/8192 chars.
- latency tradeoff: measure extra reconcile delay when window grows; target median extra latency < 6ms per 512-char increase.
- incompatibility test: model produces token id that tokenizer can't map -> server should emit reconcile with status=unmapped immediately.

References / related notes:
- need to crosslink later to runtime \"prefix-kv-caching\" doc since byte offsets affect cache keys.
- link to tokenization proto (proto file in github repo) for canonical id mappings.

Personal next steps (checklist):
- [ ] implement minimal server that returns simple token + reconcile sequence for a sample prompt.
- [ ] write client helper to merge streams and unit-test highlight correctness.
- [ ] add opt-in handshake flag for absolute offsets and wire-format negotiation.
- [ ] propose field names to API review and lock proto v0.1

Notes to self: keep it small and recoverable. This should remain tolerant (backwards compatible) — older clients may simply render token_texts and ignore offsets.

End of scratchpad.

title: nightbloom-ware-llm-tokenization-gotchas
preview:
Quick capture from 2025-09-03 call + field testing notes. Rough, mostly for internal follow-up.

Context:
- Customer: Nightbloom Ware (on-prem VPC pilot). Heavy SKU catalog (300k SKUs). Using our hosted-private build but routing through their VPC for data isolation.
- Primary ask: conversational search + summary for product pages with strict latency SLO (p95 < 220ms) and deterministic cost.
- Customer contact: Jared M. (integration lead), ops: Lina K.

Call / field notes:
- Observed: high token counts in real user queries; many SKUs have long hyphenated identifiers and embedded micro-unicode (non-breaking hyphens, zero-width joiners). Tokenizer splits these unpredictably -> token blowup (example: SKU "NB-λ-004‑XZ" became 12 tokens).
- Customer logs show repeated re-tokenization across reruns; cache hit-rate ~8% (expected 40%). Seems normalization mismatch between preproc on their edge and runtime cache key.
- Streaming latency spikes when batch sizes grow even slightly; tracing points to repeat KV-cache misses and larger-than-expected seq lengths.
- Tokenizer differences: their edge preprocessor uses a legacy sentencepiece config; model variant on server expects BPE-like token boundaries. Mismatch surface: apostrophes, slashes, emojis in product descriptions.
- One customer test produced a model truncation mid-entity -> summary lost critical warranty clause. Customer flagged as high-severity.

Immediate technical hypotheses:
1) Normalization gap: different unicode normalization forms and hyphen types causing effective cache key variance.
2) Tokenizer config mismatch: preproc pipeline not aligned with model's tokenizer; leads to 10-200% token inflation on SKU-heavy docs.
3) Caching key scope: current prefix cache uses raw prompt string; should use normalized token-id prefix or hashed normalized string.
4) Batch window/size tuning: current default batch window (50ms) is causing larger batches during midday causing KV cache thrash.

Quick mitigation ideas (fast wins):
- Ask customer to run a normalization pass on edge: NFC normalize, map U+2010/U+2011/U+2012 variants to ASCII hyphen, remove U+FEFF and zero-width chars, collapse repeated whitespace. Provide a tiny JS/Python snippet.
- Instruct Redwood-side gateway to apply same normalization before cache keying (short-term patch).
- Add token-length guard rail in routing: if predicted tokens > 1024, route to canary model that returns summarized context before main call.
- For warranty-rich responses, pin to model-variant with higher context window (and enable prefix caching).

Longer-term fixes / engineering follow-ups:
- Align preproc & tokenizer: schedule a small ticket to add a tokenizer normalization shim in our private deployment agent so customer edge and server use same tokenization (PR linked above).
- Cache key redesign: use normalized, truncated token-id prefix hashed with model-id+quantization-profile so minor whitespace changes don't bust cache.
- Quantization profile recommendation: q4f-prob for this workload seems sweet spot; test shows ~10% perf win without big quality drop on bench prompts. Document in Optimize suggestions.
- Autoscale rule: use token-weighted autoscaling (tokens/s) rather than requests/s for SKU-heavy workloads (helps predict GPU memory pressure).

Evaluation notes from small test run (09/05):
- Normalization patch applied at gateway -> cache hit-rate rose to ~42%; median latency dropped from 280ms to 195ms on sample evening load.
- Token inflation reduced: median tokens/request from 410 -> 180 for our sample of 2k queries.
- One regression: a small class of multi-language product descriptions lost diacritics after aggressive normalization; need whitelist rules to preserve unicode where semantically necessary.

Open questions / things to validate with Nightbloom:
- Can they deploy a tiny normalization function at edge (we provided snippet)? SLA for deployment?
- Which SKU formats must be preserved exactly (legal/warranty strings)? Need a whitelist.
- Are they comfortable with a canary model for overlength queries? Failure mode visible to user?

Action items (owners):
- Ava: push normalization JS + short README to their integration channel; include test harness (unit tests with example SKUs). (due 2025-09-05)
- Tom Li: spike tokenizer-shim on private agent, prototype hash-key cache strategy, measure delta. (due 2025-09-12)
- Priya Patel: update Optimize playbook entry with q4f recommendation + batch window tuning notes. Run bench across 3 model-variants and attach numbers. (due 2025-09-10)
- Jared (customer): try edge deploy and run provided 2k query script, return logs and sample failing SKUs. (tentative by 2025-09-09)

Notes to self / loose thoughts:
- Consider adding a pre-tokenization diagnostic on the Console that can show token counts per field before sending to model (useful for customers with big lexicons).
- Could be a pattern for a productized "Normalization and Token Cost" audit (billed consulting).
- Watch out for GDPR/PII stripping when normalizing — add config toggle to disable certain transforms.

Meeting follow-up: schedule 30m sync next week with Jared + eng spike owners to sign off on shim and canary fallback UX.

End of scratchpad.

title: ArgentWare — inference intake & ops scratchpad
preview:
Purpose: capture intake call (07/11), technical constraints, risk/gotchas, rough perf sizing, and immediate next steps for ArgentWare pilot (on‑prem, regulated manufacturing telemetry use case).

Call summary (notes):
- Attendees: Marta Ruiz (ArgentWare CTO), Javier Kim (Edge infra lead), Priya Shah (Redwood SE), Ethan Park (Redwood SRE), Lina Gomez (Redwood solutions).
- High level: ArgentWare wants a private deployment inside their corporate DC (not public cloud) to power an internal incident summarization assistant and a parts-recommendation tool. Data is sensitive (customer serial numbers + manufacturing logs).
- Primary KPIs: sub-600ms P99 generation latency for short prompts (<= 250 tokens) under steady load; support peak bursts up to ~180 concurrent sessions during shift change; cost/ops must be manageable for a 30‑user engineering trial progressing to 500+ users in three months.

Key constraints called out by Javier:
- On‑prem cluster: 4 x 80GB GPUs available initially (NVIDIA H100 or A100 depending on deliverable). Rack has limited egress and strict firewall; outbound only through proxy.
- No data may leave the subnet without explicit encryption+audit; preferred KMS is HashiCorp Vault (already used for secrets).
- Ops team wants minimal changes to Kubernetes control plane — prefer running Redwood Private with our agent + sidecars versus full managed control plane.

Immediate technical ask from ArgentWare:
1) Validate that a 4x80GB GPU set can meet their P99 latency target for the selected model family (open‑weights preference).
2) Provide a deployment pattern (manifest + network diagram) that uses existing Vault + internal Prometheus/ELK stack for logs/metrics.
3) Confirm how prefix/KV caching handles repeated session summaries (they expect many repeated prefixes) and whether we can limit cache size per-customer.

Preliminary sizing & notes (SE quick calc):
- Workload shape: ~200 requests/minute steady during day, bursts to 1,200 RPM during shift change (10–15 min windows). Typical prompt sizes: 40–200 tokens, average response 220 tokens.
- Goal latency: P50 ~120–180ms, P95 < 350ms, P99 < 600ms for single‑turn prompts. Streaming acceptable for longer outputs.
- Model candidate: 7B–13B open model (ArgentWare prefers smaller for onprem). If we use a quantized 13B (int8 / q4_k_m), throughput per 80GB GPU estimated ~12–18 tokens/sec for generation depending on kernel. With KV cache and batching we can improve effective throughput.
- Rough capacity: 4x80GB could handle the steady baseline plus some burst headroom if we enable request batching and prefix caching and pin hot models to all GPUs. For consistent P99 at peak bursts we should plan for at least +1 spare GPU (i.e., recommend 5x80GB equivalent) or enable a fallback to a smaller model for non-critical requests.

Operational notes & gotchas (from call + SE experience):
- Firewall/proxy: The private control plane will need egress for license checks/updates unless they want fully air-gapped. Clarify Javier's appetite for periodic outbound. If air‑gapped, we need an offline bundle and documented upgrade flow.
- Vault integration: they use Vault transit for key operations. Redwood Private supports KMS integrations but we need to test the transit signature flow for session tokens (file an integration ticket).
- Logs/auditing: ArgentWare wants per-request audit logs accessible in their ELK; ensure we provide structured log format and a small adapter to forward with proper PII redaction.
- Autoscaling constraints: their infra team is cautious about autoscaling GPUs due to power/cost. Recommend conservative autoscaling (manual scale up windows) + built-in throttles in the control plane.
- Model quantization/accuracy tradeoffs: we must preface any quantized model recommendation with evaluation on their prompt set — quality regression checks are a must.

Security / compliance touches:
- Data residency: all inference input + transient outputs must remain in network boundary; confirm retention policy for logs (they prefer 30 days then purge).
- Authentication: SSO via Okta — they want token exchange flow so users are mapped to internal IDs. Provide sample JWT exchange pattern.
- Legal: Marta asked for a short paper describing how Redwood handles data separation for multi-tenant offerings even though this will be private — prepare a 'data handling one-pager' for procurement.

Open questions (to resolve before next call):
- Javier: can we get root cause of GPU model on racks? Are the 4x80GB physical GPUs contiguous in one rack (network/hot-swap considerations)?
- Marta: which open models have internal licensing they are comfortable with? Are we allowed to ship quantized checkpoint under their procurement terms?
- Ops: offline upgrade acceptable? If yes, what cadence (monthly/quarterly)?
- UX: should summarization be synchronous (low-latency) or async with notification? Prefer synchronous for first pass but may offload long jobs.

Suggested pilot plan (milestones):
- Day 0–3: access + network baseline. Obtain read-only Vault creds for SE account, confirm outbound constraints.
- Week 1: deploy Redwood Private agent in staging subnet, connect metrics forwarding to their Prometheus, smoke test with small sample prompts. (Owner: Lina)
- Week 2: run quality sweep with 1000 prompt pairs (their production set) across 2 quantization profiles (fp16 and int8), produce regression report. (Owner: Priya + Ethan)
- Week 3: tune batching/cache config, run load test to emulate shift-change burst (1,200 RPM). Validate P99. (Owner: Ethan)
- Week 4: handoff to ArgentWare ops, prepare runbook (upgrade + incident playbook + log forwarding). (Owner: Samir)

Action items (owners + due dates):
- Priya: draft 'data handling one-pager' for procurement — due 2025-07-18.
- Ethan: perf/load test harness (k6 + synthetic prompts) and initial results — due 2025-07-22.
- Lina: prepare proposed deployment manifest + network diagram and sample Prometheus config — due 2025-07-16.
- Samir: open integration ticket for Vault transit + ELK forwarder (link to JIRA: AW-1123) — due 2025-07-15.

Notes to self:
- When testing quantized 13B ensure to capture hallucination metrics specific to instructions like "bill of materials mismatch" — manufacturing wants high precision.
- Add prompt templates for parts-recommendation where we include structured context (part IDs, sensor deltas, timestamp). Try to preserve structured output (JSON) using tool-calling/structured output mode.
- Consider recommending an LRU cap on KV cache per project to avoid memory blowouts when many sessions create large prefixes.

Meeting ask for next call (tentative 07/23):
- Show early perf numbers and quality sweep.
- Confirm air-gap vs limited egress decision.
- Walk through runbook and escalation path.

Appendix: sample short prompt (for initial perf test):
- Input: "Machine 4 (SN: A45-9QZ) reported spindle vibration spike: {sensor_deltas}. Suggest top-3 probable causes and one immediate mitigation action. Provide component list if replacement needed."
- Expect structured output: {"causes": [...], "immediate_action": "...", "parts": [{"part_id": "...","qty": 1}]}

End of scratchpad.

(If we need to spin this into a customer-facing SOW, convert milestones into deliverables + acceptance criteria. Keep this as working notes — will update after perf run.)
