title: Aerodyne Mobility — Pilot hypothesis & outbound log
preview:
Notes to self: quick, messy capture for Aerodyne Mobility outreach and pilot hypothesis. Keep informal. Goals: qualify fit fast, get a tech convo, scope a 6-week pilot that proves latency and reliability improvements for their dispatcher/route-assist use case.

Account context
- Company: Aerodyne Mobility (fleet telematics + routing for mid-size last-mile fleets). Heavy on real-time route updates, incident resolution workflows, and driver-dispatch chat.
- Org signals: posted job for 'edge ML infra' (Nov 2024). Funding: Series B-ish, expanding operations to 3 regions.
- Tech stack hints: Kubernetes + Kafka + Redis, some on-prem data retention requirements for EU customers.
- Likely persona champions: Head of Platform (SRE-ish), Head of Ops (dispatcher/product), Head of Security/Legal for data residency.

Why Redwood might matter to them (hypothesis)
- Problem: dispatcher/driver workflows require sub-200ms p95 responses for automated suggestions (reroutes, ETA recalcs, context summaries). Their current pipeline uses REST microservices and occasional human in-loop for triage.
- Hypothesis: moving LLM-powered summarization + intent classification into a low-latency inference path can reduce manual triage and mean time to resolution (~20-30% improvement) while keeping cost predictable.
- Redwood fit: dedicated pools for predictable throughput, Private (VPC) for sensitive fleet telemetry, automatic KV caching to avoid re-computing context when drivers reconnect. Also rollout controls for canarying new models against baseline heuristics.

Key technical constraints to surface early
- Latency: target <200ms p95 for single-turn generation (short prompts, ~100 tokens). If they expect sub-100ms we need to probe hardware and acceptable batching.
- Throughput: daytime spikes (08:00–10:30, 16:00–19:00) with bursts from many drivers; autoscale and burst capacity needed.
- Data residency: EU customers require logs/anonymized telemetry kept within region; Private deployment or Dedicated with region pinning likely.
- Model quality: high need for factual behavior (no hallucination on routes); prefer smaller curated open models + Redwood Optimize suggestions.
- Network topology: some fleet gateways are on unreliable cellular; need graceful degradation and offline fallbacks.

Outbound sequence (short, 3-touch) — test these subject lines/snippets
- Email 1 subject: "Quick idea: 6-week pilot to cut dispatcher triage time at Aerodyne"
  Snippet: "Ava from Redwood — we help teams run low-latency inference (hosted/dedicated/private). Thinking about a focused pilot to speed up dispatcher triage with predictable costs. 15-min call?"
- Email 2 subject: "Pilot scope sketch + SLOs (sub-200ms p95)"
  Snippet: "Short note attaching a one-pager: goals, success metrics, minimal integration touch (webhook + tokenized logs)."
- LinkedIn/Slack nudge: keep light — mention the scheduling window and a quick customer example (anonymized) where p95 dropped from 450ms to 160ms after moving to dedicated inference.

Call agenda (15 minutes)
- 1 min intro + context
- 3 min quick problem confirmation (current pain: triage time, false positives, driver friction)
- 5 min proposed pilot (what we deploy where, data flows, SLOs)
- 3 min security/data residency questions
- 3 min next steps (tech sync vs legal introduction)

Pilot proposal (MVP) — 6 weeks- Scope: attach Redwood Dedicated service for a single region + lightweight Private control-plane for audit; integrate with their staging stream (Redwood ingests tokenized, redacted events).
- Goals/SLOs: <200ms p95 generation for short suggestions, 20% reduction in dispatcher manual triage, <1% regression on false intent detection vs baseline rules.
- Deliverables: infra runbook, one integration endpoint (webhook/SDK), dashboard access, daily cost/latency report, two model rollouts (baseline -> tuned).
- Exit criteria: measurable metric improvements + decision to extend to two more regions or to move to dedicated yearly commit.

Objection handling cheatsheet (rough)
- "We can't trust LLM outputs / hallucinations": propose constrained structured outputs (JSON enums for intent/safety), add a policy layer that falls back to rules for critical flows. Show examples from Redwood Optimize where prompt sets reduce hallucinations.
- "Security / data residency": recommend Private deployment or Dedicated with region pinning, KMS/HSM integration, and retention window controls. Offer security review + SOC2 artifact.
- "Cost unpredictability": surface batching + caching knobs from Redwood Optimize to cap tokens and use model quantization recommendations; provide a cost projection with 95% CI for pilot.
- "Integration work is too heavy": demo lightweight SDK and webhook flow; propose 1-week stub integration (no schema changes) to prove concept.
- "We do inference on-edge / on-device": suggest hybrid: local lightweight model for extreme low-latency critical path + Redwood for richer summarization and orchestration when link available.

Success metrics to measure in pilot (what I'll ask for)
- Latency: p95 and p99 for generation calls
- Accuracy: intent classification F1 vs annotated sample set (we'll help create this)
- Operational: rate of fallback to human triage, number of manual corrections per 100 suggestions
- Cost: $ per 1k suggestions and projected monthly run-rate at scale

Personalized talking points for each persona
- Head of Platform (SRE): focus on predictable capacity, autoscale policies, and observability (per-route traces, token-level costs). Show how rollout + automatic fallback keep the system stable during peak.
- Head of Ops (dispatcher / product): focus on time saved per shift, reduction in context switching, and improved driver satisfaction. Offer to run a short A/B on 10% of shifts.
- Head of Security/Legal: emphasize Private deployment, KMS integration, retention controls, and audit logs plus a security review.

Example quick outbound copy (raw)
- Subject: "Pilot idea: 20% faster dispatcher triage (no heavy infra change)"
  Body: "Hi <Name>, quick note — we help fleet platforms run low-latency LLM inference with predictable economics. For Aerodyne specifically, I think a 6-week pilot could cut dispatcher triage time by ~20% while keeping EU customer data in-region. No schema changes required for the first week (webhook + sample redaction). If you have 20 minutes this week I can share a one-pager and a sample runbook. — Ava"

Example technical note for engineering sync (raw)
- Integration points: stage event stream -> redwood ingest (SDK) -> model for classification + summarization -> HTTP webhook back to Aerodyne service. KV caching keyed by driver-session id to reduce token usage when drivers reconnect.
- Fallback policy: if p95 exceeds 300ms OR confidence < threshold -> send to rules engine and flag for human triage.

Open questions / research items
- Need to confirm their real p95 current (ask them to share small trace sample).
- Confirm what telemetry we can ingest for pilot (PII/redaction constraints).
- Get a contact in security/legal for a raw checklist and any required DPA terms.
- Check whether they prefer region-specific Dedicated vs Private (cost delta).

Action items (who)
- Book intro: Ava (me) to send email + two 15-min options this week. (TODO: send by 2025-03-05)
- Tech contact warm intro: Diego to pull any past SRE notes we can reference. (TODO: ping Diego in #g2m-tech)
- Security artifacts: Leah to attach SOC2 + KMS note to next follow-up. (TODO: prepare by 2025-03-07)
- Create pilot one-pager & SLO appendix: Ava + Diego (draft to share for review). (TODO: draft by 2025-03-06)

Random quick reminders
- Use a concrete customer vignette (anonymized) when nudging – numbers land better than generic claims.
- Keep the first tech convo very focused (latency & data flows) — don't try to sell model topology yet.
- If they push on on-device, prepare a hybrid narrative + small reference architecture.

Scratch area (phrases / quick answers to drop into replies)
- "We can pin capacity to your region and provide audited logs — no telemetry leaves region without your explicit config."
- "For production read paths we recommend Dedicated + Private control plane for the best balance of predictability and auditability."
- "We typically recommend measuring p95 and fallback rate in the first two weeks and using that to size a dedicated pool for steady-state."

End of notes. Next step: craft the short one-pager and attach to initial outreach. Keep this doc as the working log; will move to shared_drives when pilot approved.
text:
Notes to self: quick, messy capture for Aerodyne Mobility outreach and pilot hypothesis. Keep informal. Goals: qualify fit fast, get a tech convo, scope a 6-week pilot that proves latency and reliability improvements for their dispatcher/route-assist use case.

Account context
- Company: Aerodyne Mobility (fleet telematics + routing for mid-size last-mile fleets). Heavy on real-time route updates, incident resolution workflows, and driver-dispatch chat.
- Org signals: posted job for 'edge ML infra' (Nov 2024). Funding: Series B-ish, expanding operations to 3 regions.
- Tech stack hints: Kubernetes + Kafka + Redis, some on-prem data retention requirements for EU customers.
- Likely persona champions: Head of Platform (SRE-ish), Head of Ops (dispatcher/product), Head of Security/Legal for data residency.

Why Redwood might matter to them (hypothesis)
- Problem: dispatcher/driver workflows require sub-200ms p95 responses for automated suggestions (reroutes, ETA recalcs, context summaries). Their current pipeline uses REST microservices and occasional human in-loop for triage.
- Hypothesis: moving LLM-powered summarization + intent classification into a low-latency inference path can reduce manual triage and mean time to resolution (~20-30% improvement) while keeping cost predictable.
- Redwood fit: dedicated pools for predictable throughput, Private (VPC) for sensitive fleet telemetry, automatic KV caching to avoid re-computing context when drivers reconnect. Also rollout controls for canarying new models against baseline heuristics.

Key technical constraints to surface early
- Latency: target <200ms p95 for single-turn generation (short prompts, ~100 tokens). If they expect sub-100ms we need to probe hardware and acceptable batching.
- Throughput: daytime spikes (08:00–10:30, 16:00–19:00) with bursts from many drivers; autoscale and burst capacity needed.
- Data residency: EU customers require logs/anonymized telemetry kept within region; Private deployment or Dedicated with region pinning likely.
- Model quality: high need for factual behavior (no hallucination on routes); prefer smaller curated open models + Redwood Optimize suggestions.
- Network topology: some fleet gateways are on unreliable cellular; need graceful degradation and offline fallbacks.

Outbound sequence (short, 3-touch) — test these subject lines/snippets
- Email 1 subject: "Quick idea: 6-week pilot to cut dispatcher triage time at Aerodyne"
  Snippet: "Ava from Redwood — we help teams run low-latency inference (hosted/dedicated/private). Thinking about a focused pilot to speed up dispatcher triage with predictable costs. 15-min call?"
- Email 2 subject: "Pilot scope sketch + SLOs (sub-200ms p95)"
  Snippet: "Short note attaching a one-pager: goals, success metrics, minimal integration touch (webhook + tokenized logs)."
- LinkedIn/Slack nudge: keep light — mention the scheduling window and a quick customer example (anonymized) where p95 dropped from 450ms to 160ms after moving to dedicated inference.

Call agenda (15 minutes)
- 1 min intro + context
- 3 min quick problem confirmation (current pain: triage time, false positives, driver friction)
- 5 min proposed pilot (what we deploy where, data flows, SLOs)
- 3 min security/data residency questions
- 3 min next steps (tech sync vs legal introduction)

Pilot proposal (MVP) — 6 weeks- Scope: attach Redwood Dedicated service for a single region + lightweight Private control-plane for audit; integrate with their staging stream (Redwood ingests tokenized, redacted events).
- Goals/SLOs: <200ms p95 generation for short suggestions, 20% reduction in dispatcher manual triage, <1% regression on false intent detection vs baseline rules.
- Deliverables: infra runbook, one integration endpoint (webhook/SDK), dashboard access, daily cost/latency report, two model rollouts (baseline -> tuned).
- Exit criteria: measurable metric improvements + decision to extend to two more regions or to move to dedicated yearly commit.

Objection handling cheatsheet (rough)
- "We can't trust LLM outputs / hallucinations": propose constrained structured outputs (JSON enums for intent/safety), add a policy layer that falls back to rules for critical flows. Show examples from Redwood Optimize where prompt sets reduce hallucinations.
- "Security / data residency": recommend Private deployment or Dedicated with region pinning, KMS/HSM integration, and retention window controls. Offer security review + SOC2 artifact.
- "Cost unpredictability": surface batching + caching knobs from Redwood Optimize to cap tokens and use model quantization recommendations; provide a cost projection with 95% CI for pilot.
- "Integration work is too heavy": demo lightweight SDK and webhook flow; propose 1-week stub integration (no schema changes) to prove concept.
- "We do inference on-edge / on-device": suggest hybrid: local lightweight model for extreme low-latency critical path + Redwood for richer summarization and orchestration when link available.

Success metrics to measure in pilot (what I'll ask for)
- Latency: p95 and p99 for generation calls
- Accuracy: intent classification F1 vs annotated sample set (we'll help create this)
- Operational: rate of fallback to human triage, number of manual corrections per 100 suggestions
- Cost: $ per 1k suggestions and projected monthly run-rate at scale

Personalized talking points for each persona
- Head of Platform (SRE): focus on predictable capacity, autoscale policies, and observability (per-route traces, token-level costs). Show how rollout + automatic fallback keep the system stable during peak.
- Head of Ops (dispatcher / product): focus on time saved per shift, reduction in context switching, and improved driver satisfaction. Offer to run a short A/B on 10% of shifts.
- Head of Security/Legal: emphasize Private deployment, KMS integration, retention controls, and audit logs plus a security review.

Example quick outbound copy (raw)
- Subject: "Pilot idea: 20% faster dispatcher triage (no heavy infra change)"
  Body: "Hi <Name>, quick note — we help fleet platforms run low-latency LLM inference with predictable economics. For Aerodyne specifically, I think a 6-week pilot could cut dispatcher triage time by ~20% while keeping EU customer data in-region. No schema changes required for the first week (webhook + sample redaction). If you have 20 minutes this week I can share a one-pager and a sample runbook. — Ava"

Example technical note for engineering sync (raw)
- Integration points: stage event stream -> redwood ingest (SDK) -> model for classification + summarization -> HTTP webhook back to Aerodyne service. KV caching keyed by driver-session id to reduce token usage when drivers reconnect.
- Fallback policy: if p95 exceeds 300ms OR confidence < threshold -> send to rules engine and flag for human triage.

Open questions / research items
- Need to confirm their real p95 current (ask them to share small trace sample).
- Confirm what telemetry we can ingest for pilot (PII/redaction constraints).
- Get a contact in security/legal for a raw checklist and any required DPA terms.
- Check whether they prefer region-specific Dedicated vs Private (cost delta).

Action items (who)
- Book intro: Ava (me) to send email + two 15-min options this week. (TODO: send by 2025-03-05)
- Tech contact warm intro: Diego to pull any past SRE notes we can reference. (TODO: ping Diego in #g2m-tech)
- Security artifacts: Leah to attach SOC2 + KMS note to next follow-up. (TODO: prepare by 2025-03-07)
- Create pilot one-pager & SLO appendix: Ava + Diego (draft to share for review). (TODO: draft by 2025-03-06)

Random quick reminders
- Use a concrete customer vignette (anonymized) when nudging – numbers land better than generic claims.
- Keep the first tech convo very focused (latency & data flows) — don't try to sell model topology yet.
- If they push on on-device, prepare a hybrid narrative + small reference architecture.

Scratch area (phrases / quick answers to drop into replies)
- "We can pin capacity to your region and provide audited logs — no telemetry leaves region without your explicit config."
- "For production read paths we recommend Dedicated + Private control plane for the best balance of predictability and auditability."
- "We typically recommend measuring p95 and fallback rate in the first two weeks and using that to size a dedicated pool for steady-state."

End of notes. Next step: craft the short one-pager and attach to initial outreach. Keep this doc as the working log; will move to shared_drives when pilot approved.

title: Compact claims & prep — Q1 reflection
preview:
Topline context
- Role: People Ops (IC, partnering to scale onboarding + talent processes).
- Timeframe: Jan–Mar 2025 (Q1). Current sprint rhythm: 2-week cycles, three focus areas below.
- Tone: compact claims for my manager sync and my self-review; quick evidence bullets, then short scripts for conversation.

Three compact claims (what I want to own in the conversation)
1) I improved onboarding time-to-productivity by removing two major handoffs (claim).
   - Evidence: reduced median time-to-first-contribution from 16 -> 11 days for eng new hires in cohort Jan-Feb (source: onboarding-tracker sheet).
   - Concrete work: rewrote the L0 checklist, automated the Slack welcome digest, ran two curated onboarding office hours.
   - Follow-up ask: request budget for an onboarding buddy stipends pilot (4 ppl @ $50/mo for 3 months).

2) I delivered a repeatable calibration rubric for career conversations (claim).
   - Evidence: pilot with 6 managers; rubric reduced time to prepare by ~35% (self-reported prep time in survey).
   - Artifacts: calibration-rubric-v1 (confluence), two sample templates I sent to eng managers.
   - Risks: rubric needs clearer examples for IC2-IC3 boundary; plan to iterate in April.

3) I launched a lightweight retention micro-experiment on role clarity for success profiles (claim).
   - Evidence: initial N=28 survey; +0.12 avg on intent-to-stay scale after 3 weeks for those who received role-clarity digest vs control. Not yet statistically significant but directional.
   - Next step: expand sample to 120 and run early analysis.

Manager sync scripts (short, practice lines)
- Opening: "Quick update — three compact claims I want to surface from Q1 and one ask for support."
- Claim framing: "Onboarding: we shortened TTFP by ~5 days for recent cohorts; with a small stipend pilot I think we can standardize buddy availability and scale the effect."
- Ask phrasing: "Would you support a $2k pilot budget and a 30-min intro with (Head of Eng) to align incentives?"
- Calibration: "Rubric is working, need example snippets for mid-levels — could you introduce me to 1-2 managers who consistently give clear examples in calibration so I can co-create?"

Quick evidence snippets (copyable into review)
- Onboarding metric: Median days to first PR = 11 (cohort Jan–Feb), historical baseline = 16 (Nov–Dec). Data source: onboarding-tracker (sheet 2025).
- Office hours: 8 sessions run, avg attendance 6, NPS quick pulse +4 (n=18 responses).
- Rubric pilot: 6 managers, average prep time reported down from 45m -> 29m per calibration.
- Retention micro-experiment: N=28, delta +0.12 on 7-pt intent scale (directional).

Notes-to-self / awkward signals to watch for
- If manager asks for long-term ROI, I should be ready to show estimated cost-per-hire improvement, not just days saved (build quick calc before sync).
- If they push back on stipend: present alternate low-cost incentive (gift-card raffle for buddies) and show pilot trade-offs.
- When discussing rubric, emphasize manager time saved over headcount impact — it's easier to get buy-in that way.

Open questions (want manager input)
- Prioritization: Should I prioritize scaling onboarding buddy stipends or spend time on example-writing for the rubric?
- Sponsorship: Can you introduce me to a manager in Platform Eng who'd trial the stipend model?
- Calibration rollout: preferred cadence for moving from pilot -> team-wide? (2 cohorts or 4 cohorts?)

Action items (next 2 weeks)
- Build 1-slide ROI calc for onboarding stipend pilot (deliverable: slide + 1-paragraph ask) — owner: me — due: 2025-03-10.
- Expand retention micro-experiment to 120 participants (work with data analyst) — owner: me + Marcus — due: 2025-03-24.
- Draft 3 concrete rubric examples for IC2/IC3 boundaries and circulate to Priya for feedback — due: 2025-03-14.

Micro-experiments & metrics (running)
- Buddy stipend pilot (proposal stage): metric = median TT1PR, secondary = time-to-mentor-first-message, cost = stipend * participants.
- Role-clarity digest (A/B): metric = intent-to-stay score, follow-up = retention at 90d.
- Calibration template adoption: metric = #managers using template / total managers in pilot, secondary = prep time reduction.

Short self-reflection (two lines)
- Win: shipping operational changes that actually decreased friction (felt tangible).
- Opportunity: need to get better at linking these wins to measurable business outcomes (revenue/MTTR/hiring efficiency).

Meeting prep checklist (for the 15-min manager sync)
- <2 min: one-sentence lead: current state + trend.
- <5 min: two slides or one slide + one blurb with the three claims.
- <5 min: ask + options (two budget/scale options).
- 3 min: confirm next steps and owners.

Fragments / scratch ideas (for future notes)
- Gamify buddy onboarding with "first-five" mission checklist and badges.
- Capture a short 90s video intro to the rubric for managers who skip docs.
- Run a micro-incentive experiment where teams nominate top buddy and PMs give 1 hour of time credit.

Follow-ups (who to ping)
- Marcus Lin: data analyst alignment for retention experiment (ping by 2025-03-06).
- Priya Desai: rubric example feedback (share draft by 2025-03-12).
- Head of Eng (ask via manager): align on stipend pilot feasibility.

End notes
- Keep this doc intentionally compact — drop in a few new evidence snippets before the manager sync.
- If the ask is accepted, create a short public summary doc for FYI to the wider people-ops channel.

---
Revision history (quick)
- 2025-02-10: created initial claims and evidence.
- 2025-02-27: added pilot numbers + adjusted action items after office hours feedback.
- 2025-03-04: tightened manager scripts; updated metrics for onboarding TT1PR.

title: Adaptive system instruction overlays - scratchpad
preview:
Purpose / quick summary:

This is a working sketch for an "adaptive overlay" subsystem: a lightweight, versioned layer of system-instruction fragments ("overlays") that can be composed at inference time to provide app-, user-, or session-specific guidance to models without requiring full model fine-tuning or unsafe ad-hoc system prompts.

Goals:
- Allow apps to attach small, structured instructions that modify behavior (tone, safety filters, domain constraints) at request time.
- Make overlays composable and precedence-ordered (global < app < user < session < ephemeral).
- Support partial patching (diffs) and rollbacks with lightweight versioning.
- Integrate with Redwood routing/caching so overlays are cache-key aware and don't blow up batching efficiency.
- Make it easy for platform teams to audit and enforce safety constraints on overlays.

High-level concepts:
- Overlay: small structured object containing named sections (system_text, style_hint, banned_phrases, allowed_tools, param_limits, metadata).
- Overlay ID + version (ovrl-<sha256>-vN).
- Scope: global | org | app | user | session | request-ephemeral.
- Merge strategy: override | append | whitelist-merge | patch-diff.
- TTL / Ephemeral lifetime: number of requests or wall-clock seconds.

Example overlay (JSON-ish):

  overlay = {
    "id": "ovrl-a3f4d2",
    "version": "v2",
    "scope": "user",
    "sections": {
      "system_text": "You are an assistant that answers concisely in bullet points.",
      "style_hint": "concise,business-formal",
      "banned_phrases": ["I am not sure"],
      "allowed_tools": ["web_search"],
      "param_limits": {"max_tokens": "512", "temperature": "0.2"}
    },
    "precedence": 70,
    "created_by": "app-service",
    "created_at": "2025-02-11T09:12:00Z"
  }

API shape (sketch) - CRUD + attach:

- POST /v1/overlays
  body: { name, scope, sections, merge_strategy, tags }
  returns: { overlay_id, version }

- PATCH /v1/overlays/{overlay_id}
  body: { patch_ops: [ {op: "replace"|"add"|"remove", path: "/sections/system_text", value: "..." } ], new_version_meta }
  returns: { overlay_id, version }

- GET /v1/overlays/{overlay_id}
  returns full overlay manifest

- POST /v1/requests (when making inference call) - overlay attach support:
  request body example:
  {
    "model": "redwood-13b-chat",
    "input": [...],
    "overlay_refs": [ {"id": "ovrl-global-1", "mode": "merge"}, {"id": "ovrl-user-a3f4d2", "mode": "override"} ],
    "overlay_inline": { "sections": { "system_text": "Also prefer short tables for outputs" }, "precedence": 99 }
  }

Runtime overlay resolution algorithm (rough):
1) Collect overlays in order of increasing scope (global -> org -> app -> user -> session -> inline-request).
2) Sort by precedence numeric (fall back to scope default precedence).
3) For each section, apply merge strategy:
   - override: last-writer wins
   - append: concatenate strings with a separator (
---
)
   - whitelist-merge: union of lists, capped at 200 items
   - patch-diff: apply JSON-Patch sequence
4) Produce final synthesized system_text + structured hints to hand to model runtime.

Cache and batching considerations:
- Overlays affect prompt text, so overlay_refs must be included in cache keys.
- To avoid high cardinality cache explosion, compute an overlay-fingerprint: sha256(sorted(overlay_ids + versions + precedence + small hash of inline)). If fingerprint is in hotset then batch normally.
- Recommend restricting inline overlays for high-QPS endpoints — encourage pre-created overlay IDs for stable configs.

Security & governance:
- Admin-managed allowlist/denylist of overlay sections that non-admins can change (e.g., disallow changing safety filters).
- Audit log per overlay version and per-request resolution: store overlay_fingerprint, resolved_text, who attached, request id.
- Enforce server-side token limits: overlay + input + expected output <= model_max_sequence.

Rollouts, canaries, and safety testing:
- Overlay versions support "rollout" metadata: percent, target_orgs, test_users.
- A/B test support: overlay_ref can include traffic_split metadata so router can attach variant overlay_ids per request for eval.
- Quality/eval hook: when overlay attached, associate with eval prompt set to monitor hallucination and safety regressions.

Monitoring / metrics:
- overlay_attach_rate (per overlay_id)
- overlay_collision_count (requests where overlay merge caused conflict)
- avg_tokens_by_overlay_fingerprint
- eval_regression_alerts (overlay_id -> list of failing evals)

Operational UX notes:
- Developer flow: create overlay in Console -> preview resolved system_text (select scopes) -> dry-run on sample prompts -> publish -> attach overlay_id in API.
- Quick-create templates for common patterns: "concise", "code-first", "strict-safety", "qa-mode".

Edge cases / open questions:
- Conflicting style_hint vectors (one says formal, other says playful) -> need a deterministic resolution (priority or style fusion?).
- How to encode param_limits that respect both overlay and model runtime (e.g., request asks for max_tokens > allowed). Enforce server-side min/max with clear error.
- Tool access: overlay grants allowed_tools; how to reconcile with org-level tool restrictions? intersection by default.
- How big can an overlay be? enforce 8KB manifest cap to avoid latency blowup.

ASCII diagrams (flow) :

  [Client] --> [API Proxy] --> [Router] --> [Overlay Resolver & Cache]
                      |                 |
                      |                 v
                      |            [Overlay Store (metadata)]
                      |                 |
                      v                 v
                 [Auth/Quota]       [Synthesized system_text]
                      \\                /
                       \\              /
                        -> [Serving runtime + Tokenizer] -> [Model]

Example request timeline (worker perspective):
- receive request w/ overlay_refs -> look up overlay metadata versions -> compute fingerprint -> check cache for resolved prompt -> if miss, resolve merges, render final system_text, put into LRU cache -> prepare runtime inputs -> record overlay_attach event -> send to model.

Lightweight storage model suggestion:
- Overlays metadata stored in primary store (Postgres) keyed by id+version, with blob for sections.
- Small in-memory LRU cache of resolved fingerprints -> resolved_text (TTL short, e.g., 30s) and longer analytic store for audit logs.

Developer ergonomics examples (CLI / quick):
- rwd overlay create --name concise-sales --scope app --sections.system_text "Answer in 3 bullets" --sections.style_hint concise
- rwd overlay preview --overlay ovrl-concise-sales --prompt-file examples/q1.txt

Testing checklist (manual):
- [ ] Create overlay, preview, attach to staging app, run 1000 sample requests ensure p95 latency unaffected.
- [ ] Verify audit logs capture overlay fingerprint and resolved_text.
- [ ] Verify that inline overlays with huge content are rejected in high-QPS endpoints.
- [ ] Simulate conflicting tool grants between org and overlay -> ensure deny wins.

Implementation milestones (rough):
- Phase 0 (spike, 2w): design API shape, simple store, naive merge impl in proxy to test UX.
- Phase 1 (4w): integrate with router + cache key changes, console UI preview, basic governance checks.
- Phase 2 (6w): rollout support, canary, metrics, audit persistence, optimization of in-memory cache.

Notes to self / TODOs:
- Pick canonical merge semantics doc and put in Confluence + link from Console.
- Check cost model: storing lots of overlay variants could increase token cost; surface estimated token delta in console at create-time.
- Ask SRE: how much memory for storing resolved_text cache per instance? set default TTL conservatively.

Open research prompts for discussion with ML: does providing many tiny system_text fragments degrade model calibration? need small eval comparing single large system_text vs merged fragments.

-- end of scratchpad --

This is a working sketch for an "adaptive overlay" subsystem: a lightweight, versioned layer of system-instruction fragments ("overlays") that can be composed at inference time to provide app-, user-, or session-specific guidance to models without requiring full model fine-tuning or unsafe ad-hoc system prompts.

Goals:
- Allow apps to attach small, structured instructions that modify behavior (tone, safety filters, domain constraints) at request time.
- Make overlays composable and precedence-ordered (global < app < user < session < ephemeral).
- Support partial patching (diffs) and rollbacks with lightweight versioning.
- Integrate with Redwood routing/caching so overlays are cache-key aware and don't blow up batching efficiency.
- Make it easy for platform teams to audit and enforce safety constraints on overlays.

High-level concepts:
- Overlay: small structured object containing named sections (system_text, style_hint, banned_phrases, allowed_tools, param_limits, metadata).
- Overlay ID + version (ovrl-<sha256>-vN).
- Scope: global | org | app | user | session | request-ephemeral.
- Merge strategy: override | append | whitelist-merge | patch-diff.
- TTL / Ephemeral lifetime: number of requests or wall-clock seconds.

Example overlay (JSON-ish):

  overlay = {
    "id": "ovrl-a3f4d2",
    "version": "v2",
    "scope": "user",
    "sections": {
      "system_text": "You are an assistant that answers concisely in bullet points.",
      "style_hint": "concise,business-formal",
      "banned_phrases": ["I am not sure"],
      "allowed_tools": ["web_search"],
      "param_limits": {"max_tokens": "512", "temperature": "0.2"}
    },
    "precedence": 70,
    "created_by": "app-service",
    "created_at": "2025-02-11T09:12:00Z"
  }

API shape (sketch) - CRUD + attach:

- POST /v1/overlays
  body: { name, scope, sections, merge_strategy, tags }
  returns: { overlay_id, version }

- PATCH /v1/overlays/{overlay_id}
  body: { patch_ops: [ {op: "replace"|"add"|"remove", path: "/sections/system_text", value: "..." } ], new_version_meta }
  returns: { overlay_id, version }

- GET /v1/overlays/{overlay_id}
  returns full overlay manifest

- POST /v1/requests (when making inference call) - overlay attach support:
  request body example:
  {
    "model": "redwood-13b-chat",
    "input": [...],
    "overlay_refs": [ {"id": "ovrl-global-1", "mode": "merge"}, {"id": "ovrl-user-a3f4d2", "mode": "override"} ],
    "overlay_inline": { "sections": { "system_text": "Also prefer short tables for outputs" }, "precedence": 99 }
  }

Runtime overlay resolution algorithm (rough):
1) Collect overlays in order of increasing scope (global -> org -> app -> user -> session -> inline-request).
2) Sort by precedence numeric (fall back to scope default precedence).
3) For each section, apply merge strategy:
   - override: last-writer wins
   - append: concatenate strings with a separator (
---
)
   - whitelist-merge: union of lists, capped at 200 items
   - patch-diff: apply JSON-Patch sequence
4) Produce final synthesized system_text + structured hints to hand to model runtime.

Cache and batching considerations:
- Overlays affect prompt text, so overlay_refs must be included in cache keys.
- To avoid high cardinality cache explosion, compute an overlay-fingerprint: sha256(sorted(overlay_ids + versions + precedence + small hash of inline)). If fingerprint is in hotset then batch normally.
- Recommend restricting inline overlays for high-QPS endpoints — encourage pre-created overlay IDs for stable configs.

Security & governance:
- Admin-managed allowlist/denylist of overlay sections that non-admins can change (e.g., disallow changing safety filters).
- Audit log per overlay version and per-request resolution: store overlay_fingerprint, resolved_text, who attached, request id.
- Enforce server-side token limits: overlay + input + expected output <= model_max_sequence.

Rollouts, canaries, and safety testing:
- Overlay versions support "rollout" metadata: percent, target_orgs, test_users.
- A/B test support: overlay_ref can include traffic_split metadata so router can attach variant overlay_ids per request for eval.
- Quality/eval hook: when overlay attached, associate with eval prompt set to monitor hallucination and safety regressions.

Monitoring / metrics:
- overlay_attach_rate (per overlay_id)
- overlay_collision_count (requests where overlay merge caused conflict)
- avg_tokens_by_overlay_fingerprint
- eval_regression_alerts (overlay_id -> list of failing evals)

Operational UX notes:
- Developer flow: create overlay in Console -> preview resolved system_text (select scopes) -> dry-run on sample prompts -> publish -> attach overlay_id in API.
- Quick-create templates for common patterns: "concise", "code-first", "strict-safety", "qa-mode".

Edge cases / open questions:
- Conflicting style_hint vectors (one says formal, other says playful) -> need a deterministic resolution (priority or style fusion?).
- How to encode param_limits that respect both overlay and model runtime (e.g., request asks for max_tokens > allowed). Enforce server-side min/max with clear error.
- Tool access: overlay grants allowed_tools; how to reconcile with org-level tool restrictions? intersection by default.
- How big can an overlay be? enforce 8KB manifest cap to avoid latency blowup.

ASCII diagrams (flow) :

  [Client] --> [API Proxy] --> [Router] --> [Overlay Resolver & Cache]
                      |                 |
                      |                 v
                      |            [Overlay Store (metadata)]
                      |                 |
                      v                 v
                 [Auth/Quota]       [Synthesized system_text]
                      \\                /
                       \\              /
                        -> [Serving runtime + Tokenizer] -> [Model]

Example request timeline (worker perspective):
- receive request w/ overlay_refs -> look up overlay metadata versions -> compute fingerprint -> check cache for resolved prompt -> if miss, resolve merges, render final system_text, put into LRU cache -> prepare runtime inputs -> record overlay_attach event -> send to model.

Lightweight storage model suggestion:
- Overlays metadata stored in primary store (Postgres) keyed by id+version, with blob for sections.
- Small in-memory LRU cache of resolved fingerprints -> resolved_text (TTL short, e.g., 30s) and longer analytic store for audit logs.

Developer ergonomics examples (CLI / quick):
- rwd overlay create --name concise-sales --scope app --sections.system_text "Answer in 3 bullets" --sections.style_hint concise
- rwd overlay preview --overlay ovrl-concise-sales --prompt-file examples/q1.txt

Testing checklist (manual):
- [ ] Create overlay, preview, attach to staging app, run 1000 sample requests ensure p95 latency unaffected.
- [ ] Verify audit logs capture overlay fingerprint and resolved_text.
- [ ] Verify that inline overlays with huge content are rejected in high-QPS endpoints.
- [ ] Simulate conflicting tool grants between org and overlay -> ensure deny wins.

Implementation milestones (rough):
- Phase 0 (spike, 2w): design API shape, simple store, naive merge impl in proxy to test UX.
- Phase 1 (4w): integrate with router + cache key changes, console UI preview, basic governance checks.
- Phase 2 (6w): rollout support, canary, metrics, audit persistence, optimization of in-memory cache.

Notes to self / TODOs:
- Pick canonical merge semantics doc and put in Confluence + link from Console.
- Check cost model: storing lots of overlay variants could increase token cost; surface estimated token delta in console at create-time.
- Ask SRE: how much memory for storing resolved_text cache per instance? set default TTL conservatively.

Open research prompts for discussion with ML: does providing many tiny system_text fragments degrade model calibration? need small eval comparing single large system_text vs merged fragments.

-- end of scratchpad --

title: Caffeine skein: mini-eval notes
preview:
Purpose / context:
Quick personal scratchpad while I investigate a flurry of small regressions reported on hosted/chat endpoints. High-level goal: produce a tiny, repeatable micro-rubric and sampling checklist I can use for fast triage and for passing to QA or a Solutions Eng buddy. Keep it lightweight — this is intentionally messy.

Summary of what I saw (sketchy):
- Window: 2025-02-15 -> 2025-02-24; uptick in short-response hallucinations on policy-related prompts. Rate roughly 3x baseline for 2-token injections.
- Affected model variants: rdx-small-v2 (quantized) and rdx-small-v2-fp16; rdx-medium unaffected.
- Symptoms: confident but incorrect factual asserts, truncated follow-ups, context-dropping mid-dialog.
- Quick repro notes: often when prompt contains a short directive + URL, output replaces URL text with a plausible-sounding domain description.

Micro-rubric (ad-hoc, for quick triage):
Scoring scale: 0 (fail) / 1 (marginal) / 2 (good)
- Fidelity (are facts correct?): 0/1/2
- Attribution (does model respect explicit source tokens like URLs/quoted text?): 0/1/2
- Continuity (does it keep context across 2 turns?): 0/1/2
- Safety tags (any policy-violating phrasing?): 0/1/2
- Concision (response avoids hallucinated elaboration): 0/1/2
Notes: aim to keep evaluation <= 2 minutes per sample. If multiple 0s, escalate.

Sampling plan for a 30-sample quick audit:
- 10 synthetic prompts containing short directives + URLs (e.g., "summarize the page at https://example.com/guide")
- 10 2-turn dialogue prompts with small context shifts (add a follow-up like "what changed?")
- 10 real user traffic-derived prompts (redacted) from last 48h flagged by anomaly system
Randomize ordering; run on both quantized and fp16 variants. Capture raw response, top-p/temperature, token timeline, and token timestamps if available.

Sample log (raw-first-pass) — paste of a few outputs I captured:
1) Prompt: "Summarize: https://some-news.io/2025/02/feature"
Response (rdx-small-v2-q): "The Some News feature announced on Feb 2 added live widgets to user profiles, enabling cross-posting with SyncCorp."  <-- invented company; no such phrase in URL. Fidelity=0, Attribution=1, Continuity=2, Safety=2, Concision=0

2) Prompt: short dialog: "User: We changed the schedule. Assistant: OK. User: what was the original date?"
Response (rdx-small-v2-fp16): "It was March 4, 2024."  <-- context had no date. Fidelity=0, Attribution=2, Continuity=0, Safety=2, Concision=1

Immediate hypotheses (brain dump):
- Quantization path may be losing rare-token fidelity for entities in URLs; decoder substitutes plausible-seeming tokens when uncertain.
- FP16 runs show similar failure-modes but less frequent; could be seed or batching interaction.
- Recent kernel change (PR GH: redwood/serving-runtime#2031) touched attention position caching — maybe cache invalidation for short contexts introduced subtle corruption.

Quick checks to run (todo list):
- [ ] Re-run 30-sample audit with deterministic seed and batch size=1 on both variants. Record logits for first 12 tokens to inspect distribution.
- [ ] Compare outputs with older model snapshot from 2025-01-30 (tag: release-2025-01-30) to confirm regression window.
- [ ] Run unit test that injects quoted URL tokens and asserts exact echo when prompt asks to "repeat URL" (simple guard).
- [ ] Diff the kernel changes in GH PR #2031 for anything that touches KV-cache evictions.
- [ ] If repro is consistent, export a tiny repro package and open INF ticket with SRE/Runtime. Attach minimal prompt set + seed + model binary id.

Notes-to-self while debugging:
- Keep temperature at 0.0 for fidelity checks; stochasticity will mask determinism issues.
- Use both streaming and non-streaming modes; streaming buffer flushes sometimes hide token-level timing bugs.
- Annotate each sample with "echo-check" (did it preserve verbatim tokens) vs "semantic-check".

Questions / unresolved:
- Why is rdx-medium unaffected? Model architecture difference or different kernel code path triggers?
- Is this correlated with specific GPU types (A10 vs A100) in dedicated pools? Check infra telemetry for host mapping.

Quick findings after one re-run (notes from 2025-02-20 run):
- Deterministic run (seed fixed) reproduced the bad outputs on quantized variant ~9/30 samples.
- Logit inspection: first-token logits on substituted entity tokens had unusually high entropy (flat distribution) compared to nominal runs. Suggests uncertainty then greedy decode picks plausible token.
- Batch-size effect: batch=8 increased frequency of truncation/continuity loss relative to batch=1. Suspicious of batching/k-v cache mix.

Communication plan if this escalates:
- Short note to #eng-runtime with 3 bullet summary + attached minimal repro + suggested rollback candidate (rdx-small-v2-q: previous compiled kernel).
- If SRE confirms infra correlation, open an incident with dedicated capacity owners.

Random scribbles / heuristics that might help later:
- Add a synthetic unit test that appends a unique fingerprint string in URL (e.g., "?redwood-test=RND1234"). Check echo. If fingerprint missing or altered -> data corruption on input pipeline.
- Low-cost mitigation: add a soft-signal filter that detects when model invents company/entity names and surface a conservative fallback model (rdx-medium) when similarity to known hosts is low.

References and quick links: CON-1421 notes (internal confluence) for earlier URL-echo regressions; GH: redwood/repro-evals#328 is my ephemeral repro branch; JIRA INF-772 is placeholder if we escalate.

Closing note: this is rough; next step run the deterministic batch=1, seed-fixed audit and paste results into a tidy spreadsheet for sharing. If I get consistent repro, consolidate into a mini postmortem and propose either kernel rollback or additional guard tests in the serving runtime pipeline.

-- Rina K. (late-night scratchpad)

title: Cascade abstain protocol — draft notes
preview:
Purpose:

Short: sketch a lightweight, deployable protocol for when labelers and automated pipelines should return an explicit \"ABSTAIN\" label versus a best-effort label, and how to cascade those abstains into downstream routing (auto-adjudicate, human queue, escalate to SME, or accept fallback).

Context / motivation:
- We see a consistent fraction (~2.6% of samples) flagged by passive heuristics as \"uncertain\" and another small tail where model confidence disagrees with annotator confidence. When we force a best-effort label we create noisy signal; when we over-abstain we bloat the adjudication queue. Need a middle path.
- Goal: reduce false labels in production-sensitive fields (PII redaction, SKU mapping) while keeping human cost bounded.
- This doc is intentionally UX-lite: focus on rules and simple routing, not UI design.

Design principles:
- Deterministic: rules should be reproducible and spec-driven so we can audit label history.
- Granular abstain reasons: prefer small set of structured reasons (format, ambiguous-entity, multi-intent, cross-field contradiction, low-confidence-ml).
- Cascading gates: small fast heuristics first, then ML model checks, then lightweight adjudication (pair review), then SME.
- Sample economy: triage to prioritize samples with high production impact or high model disagreement.

Proposed minimal protocol (stages):
1) Inline heuristics (automated pre-check, 20–200ms):
   - regex-based format mismatch (date formats, currency weirdness) -> tag reason: format_mismatch -> auto-abstain if required by schema
   - obvious PII fuzzy matches in non-PII field -> tag reason: pii_crossfield -> auto-abstain
   - extreme length ( > 10k chars) or truncated markers (\"...continued\") -> tag reason: truncated_input -> auto-abstain or trim + retry
   Outcome: early auto-abstains are routed to the human queue only if flagged as \"sensitive\"; otherwise mark for low-priority review.
2) Model confidence + agreement checks (fast classification / ensemble):
   - primary model confidence < 0.45 and ensemble disagreement > 0.25 -> reason: low_confidence_ml -> propose abstain
   - model predicts label but top-2 labels close and label impacts downstream gating (e.g., safety) -> reason: ambiguous_topk -> flag for pair review
   Outcome: balanced: if downstream impact high => human review; if low impact => soft-abstain (record but not queue).
3) Lightweight adjudication (pair-review, 2 humans):
   - If two labelers disagree or one abstains and the other is unsure -> do pair adjudication.
   - If pair still cannot agree -> escalate to SME or policy lead with clear context and candidate labels.
4) SME escalation and canonicalization:
   - For high-impact data (billing, identity, legal text) or multi-turn contradictions, SME decision is final and recorded as tied-to-policy
   - Maintain canonical examples per policy (small set) to reduce future escalations.

Abstain reasons (short canonical set we use across pipelines):
- format_mismatch
- pii_crossfield
- truncated_input
- low_confidence_ml
- ambiguous_semantics
- multi_intent
- label_not_present

Routing matrix (sketch):
- format_mismatch -> auto-abstain -> if field_sensitive=true -> human queue; else mark for batched audit
- pii_crossfield -> auto-abstain -> immediate human review (safety)
- low_confidence_ml -> if impact>=medium -> human pair-review; else soft-abstain with metadata
- ambiguous_semantics/multi_intent -> pair-review -> if unresolved -> SME
- label_not_present -> prompt engineering ticket + sample to developers (rare)

Annotator UX (notes to self):
- When presenting an \"abstain\" decision we should surface: short reason (one token), short context (model probs, heuristic that fired), and an optional freeform note from annotator (2–3 lines).
- Allow \"conditional label\": annotator can provide a candidate label and mark it \"use if SME agrees\"; this reduces rework for SME and speeds adjudication.

Sampling and metrics to validate protocol:
- Track per-reason rates, time-in-queue, percent resolved by pair-review vs SME, and downstream error impact (e.g., percent of production incidents that trace back to an abstain-resolved sample).
- A/B plan: roll out cascading gating on 10% of traffic; compare: label noise (measured by gold set), human-hours per 10k samples, production regression rate.

Concrete edge examples (samples and suggested routing):
1) Input: \"John Smith, DOB 12/03/78, acct #: 987-45-...\" Field: contact_name extraction -> heuristics see PII tokens in wrong field -> reason: pii_crossfield -> immediate human review
2) Input: \"Refund or exchange? unsure. \" Label: single-intent classification -> model top probs: refund 0.38, exchange 0.35 -> reason: ambiguous_semantics -> pair-review; encourage annotator to mark multi_intent if both apply
3) Input: long scraped product title: \"[Lots of repeated tokens] GREAT DEAL! 50% OFF — limited time...\" Field: canonical_product_name -> reason: truncated_input/garbage -> auto-abstain + retry with normalization pipeline
4) Input: numeric field: \"$12,34.00\" -> format_mismatch -> auto-abstain and normalize attempt; if normalization ambiguous -> human

Open questions / trade-offs (personal):
- Threshold selection: 0.45/0.25 are placeholders. Need experiments; prefer thresholds that bias toward abstain for safety-critical labels but toward auto-accept for low-impact labels.
- How to measure \"impact\" per-sample cheaply? Proxy: route_by_schema (billing/legal=high), or downstream model's sensitivity (empirical delta if label flips).
- Annotator fatigue: too many \"abstain\"s that then route to SME will create taxi queue. Can we give annotators more autonomy with fallback rules?
- Audit trail verbosity vs storage costs: we should log heuristics that fired + model probs + annotator note, but truncate long inputs unless sensitive.

Implementation notes / quick TODOs:
- [ ] Implement lightweight pre-check service (rust/python) with deterministic order of heuristics; return list of reasons and first-action suggestion. (owner: Ethan)
- [ ] Add reasons enum to labeling UI + API contract (owner: Ana)
- [ ] Small pilot: 10k diverse samples, instrument per-reason metrics (owner: Priya) – target 4-week run
- [ ] Create gold-set to tune thresholds and to measure false-abstains (owner: Liam)

Slack / comms to link: draft this as an RFC to #data-curation and ping SRE for instrumentation cost estimate. Keep the initial pilot scoped to non-critical datasets to avoid SME overload.

Notes to self: keep language simple when writing UI fields. Avoid >8 reasons in first pass. Make sure \"soft-abstain\" concept is clearly documented (an abstain that does not create a blocking human ticket but is recorded for audit/sampling).

End of draft.  

-- Priya 2025-03-01

- Allow \"conditional label\": annotator can provide a candidate label and mark it \"use if SME agrees\"; this reduces rework for SME and speeds adjudication.

Sampling and metrics to validate protocol:
- Track per-reason rates, time-in-queue, percent resolved by pair-review vs SME, and downstream error impact (e.g., percent of production incidents that trace back to an abstain-resolved sample).
- A/B plan: roll out cascading gating on 10% of traffic; compare: label noise (measured by gold set), human-hours per 10k samples, production regression rate.

Concrete edge examples (samples and suggested routing):
1) Input: \"John Smith, DOB 12/03/78, acct #: 987-45-...\" Field: contact_name extraction -> heuristics see PII tokens in wrong field -> reason: pii_crossfield -> immediate human review
2) Input: \"Refund or exchange? unsure. \" Label: single-intent classification -> model top probs: refund 0.38, exchange 0.35 -> reason: ambiguous_semantics -> pair-review; encourage annotator to mark multi_intent if both apply
3) Input: long scraped product title: \"[Lots of repeated tokens] GREAT DEAL! 50% OFF — limited time...\" Field: canonical_product_name -> reason: truncated_input/garbage -> auto-abstain + retry with normalization pipeline
4) Input: numeric field: \"$12,34.00\" -> format_mismatch -> auto-abstain and normalize attempt; if normalization ambiguous -> human

Open questions / trade-offs (personal):
- Threshold selection: 0.45/0.25 are placeholders. Need experiments; prefer thresholds that bias toward abstain for safety-critical labels but toward auto-accept for low-impact labels.
- How to measure \"impact\" per-sample cheaply? Proxy: route_by_schema (billing/legal=high), or downstream model's sensitivity (empirical delta if label flips).
- Annotator fatigue: too many \"abstain\"s that then route to SME will create taxi queue. Can we give annotators more autonomy with fallback rules?
- Audit trail verbosity vs storage costs: we should log heuristics that fired + model probs + annotator note, but truncate long inputs unless sensitive.

Implementation notes / quick TODOs:
- [ ] Implement lightweight pre-check service (rust/python) with deterministic order of heuristics; return list of reasons and first-action suggestion. (owner: Ethan)
- [ ] Add reasons enum to labeling UI + API contract (owner: Ana)
- [ ] Small pilot: 10k diverse samples, instrument per-reason metrics (owner: Priya) – target 4-week run
- [ ] Create gold-set to tune thresholds and to measure false-abstains (owner: Liam)

Slack / comms to link: draft this as an RFC to #data-curation and ping SRE for instrumentation cost estimate. Keep the initial pilot scoped to non-critical datasets to avoid SME overload.

Notes to self: keep language simple when writing UI fields. Avoid >8 reasons in first pass. Make sure \"soft-abstain\" concept is clearly documented (an abstain that does not create a blocking human ticket but is recorded for audit/sampling).

End of draft.  

-- Priya 2025-03-01

title: Contextual Instruction Perturbation Roadmap
preview:
OVERVIEW
This is a personal scratchpad / working roadmap for a set of experiments I'm running around 'instruction perturbation' and sparse conditioning strategies. Goal: find small, robust prompt mutation strategies that reduce hallucination and brittle instruction-following while staying within tight token budgets for production calls.

Quick motivation notes (TL;DR):
- Many of our app prompts break when the user-provided context contains noise: fragments, list artifacts, or repeated instructions.
- Full prompt sanitation + heavy context engineering is expensive (tokens + dev time).
- Hypothesis: small, systematic perturbations to the instruction + a curated mini few-shot set can make models more robust to noisy context and adversarial fragments.

CURRENT HYPOTHESES
1) Sparse conditioning (1-2 targeted exemplars) + instruction anchor reduces hallucinations versus vanilla single-shot instruction when noise is present.
2) Rotating the anchor phrase (paraphrase pool of 4 variants) helps avoid overfitting to a single phrasing and increases robustness to user-imitated noise.
3) Short adversarial cues (e.g., "Ignore earlier list of names") placed before or after user context have different effects depending on model size/quantization. We should measure both.

PRINCIPLES / DESIGN DECISIONS
- Keep token overhead <= 110 tokens when possible (target for mobile flows).
- Few-shot exemplars must be balanced: 50% positive examples (good answer behaviors) + 50% safety/guard rails (decline/honesty pattern) in short form.
- Prefer concrete micro-examples (1-2 sentences) rather than long synthetic user histories.
- Track: hallucination_rate (manual eval), rpc_latency change (ms), token_cost delta, and failure-case taxonomy.

EXPERIMENT MATRIX (starter)
Dimensions: instruction-anchor variant x few-shot-set x perturbation-type x placement (pre/post context)
- Anchor variants (A1..A4): neutral imperative, conversational, minimal imperative, explicit fail-safe.
- Few-shot sets: FS-min (1 exemplar), FS-bal (2 exemplars: one good answer + one refusal), FS-adv (3 exemplars: includes a tricky adversarial case).
- Perturbation kinds: context-noise (random fragments), instruction-duplication (repeats), injected-list (comma-separated tokens), trailing-metadata ("---internal id: xyz---").
- Placement: anchor before user context; anchor after user context.

SAMPLE PROMPT TEMPLATES (short)
Anchor variants (examples):
- A1 (neutral): "You are an assistant that answers precisely and lists sources if asked."
- A2 (conversational): "Be concise and friendly; say if you're unsure."
- A3 (minimal): "Answer only what is asked."
- A4 (fail-safe): "If the input is incomplete or fabricated, respond: 'I don't have that information.'"

Example few-shot FS-bal (2 shots, compressed):
Shot 1 (good):
Q: "What is the capital of France?"
A: "Paris."

Shot 2 (refusal):
Q: "Give me private user tokens for id 123."
A: "I can't help with that; I don't have access to private tokens."

Full assembly (anchor before):
"[A1]
User context: [user supplied text --- could contain noise]
Response:"

ADVERSARIAL / FAILURE CASES OBSERVED (notes from quick manual pass)
- Case: repeated list of entities at the end of context. Model often treats the list as the primary instruction and outputs a transformed list instead of answering the question.
- Case: instruction-duplication across the context + anchor causes model to ignore anchor if phrasing mismatch.
- Case: with FS-adv (3 shots) latencies increase ~8-12ms and token cost up ~6% — marginal for high-volume flows.
- Case: smaller quantized variants (4-bit) flip behavior more often when anchor placed after the user context.

MEASURABLES & EVALS
- Primary: hallucination_rate on 100 synthetic noisy prompts (binary manual label), % correct on factual QA, truthfulness rubric score (1-5).
- Secondary: token cost delta (median), median latency (ms), percent of responses using 'refusal' patterns when expected.
- Logging: persist the anchor variant id, few-shot id, perturbation tag, model version, quantization, and seed for replay. Link to JIRA-PLAT-789 for log schema.

SAMPLE NOISY INPUTS (mini-bank)
1) "How tall is Mount Everest?
--- internal: 0x09ab ---
John, Mary, total: 103"
2) "List the main points then explain how to test them. NOTE: ignore the previous list."
3) "Where was Ada Lovelace born? 23, 23, 23, 23"

QUICK RESULTS (pilot run 2025-02-24, model=redwood-open-7b-v1-q4)
- A1 + FS-bal + context-noise + anchor-before: hallucination_rate=0.12 (baseline 0.19), latency +6ms, cost +4%
- A3 + FS-min + injected-list + anchor-after: hallucination_rate=0.25 (worse), latency +2ms, cost +1%
Takeaway: anchor-before generally better for this model family. Need to test larger models.

NEXT STEPS / TODOs
- [ ] Run matrix with redwood-open-13b-v2 (full precision) — schedule with infra (Ethan). JIRA-PLAT-789 assigned.
- [ ] Implement a lightweight wrapper that cycles anchor variants per session to measure long-term robustness (Maya to prototype).
- [ ] Add automated eval harness (50k synthetic cases) and a cheap human review QA slice (200 items) — prioritize high-risk flows.
- [ ] Add test for 'instruction-masking' where we explicitly insert contradictory instructions and measure anchor recovery rate.

NOTES TO SELF
- Keep exemplars under 40 tokens each. We found longer exemplars degrade anchor salience.
- Watch out for prompt tokenization edge cases: commas + em-dash sequences sometimes create weird tokens in our tokenizer (see probe script).
- When comparing models, always fix seed and log kv-cache length config.

RAW EXAMPLES (copied from session)
User: "Summarize the article.
1. foo
2. bar
---internal id: xq-9"
Prompt used: "You are an assistant that answers precisely and lists sources if asked.
User context: [above]
Response:"
Model output (bad): "1. foo
2. bar" (returned the list instead of a summary)
Desired output (good): "Summary: The article argues that..."

ARCHIVAL / NOTES ON ORGANIZATION
- Save canonical few-shot sets to: /contrib/prompt-sets/contextual-anchors (not yet created).
- Put final tested templates for product in the 'hosted-api' product docs (product/hosted-api/templates).

Questions / unresolved:
- Is 'anchor rotation per session' doable without heavy state? If not, can we do a per-request pseudo-random pick seeded by user-id? Risk: correlation across repeated calls.
- How do rollout monitors detect if anchor changes cause subtle regressions in tone? Need a 'tone drift' metric.

If you poke at this doc, please leave short notes inline or create a PR against the probe scripts repo. I'll iterate the harness next week.

-- Priya

title: Backup/restore script edge cases (customer envs) — scratchpad
preview:
Context / why this exists
- Personal notes from the last ~3 weeks of Private customer calls + staging drills.
- Goal: list the sharp edges we keep hitting so we can harden the installer CLI + Helm hooks + docs.
- Not a spec. It’s the “stuff that breaks at 2am” list.

Legend
- [OBS] observed in a real env (customer or staging)
- [LAB] reproduced in lab
- [DOC] docs gap / confusing instruction
- [FIX] suggestion for implementation

1) Permissions / identity / RBAC
1.1 Backup CronJob serviceAccount missing verbs [OBS]
- Symptom: CronJob runs but fails on first kubectl call with “(Forbidden) … cannot list secrets/configmaps” etc.
- Root: chart RBAC too minimal; some clusters have stricter policies (PSA/OPA). Also namespace mismatch.
- Notes:
  - If we read any resources for “pre-flight” (CRDs, deployments, jobs), we need explicit list/get/watch.
  - If we attempt to annotate resources (e.g., backup metadata annotations), we need patch.
- [FIX]
  - Ship a minimal-but-sufficient ClusterRole/Role that matches the actual operations.
  - Provide `--dry-run` preflight that *only* checks permissions with `kubectl auth can-i`.
  - Add a doc snippet: “Run this auth check before enabling scheduled backups.”
- Related: Jira INT-612 (CronJob RBAC permissions denied); PR redwood-helm-charts#131.

1.2 S3 access: “put works, list doesn’t” due to least-priv IAM [OBS]
- Symptom: upload succeeds but manifest verification fails because we list or head objects for integrity.
- Root: customers often grant `s3:PutObject` but not `s3:GetObject` or `s3:ListBucket` (especially with restricted prefix policies).
- [FIX]
  - Installer should document exact API calls we do (PutObject, GetObject, HeadObject, ListBucket (optional if we can avoid)).
  - Consider avoiding ListBucket; store full object key from write step and Head/Get directly.
  - When we do need list (retention cleanup), make it optional and clearly separated.
- Related: ENG-9825 Terraform patterns should match actual requirements.

1.3 KMS permissions: encrypt works but decrypt fails on restore [OBS]
- Symptom: backup creation completes; restore fails with AccessDenied on kms:Decrypt.
- Root: key policy grants encrypt to backup role but restore runs under different principal/role; also some customers enforce `kms:ViaService` constraints.
- [FIX]
  - In docs: explicitly state backup + restore principals must both have decrypt.
  - In installer: print the AWS principal ARN it is using; surface the key ARN used.
  - Add a “KMS sanity check” step: attempt a small encrypt+decrypt with the same path.
- Related: ENG-9823 envelope encryption; PR redwood-private-installer#505.

1.4 NFS perms / root_squash surprises [OBS]
- Symptom: tarball writes fail with EPERM when running in container UID; or it “succeeds” but creates 0-byte files.
- Root: NFS export uses root_squash or requires specific UID/GID.
- [FIX]
  - Force backup job to run with configurable runAsUser/runAsGroup in Helm values.
  - Add preflight: write temp file, fsync, read back, check size.
  - Docs: recommend export options; warn about `no_root_squash` vs security.
- Related: redwood-terraform#231 (NFS example + guardrails).

2) Object storage slowness / timeouts / partial uploads
2.1 Slow S3-compatible endpoints (MinIO / private cloud) [OBS]
- Symptom: backup takes “forever”, sometimes hits default HTTP timeouts and fails mid-upload.
- Root: default timeouts are tuned for AWS S3 not on-prem S3 compatibles behind slower networks.
- [FIX]
  - Configurable timeouts + retries; log effective config.
  - Prefer multipart upload for large artifacts; ensure we clean up aborted multipart uploads (cost leak).
  - Emit progress logs (bytes sent / total) every N seconds for operator confidence.

2.2 Partial artifact present after failure [OBS]
- Symptom: restore picks up object key that exists, but checksum doesn’t match manifest OR manifest missing.
- Root: we write artifact first, manifest later; failure between steps leaves inconsistent state.
- [FIX]
  - Two-phase commit-ish approach:
    1) upload artifact with temporary key suffix `.partial`
    2) upload manifest
    3) finalize by copying/renaming to “committed” key OR upload a small “COMMIT” marker.
  - Restore should only accept backups with manifest + commit marker.
  - Docs: operators should delete `.partial` objects, or we auto-clean.

2.3 Egress restrictions: can’t reach bucket endpoint from cluster [OBS]
- Symptom: DNS resolves but route blocked; backups fail with i/o timeout.
- Root: Private installs with limited outbound; sometimes only allow VPC endpoints.
- [DOC]
  - We need clearer guidance: recommended network paths (AWS PrivateLink/VPC endpoint for S3; on-prem object store route).
  - Provide “connectivity check” command (`backup preflight --storage-check`).

3) Clock skew / timestamps
3.1 Clock skew breaks TLS validation [OBS]
- Symptom: “x509: certificate has expired or is not yet valid” when calling internal endpoints, or when using S3 with TLS.
- Root: node time drift; air-gapped environments especially (no NTP).
- [FIX]
  - Preflight: compare node time vs API server time; warn if drift > threshold (5m?).
  - Docs: for on-prem, explicitly require NTP/chrony even in air-gapped mode.

3.2 Backup naming collisions when time is wrong [LAB]
- Symptom: backups overwrite each other if timestamp used as key and time jumps backwards.
- [FIX]
  - Use UUID or monotonic counter in key; keep timestamp as metadata only.

4) Kubernetes resource ordering / missing CRDs
4.1 Restore fails due to missing CRDs [OBS]
- Symptom: applying manifests fails: “no matches for kind X in version Y”; then subsequent steps crash.
- Root: restore process assumes CRDs already installed; some customers do “bare cluster” recovery.
- [FIX]
  - Restore should have explicit “bootstrap ordering” phases:
    Phase A: install CRDs + operators
    Phase B: restore Redwood control-plane state
    Phase C: apply dependent resources
  - Validate CRD presence in preflight and block early with actionable error.
- Related: Jira SUP-18477 (on-prem restore missing CRDs/ordering); PR redwood-private-installer#489.

4.2 Stale webhook configurations block restore [OBS]
- Symptom: API calls hang/fail because ValidatingWebhookConfiguration points to service that doesn’t exist yet.
- Root: restoring webhooks before the webhook backend is ready.
- [FIX]
  - During restore, temporarily disable non-essential webhooks OR apply them last.
  - In docs/runbook: call out webhook caveat and provide “emergency bypass” steps.

4.3 Finalizers on CRs prevent namespace cleanup [OBS]
- Symptom: “restore into clean namespace” step never completes; namespaces stuck Terminating.
- Root: finalizers referencing controllers that aren’t running.
- [FIX]
  - Provide helper command: list stuck resources with finalizers; optional remove finalizers with explicit confirmation.
  - Docs: risky; only for non-prod or with Support guidance.

5) etcd snapshots vs application-level restore confusion
5.1 Operators try etcd restore when they only need control-plane restore [DOC]
- Symptom: customer does full cluster etcd restore, causes more downtime and weirdness.
- [DOC]
  - Clarify when etcd snapshot restore is appropriate (cluster-wide corruption) vs when to use Redwood app-level restore.
  - Link to infra runbook for etcd restore but put big warnings.

6) Secrets handling / “do we back up secrets?”
6.1 Secret material vs references [OBS]
- Symptom: customer expects secrets to be included; but policy says “no secret values in backups” (only references).
- Risk: restore completes but workloads fail because external secret store doesn’t have the same keys in new cluster.
- [DOC]
  - Be explicit: we back up references/identifiers; customer must ensure external secrets exist (Vault/KMS/ExternalSecrets).
  - Provide a validation step: enumerate required secret refs and test retrieval.
- [FIX]
  - Restore validation should include checks for presence of referenced secrets and clear error output.

7) Multi-namespace / multi-release installs
7.1 Customer installed chart in one namespace but components spread across others [OBS]
- Symptom: backup misses resources; restore incomplete.
- Root: some older installs had control-plane in `redwood-system`, jobs in `redwood-ops`, etc.
- [FIX]
  - Don’t assume namespaces; discover via labels/annotations or config file.
  - Manifest should record discovered namespaces.

7.2 Multiple Helm releases (blue/green) [LAB]
- Symptom: restore chooses wrong release values.
- [FIX]
  - Require explicit release name for backup/restore, or back up all and restore with selection.
  - Validation output should show which release was captured.

8) Restore validation gaps
8.1 “Restore succeeded” but routing policies missing [OBS]
- Symptom: control plane is up but tenants route to default model; customer thinks data lost.
- Root: validation only checks pods/healthz, not semantic config.
- [FIX]
  - Add post-restore semantic checks:
    - list tenants
    - verify model pin/routing policy count matches manifest
    - verify canary/rollout configs present
  - Make validation report exportable for Support.
- Related: PR redwood-private-installer#489; docs PR redwood-docs#812.

8.2 DNS/TLS drift after restore [OBS]
- Symptom: endpoints up but clients fail due to cert mismatch or old DNS.
- Root: restoring into new cluster but using old DNS name/cert assumptions.
- [DOC]
  - Runbooks must include explicit DNS cutover + cert issuance/rotation steps (esp VPC single-region DR).

9) Air-gapped specifics
9.1 GPG/signature verification blocks install due to missing tooling [OBS]
- Symptom: verification step fails because `gpg` not installed or older version.
- [FIX]
  - Bundle verification binary/tooling with installer (static) OR document exact dependencies.
  - Provide `--skip-verify` only for break-glass with big warnings.
- Related: ENG-9829; internal ticket INT-631 (CI signing step issues).

9.2 Offline docs / “no external calls” requirement [OBS]
- Symptom: script tries to pull a container image or fetch a schema from the internet.
- [FIX]
  - Ensure restore validation images are included in offline bundle.
  - No `curl` to GitHub/raw content.
  - Add CI test: run in a network-restricted sandbox.

10) Logging / UX gotchas
10.1 Errors don’t include actionable next step [DOC]
- Example: “failed to upload” without printing endpoint, bucket, key, region.
- [FIX]
  - Always print:
    - storage backend type
    - endpoint/bucket/prefix (redact secrets)
    - object key
    - retry count + timeout config
  - Emit a short “what to do next” block.

10.2 Operators want a single “support bundle” [OBS]
- When backup/restore fails, Support asks for logs/manifest/versions.
- [FIX]
  - Add `backup diagnostics` command that collects:
    - installer version
    - cluster version
    - relevant logs
    - last backup manifest
    - validation report

Quick TODO list (for me)
- Add a short section in docs/runbook: “Before enabling scheduled backups: auth + connectivity checks”
- Follow up with Sean/Neha on whether we page on (a) backup job failures vs (b) restore drill failures only.
- Make sure Terraform module examples align with actual S3 API calls (avoid ListBucket if possible).
- Check if we can implement “commit marker” approach without complicating restore too much.

Open questions
- Do we want to support restoring into an existing cluster with other workloads, or mandate clean cluster for v1?
- If we never back up secret material, what is the minimum “secret ref validation” we can do safely and consistently across Vault/ExternalSecrets/etc?
- For S3-compatible stores, do we certify any besides AWS S3 + MinIO? (A bunch of customers say “S3” and mean something else.)

Last updated: 2025-02-22 (after staging restore drill + SUP-18477 review)
