1.2 S3 access: “put works, list doesn’t” due to least-priv IAM [OBS]
- Symptom: upload succeeds but manifest verification fails because we list or head objects for integrity.
- Root: customers often grant `s3:PutObject` but not `s3:GetObject` or `s3:ListBucket` (especially with restricted prefix policies).
- [FIX]
  - Installer should document exact API calls we do (PutObject, GetObject, HeadObject, ListBucket (optional if we can avoid)).
  - Consider avoiding ListBucket; store full object key from write step and Head/Get directly.
  - When we do need list (retention cleanup), make it optional and clearly separated.
- Related: ENG-9825 Terraform patterns should match actual requirements.

1.3 KMS permissions: encrypt works but decrypt fails on restore [OBS]
- Symptom: backup creation completes; restore fails with AccessDenied on kms:Decrypt.
- Root: key policy grants encrypt to backup role but restore runs under different principal/role; also some customers enforce `kms:ViaService` constraints.
- [FIX]
  - In docs: explicitly state backup + restore principals must both have decrypt.
  - In installer: print the AWS principal ARN it is using; surface the key ARN used.
  - Add a “KMS sanity check” step: attempt a small encrypt+decrypt with the same path.
- Related: ENG-9823 envelope encryption; PR redwood-private-installer#505.

1.4 NFS perms / root_squash surprises [OBS]
- Symptom: tarball writes fail with EPERM when running in container UID; or it “succeeds” but creates 0-byte files.
- Root: NFS export uses root_squash or requires specific UID/GID.
- [FIX]
  - Force backup job to run with configurable runAsUser/runAsGroup in Helm values.
  - Add preflight: write temp file, fsync, read back, check size.
  - Docs: recommend export options; warn about `no_root_squash` vs security.
- Related: redwood-terraform#231 (NFS example + guardrails).

2) Object storage slowness / timeouts / partial uploads
2.1 Slow S3-compatible endpoints (MinIO / private cloud) [OBS]
- Symptom: backup takes “forever”, sometimes hits default HTTP timeouts and fails mid-upload.
- Root: default timeouts are tuned for AWS S3 not on-prem S3 compatibles behind slower networks.
- [FIX]
  - Configurable timeouts + retries; log effective config.
  - Prefer multipart upload for large artifacts; ensure we clean up aborted multipart uploads (cost leak).
  - Emit progress logs (bytes sent / total) every N seconds for operator confidence.

2.2 Partial artifact present after failure [OBS]
- Symptom: restore picks up object key that exists, but checksum doesn’t match manifest OR manifest missing.
- Root: we write artifact first, manifest later; failure between steps leaves inconsistent state.
- [FIX]
  - Two-phase commit-ish approach:
    1) upload artifact with temporary key suffix `.partial`
    2) upload manifest
    3) finalize by copying/renaming to “committed” key OR upload a small “COMMIT” marker.
  - Restore should only accept backups with manifest + commit marker.
  - Docs: operators should delete `.partial` objects, or we auto-clean.

2.3 Egress restrictions: can’t reach bucket endpoint from cluster [OBS]
- Symptom: DNS resolves but route blocked; backups fail with i/o timeout.
- Root: Private installs with limited outbound; sometimes only allow VPC endpoints.
- [DOC]
  - We need clearer guidance: recommended network paths (AWS PrivateLink/VPC endpoint for S3; on-prem object store route).
  - Provide “connectivity check” command (`backup preflight --storage-check`).

3) Clock skew / timestamps
3.1 Clock skew breaks TLS validation [OBS]
- Symptom: “x509: certificate has expired or is not yet valid” when calling internal endpoints, or when using S3 with TLS.
- Root: node time drift; air-gapped environments especially (no NTP).
- [FIX]
  - Preflight: compare node time vs API server time; warn if drift > threshold (5m?).
  - Docs: for on-prem, explicitly require NTP/chrony even in air-gapped mode.

3.2 Backup naming collisions when time is wrong [LAB]
- Symptom: backups overwrite each other if timestamp used as key and time jumps backwards.
- [FIX]
  - Use UUID or monotonic counter in key; keep timestamp as metadata only.

4) Kubernetes resource ordering / missing CRDs
4.1 Restore fails due to missing CRDs [OBS]
- Symptom: applying manifests fails: “no matches for kind X in version Y”; then subsequent steps crash.
- Root: restore process assumes CRDs already installed; some customers do “bare cluster” recovery.
- [FIX]
  - Restore should have explicit “bootstrap ordering” phases:
    Phase A: install CRDs + operators
    Phase B: restore Redwood control-plane state
    Phase C: apply dependent resources
  - Validate CRD presence in preflight and block early with actionable error.
- Related: Jira SUP-18477 (on-prem restore missing CRDs/ordering); PR redwood-private-installer#489.

4.2 Stale webhook configurations block restore [OBS]
- Symptom: API calls hang/fail because ValidatingWebhookConfiguration points to service that doesn’t exist yet.
- Root: restoring webhooks before the webhook backend is ready.
- [FIX]
  - During restore, temporarily disable non-essential webhooks OR apply them last.
  - In docs/runbook: call out webhook caveat and provide “emergency bypass” steps.

4.3 Finalizers on CRs prevent namespace cleanup [OBS]
- Symptom: “restore into clean namespace” step never completes; namespaces stuck Terminating.
- Root: finalizers referencing controllers that aren’t running.
- [FIX]
  - Provide helper command: list stuck resources with finalizers; optional remove finalizers with explicit confirmation.
  - Docs: risky; only for non-prod or with Support guidance.

5) etcd snapshots vs application-level restore confusion
5.1 Operators try etcd restore when they only need control-plane restore [DOC]
- Symptom: customer does full cluster etcd restore, causes more downtime and weirdness.
- [DOC]
  - Clarify when etcd snapshot restore is appropriate (cluster-wide corruption) vs when to use Redwood app-level restore.
  - Link to infra runbook for etcd restore but put big warnings.

6) Secrets handling / “do we back up secrets?”
6.1 Secret material vs references [OBS]
- Symptom: customer expects secrets to be included; but policy says “no secret values in backups” (only references).
- Risk: restore completes but workloads fail because external secret store doesn’t have the same keys in new cluster.
- [DOC]
  - Be explicit: we back up references/identifiers; customer must ensure external secrets exist (Vault/KMS/ExternalSecrets).
  - Provide a validation step: enumerate required secret refs and test retrieval.
- [FIX]
  - Restore validation should include checks for presence of referenced secrets and clear error output.

7) Multi-namespace / multi-release installs
7.1 Customer installed chart in one namespace but components spread across others [OBS]
- Symptom: backup misses resources; restore incomplete.
- Root: some older installs had control-plane in `redwood-system`, jobs in `redwood-ops`, etc.
- [FIX]
  - Don’t assume namespaces; discover via labels/annotations or config file.
  - Manifest should record discovered namespaces.

7.2 Multiple Helm releases (blue/green) [LAB]
- Symptom: restore chooses wrong release values.
- [FIX]
  - Require explicit release name for backup/restore, or back up all and restore with selection.
  - Validation output should show which release was captured.

8) Restore validation gaps
8.1 “Restore succeeded” but routing policies missing [OBS]
- Symptom: control plane is up but tenants route to default model; customer thinks data lost.
- Root: validation only checks pods/healthz, not semantic config.
- [FIX]
  - Add post-restore semantic checks:
    - list tenants
    - verify model pin/routing policy count matches manifest
    - verify canary/rollout configs present
  - Make validation report exportable for Support.
- Related: PR redwood-private-installer#489; docs PR redwood-docs#812.

8.2 DNS/TLS drift after restore [OBS]
- Symptom: endpoints up but clients fail due to cert mismatch or old DNS.
- Root: restoring into new cluster but using old DNS name/cert assumptions.
- [DOC]
  - Runbooks must include explicit DNS cutover + cert issuance/rotation steps (esp VPC single-region DR).

9) Air-gapped specifics
9.1 GPG/signature verification blocks install due to missing tooling [OBS]
- Symptom: verification step fails because `gpg` not installed or older version.
- [FIX]
  - Bundle verification binary/tooling with installer (static) OR document exact dependencies.
  - Provide `--skip-verify` only for break-glass with big warnings.
- Related: ENG-9829; internal ticket INT-631 (CI signing step issues).

9.2 Offline docs / “no external calls” requirement [OBS]
- Symptom: script tries to pull a container image or fetch a schema from the internet.
- [FIX]
  - Ensure restore validation images are included in offline bundle.
  - No `curl` to GitHub/raw content.
  - Add CI test: run in a network-restricted sandbox.

10) Logging / UX gotchas
10.1 Errors don’t include actionable next step [DOC]
- Example: “failed to upload” without printing endpoint, bucket, key, region.
- [FIX]
  - Always print:
    - storage backend type
    - endpoint/bucket/prefix (redact secrets)
    - object key
    - retry count + timeout config
  - Emit a short “what to do next” block.

10.2 Operators want a single “support bundle” [OBS]
- When backup/restore fails, Support asks for logs/manifest/versions.
- [FIX]
  - Add `backup diagnostics` command that collects:
    - installer version
    - cluster version
    - relevant logs
    - last backup manifest
    - validation report

Quick TODO list (for me)
- Add a short section in docs/runbook: “Before enabling scheduled backups: auth + connectivity checks”
- Follow up with Sean/Neha on whether we page on (a) backup job failures vs (b) restore drill failures only.
- Make sure Terraform module examples align with actual S3 API calls (avoid ListBucket if possible).
- Check if we can implement “commit marker” approach without complicating restore too much.

Open questions
- Do we want to support restoring into an existing cluster with other workloads, or mandate clean cluster for v1?
- If we never back up secret material, what is the minimum “secret ref validation” we can do safely and consistently across Vault/ExternalSecrets/etc?
- For S3-compatible stores, do we certify any besides AWS S3 + MinIO? (A bunch of customers say “S3” and mean something else.)

Last updated: 2025-02-22 (after staging restore drill + SUP-18477 review)

title: Attenuation Policy Language (APL) — proposal
preview:
Purpose
---
Quick, messy proposal for an "Attenuation Policy Language" (APL) to express runtime attenuation rules: reduce, mask, or transform model outputs at the serving layer when outputs hit a safety/PII/contract surface. This is a personal scratchpad — not final.

Motivation
- We need a compact, reviewable way to express output-safety rules that can be evaluated in the runtime before tokens are emitted (or in a streaming-safe fencing point).
- Existing approaches are ad-hoc (per-model hooks, hardcoded regexes, or heavy NLP classifiers). APL aims to: be auditable, versionable, testable, and cheap to evaluate.
- Use cases: redact user PII, enforce contractual phrases, clamp hallucination risk for certain tenant tiers, or apply length/verbosity attenuation in low-cost routes.

Goals / non-goals
- Goals: declarative, composable attenuation primitives; static analysis for rollout; telemetry hooks; simple VM/runtime to evaluate rules with bounded CPU; fallback semantics (noop vs block vs degrade).
- Non-goals: replace full moderation pipeline or attempt to statically guarantee correctness. APL is last-mile attenuation and must integrate with upstream moderation.

Constraints
- Must be evaluable in-stream (token-by-token or chunk boundaries) with small per-request memory and CPU cost.
- Configs must be small (<= 2KB typical) and cached per-route.
- Must have safe failure modes (default: do-nothing or safe-redact depending on policy).

Core concepts (rough)
- "scope": runtime context where a rule applies: model, route, tenant, region, or response-channel (stdout, tools).
- "selector": predicate over metadata + token stream (regex, heuristic classifier tag, structured match).
- "action": attenuate (score down), redact (mask), transform (rewrite), clamp (truncate), rate-limit (max tokens), flag (emit telemetry + allow).
- "mode": streaming | batch. Streaming mode needs incremental semantics.
- "priority": numeric to order rules and resolve conflicts.

Example APL snippets (YAML-like pseudo)
- single redact rule:
  - name: redact-ssn-like
    scope: route:public-chat
    selector: regex: "(\\\\b\\\\d{3}-\\\\d{2}-\\\\d{4}\\\\b)"
    action: redact
    priority: 100
    mode: streaming

- attenuate hallucination risky-signal (classifier tag):
  - name: attenuate-claims
    scope: tenant:low-trust
    selector: classifier:claim-risk > 0.7
    action: attenuate: {factor: 0.4}  # multiply next-token logits by factor at selection points
    priority: 80
    mode: streaming

- transform contractual-language for enterprise customers:
  - name: enforce-no-guarantee
    scope: tenant:enterprise-legal
    selector: contains_phrase: "guarantee"
    action: transform: {rewrite_template: "I cannot guarantee outcomes; typical results include: {{examples}}"}
    priority: 200
    mode: batch

API shapes / runtime hooks (sketch)
- Config ingestion (control plane): POST /apl/configs
  payload: {name, scope, spec (apl yaml/json), version, applied_by, rollout_strategy}
- Runtime evaluation (fast path, inline): Evaluate(context_meta, token_prefix, candidate_next_token) -> Decision {allow|score_adjust|mask|rewrite|flag, rationale_id}
- Telemetry: emit event when action != allow: {rationale_id, rule_name, scope, tenant, latency_ms, token_context_hash}
- Introspection API: GET /apl/configs?scope=route:public-chat -> diff-able configs and active version ids

Runtime semantics notes
- Streaming: rules are evaluated at emit-time using rolling window of last N tokens (configurable, default 64). Selector ops must be incremental (regex with streaming anchors or classifier that can operate on suffix).
- Score-adjust (attenuate) acts as a multiplicative factor on candidate-logit scores for top-K sampling, applied at the ranking step before temperature/top-k selection.
- Redact/mask: when matched, either replace tokens with a configured mask-token or trigger a backoff to a fallback model/variant. Masking must be reversible only in logs (or not reversible depending on retention policy).

Conflict resolution
- Higher priority wins. If same priority: order by specificity (tenant > route > model). If actions are incompatible, provide deterministic merge: e.g., redact overrides transform; attenuate accumulates multiplicatively.

Policy testing/CI
- Unit harness: small linting tool to assert that rules don't conflict, size limit, and have explicit safe-fallback behavior.
- Golden prompt set: per-tenant/route prompt bank to run APL configs against during CI; output diffs captured to detect regressions.

Rollout model
- Config versions with canary buckets: per-route rollout percentages. Must support immediate rollback by toggling version.
- Telemetry threshold alerts: spike in flagged events or increased latency triggers auto-rollback.

Telemetry & observability
- Minimal events for every non-allow decision. Aggregate: per-rule hit rate, per-tenant hit heatmap, tail latency for evaluation.
- Privacy: strip user identifiers in telemetry; sample payloads for debugging with explicit opt-in.

Security / trust boundaries
- APL configs are sensitive (can change output of models) -> only admins and safetymgr role can deploy for broad scopes.
- Evaluate role-based access and sign configs (GPG/KMS) before runtime loads.

Operator UX sketch
- Console view: rule list, sample inputs, hit-rate sparkline, last 24h examples (redacted), rollback button.
- CLI: aplctl apply ./my-policy.yaml --scope=route:public-chat --canary=5%

Open questions / TODOs
- Selector language: how expressive? Do we include JS-like predicates (dangerous) or restrict to declarative ops (regex, token-prefix, classifier-score, structured fields)?
- Streaming regex runtime cost: benchmark PCRE vs custom automata. Need O(N) incremental matcher.
- How to express multi-step transformations (rewrite + attenuate)? Need composability rules.
- Logging/PII tradeoffs: how much context can we emit for debugging? Policy.
- Compatibility with function/tool calling: if a tool output is attenuated, how do we route its suppressed output to a human review queue?

Immediate next steps (for me / small experiment)
- Prototype a tiny evaluator in Rust/WASM with 3 primitive selectors: regex (streaming), classifier score, phrase-contains.
- Add a CLI and a harness that runs a golden prompt set and reports diffs.
- Wire telemetry stub into runtime mock to measure per-eval latency budget (target < 0.5ms median).

Notes (brain dump)
- Keep policy language intentionally small to avoid misconfig explosion. Start with a single-file YAML + limited predicate set.
- Consider exposing a "simulator" endpoint in console that runs candidate policies against sanitized prompt bank to preview effects.
- For attenuate action we need cooperation from sampling layer. Could be an interface like SamplingPolicy.apply_adjustments(ctx, logits) -> logits'.

References / similar ideas
- HTTP middleware-style transformers (envoy filters) for inspiration (policy table + small exec unit).
- Content-moderation classifiers + a short-circuit redact glue we already have.

If you read this: ping me in #eng-runtime (maya.t) with +1s or quick objections. This is intentionally fuzzy.

-- Maya

title: BlueCrest insurtech partner play - roughs
preview:
Quick context:
- BlueCrest = Series B insurtech (specializes in small-commercial property & casualty underwriting). ~220 employees, HQ Chicago.
- CTO: Anika Shah (technical buyer). Head of Innovation: Marcus Li (product / pilot sponsor). Procurement: likely later.
- Why now: they announced a claims triage automation initiative (press release Nov 2024) and are hiring ML infra/ML engineering roles. Good timing for pilot.

What we think they care about (hypothesis):
- Cost predictability for production inference (claims volume spikes during storms)
- Data isolation / compliance (PII in claims text, PHI in occasional medical invoices)
- Low-lift integration for product teams to show ROI quickly (customer-facing, underwriters + claims adjusters)
- Telemetry: need traces, per-route latency/cost to justify economics to CFO

Value props to emphasize (tailored):
1) Dedicated capacity + autoscaling bursts: predictable token throughput for daily/seasonal spikes — avoids noisy neighbors during catastrophe events.
2) Private deployment option (VPC) + KMS integration: addresses data residency/compliance ask without forcing full on-prem.
3) Optimize tooling: quantization recommendations and batching rules tuned to their short claim texts -> 2-3x cost reduction vs naive CPU/GPU server.
4) Observability out-of-the-box: token-level cost breakdown, per-route dashboard to show how the pilot moves cost per claim and time per claim.

Potential pilot concept (2–6 weeks):
- Scope: Claims intake -> automated triage + recommended severity + top-3 suggested next actions for adjuster. Keep human-in-loop.
- Metrics: accuracy vs baseline (human triage), median time-saved per claim, cost per triaged claim, failure rate, latency percentile (p50/p95).
- Infra: start on Hosted API for fastest timeline (1–2 weeks), run live A/B with 10% of inbound claims. After initial validation, migrate pilot to Dedicated pool for stable SLOs and cost predictability.
- Data controls: scrub PII before sending to hosted if legal requires; or start with dedicated VPC private from day 1 if they push.

Objections + suggested rebuttals (quick scripts):
- \"We can't send PII to a hosted API\" -> \"Totally understood. We can start with a minimal subset on Hosted using redaction, or run the pilot on a Dedicated / Private deployment. Dedicated gives you reserved capacity and VPC peering so data never leaves your network control plane.\"
- \"We already have a small ML team\" -> \"Great — the pilot is designed to minimize infra lift. We provide reference integration, prompt/eval sets, and a rollout dashboard so your team can measure and iterate rather than build the entire serving layer.\"
- \"How do you handle spikes (storms) without huge cost?\" -> \"Autoscaling + burst pools and smart routing: we can route to lower-cost quantized variants for non-critical paths and keep critical routes on higher perf instances. Optimize will recommend the split based on their traffic.\"
- \"Will it change inference accuracy?\" -> \"We tie quality monitoring to prompt/eval sets and alert on regressions; changes to quantization or model variant are tested against the eval set before rollout.\"

Sample outbound sequence (email + LinkedIn + follow-ups):
- Day 0: Short intro email (subject A or B) + one-line value: \"Reduce average triage time by X mins — pilot idea for BlueCrest claims\". Mention 10-day pilot option and metrics we'll deliver.
- Day 3: LinkedIn connect + brief note referencing recent press (claims automation).
- Day 7: Follow-up — attach 1-pager pilot outline and success metrics.
- Day 14: Call-to-action: 20-min sync to show a demo and a sample triaged claim.
- Day 21: Final brief — offer exec digest + pilot T&Cs.

Subject lines to A/B test:
- \"Pilot: 10-day claims triage test for BlueCrest\"
- \"Reduce claims triage time — quick pilot proposal\"
- \"How BlueCrest can lower per-claim cost during peak\"

Cold email draft (short):
- Hi Marcus,
- Noticed BlueCrest's recent push on claims automation — congrats. We run an inference platform that helps insurers run production LLMs with predictable latency and costs and options for VPC/private deployments.
- We can run a 10–14 day pilot that integrates with one intake stream and shows median time-saved per claim + cost per triage. No permanent infra changes required.
- Would you be open to a 20-min call next week?
- Cheers,
- Priya (Redwood Inference)

Longer email when interest shown (attach pilot one-pager):
- Outline technical approach, data pipeline (redaction -> staging -> calls), metrics, timeline (Week 0 integration, Week 1 baseline + A/B, Week 2 analysis + recommendation), and next steps for Dedicated migration.

Meeting agenda (20 min):
- 0–3 min intros & success criteria
- 3–8 min quick product demo + telemetry examples from similar pilot (anonymized)
- 8–12 min discuss data path and compliance (redaction options, VPC)
- 12–17 min pilot plan + measurement plan
- 17–20 min next steps and decision point

Internal TODOs / tickets:
- Create one-pager pilot doc (owner: Priya) -> JIRA-1428 (linked)
- Ask applied-ml for sample prompt/eval templates for claims triage -> OWNED: Ethan Park (due 2025-02-10)
- Legal: draft data processing addendum template for small pilot (point: limit retention, redaction) -> Legal engage Sofia (due 2025-02-14)
- Solutions Eng: spin up demo dataset and mock integration (demo flow) -> SE ticket (SENG-559)

Notes from discovery research (quick scribbles):
- Tech stack: they use AWS, some GCP for analytics, in-house Postgres + Kafka for ingestion. Good signal for quick VPC peering.
- Recent hires: Senior ML infra hire (LinkedIn shows start Jan 2025) — likely to own rollout if pilot goes well.
- Competitors: ChorusAI (small POC last year, looks flimsy), large cloud vendor managed endpoints (buy-in but cost unpredictability).

Risk flags / open questions:
- How strict is their procurement? May require SOC2 + ISO docs before any POC on Hosted. If procurement is heavy, target a Technical PoC directly with Engineering sponsor and commit to Dedicated trial.
- SLA needs? For a small pilot we can offer runbooks and a 99.9% demo SLA but procurement may want longer terms.

Hypothesis KPI targets to propose in pilot agreement:
- Median triage time reduction >= 30%
- Cost per triaged claim <= $X (need bench from similar deals; ask finance)
- No more than 1% failure rate (system returns \"unknown\" or manual handoff)

Quick ask to team before outbound:
- Ethan: sample eval set + baseline triage time numbers (if you have anonymized public dataset we can use)
- SE: canned demo integrated with a fake claim queue (Kafka -> Redwood -> output) by Feb 14
- Legal: DPA snippet and hosted vs dedicated recommendation for DPA language

Next steps (this doc = living):
- Merge feedback from Ethan and Sofia, finalize one-pager, schedule intro with Marcus/Anika.

Random notes / thinking out loud:
- Could offer a small commercial incentive: free 30-day Dedicated trial for pilot customers who sign a 6–12 month reserved capacity within 60 days. Might accelerate procurement decision.
- Consider putting together an \"insurer triage benchmark\" slide: show sample throughput, p50 latency, cost per 1,000 claims for three model variants (gpt-like small/medium/large) to make the economics tangible.

End of scratchpad.

text:
Quick context:
- BlueCrest = Series B insurtech (specializes in small-commercial property & casualty underwriting). ~220 employees, HQ Chicago.
- CTO: Anika Shah (technical buyer). Head of Innovation: Marcus Li (product / pilot sponsor). Procurement: likely later.
- Why now: they announced a claims triage automation initiative (press release Nov 2024) and are hiring ML infra/ML engineering roles. Good timing for pilot.

What we think they care about (hypothesis):
- Cost predictability for production inference (claims volume spikes during storms)
- Data isolation / compliance (PII in claims text, PHI in occasional medical invoices)
- Low-lift integration for product teams to show ROI quickly (customer-facing, underwriters + claims adjusters)
- Telemetry: need traces, per-route latency/cost to justify economics to CFO

Value props to emphasize (tailored):
1) Dedicated capacity + autoscaling bursts: predictable token throughput for daily/seasonal spikes — avoids noisy neighbors during catastrophe events.
2) Private deployment option (VPC) + KMS integration: addresses data residency/compliance ask without forcing full on-prem.
3) Optimize tooling: quantization recommendations and batching rules tuned to their short claim texts -> 2-3x cost reduction vs naive CPU/GPU server.
4) Observability out-of-the-box: token-level cost breakdown, per-route dashboard to show how the pilot moves cost per claim and time per claim.

Potential pilot concept (2–6 weeks):
- Scope: Claims intake -> automated triage + recommended severity + top-3 suggested next actions for adjuster. Keep human-in-loop.
- Metrics: accuracy vs baseline (human triage), median time-saved per claim, cost per triaged claim, failure rate, latency percentile (p50/p95).
- Infra: start on Hosted API for fastest timeline (1–2 weeks), run live A/B with 10% of inbound claims. After initial validation, migrate pilot to Dedicated pool for stable SLOs and cost predictability.
- Data controls: scrub PII before sending to hosted if legal requires; or start with dedicated VPC private from day 1 if they push.

Objections + suggested rebuttals (quick scripts):
- \"We can't send PII to a hosted API\" -> \"Totally understood. We can start with a minimal subset on Hosted using redaction, or run the pilot on a Dedicated / Private deployment. Dedicated gives you reserved capacity and VPC peering so data never leaves your network control plane.\"
- \"We already have a small ML team\" -> \"Great — the pilot is designed to minimize infra lift. We provide reference integration, prompt/eval sets, and a rollout dashboard so your team can measure and iterate rather than build the entire serving layer.\"
- \"How do you handle spikes (storms) without huge cost?\" -> \"Autoscaling + burst pools and smart routing: we can route to lower-cost quantized variants for non-critical paths and keep critical routes on higher perf instances. Optimize will recommend the split based on their traffic.\"
- \"Will it change inference accuracy?\" -> \"We tie quality monitoring to prompt/eval sets and alert on regressions; changes to quantization or model variant are tested against the eval set before rollout.\"

Sample outbound sequence (email + LinkedIn + follow-ups):
- Day 0: Short intro email (subject A or B) + one-line value: \"Reduce average triage time by X mins — pilot idea for BlueCrest claims\". Mention 10-day pilot option and metrics we'll deliver.
- Day 3: LinkedIn connect + brief note referencing recent press (claims automation).
- Day 7: Follow-up — attach 1-pager pilot outline and success metrics.
- Day 14: Call-to-action: 20-min sync to show a demo and a sample triaged claim.
- Day 21: Final brief — offer exec digest + pilot T&Cs.

Subject lines to A/B test:
- \"Pilot: 10-day claims triage test for BlueCrest\"
- \"Reduce claims triage time — quick pilot proposal\"
- \"How BlueCrest can lower per-claim cost during peak\"

Cold email draft (short):
- Hi Marcus,
- Noticed BlueCrest's recent push on claims automation — congrats. We run an inference platform that helps insurers run production LLMs with predictable latency and costs and options for VPC/private deployments.
- We can run a 10–14 day pilot that integrates with one intake stream and shows median time-saved per claim + cost per triage. No permanent infra changes required.
- Would you be open to a 20-min call next week?
- Cheers,
- Priya (Redwood Inference)

Longer email when interest shown (attach pilot one-pager):
- Outline technical approach, data pipeline (redaction -> staging -> calls), metrics, timeline (Week 0 integration, Week 1 baseline + A/B, Week 2 analysis + recommendation), and next steps for Dedicated migration.

- 0–3 min intros & success criteria
- 3–8 min quick product demo + telemetry examples from similar pilot (anonymized)
- 8–12 min discuss data path and compliance (redaction options, VPC)
- 12–17 min pilot plan + measurement plan
- 17–20 min next steps and decision point

title: Brindle Telehealth — async triage onramp scratchpad
preview:
Purpose: capture raw notes from intake call + initial technical constraints for Brindle Health async triage pilot. Not a polished doc — working capture for SE/CS handoff.

Call summary (2025-02-12, 11:00 PT):
- Attendees: Marcus (Redwood SE), Ava (CS), Dr. Harini Rao (Brindle CTO), Jamal Perez (platform lead), Nina (compliance).
- Brindle runs an asynchronous symptom intake flow: patient submits text + short voice memo, the system extracts structured symptoms, risk signals, and suggested next steps (self-care vs escalate). They want LLM to power summarization + suggested dispositions, integrated into their workflow engine.
- Target scope for PoC: English-first, adult primary care, ~10k interactions/day initially, spike to 40k/day in month 3. Latency target for API call: median < 300ms for inference decision (they accept small async queue for audio transcription step).

Key requirements captured:
- PHI/PII: all data is PHI; must be isolated. Customer requires either Dedicated or Private (VPC) deployment. Not comfortable with multi-tenant hosted unless dedicated capacity with encryption-at-rest and CMEK.
- Auditability: per-request audit log retention 90 days for PoC, links to clinical IDs. Must include prompt + model version + scoring confidence in logs.
- Model determinism: they want stable outputs for clinical flows — will require prompt/version pinning and regression alerts.
- Throughput & batching: typical request size small (avg 120 tokens output), but bursty after clinic hours. Want autoscaling tolerance for 5x baseline traffic.
- Cost sensitivity: mid-market price band; need guidance on batching, KV caching, quantization options for 7B/13B candidate models.
- Security controls: SOC2 baseline + support for customer-managed KMS (CMEK) and log forwarding to Splunk.

Technical constraints / notes:
- Network: Brindle runs in GCP us-central1. Would prefer Redwood Private in their VPC (VPC peering or PrivateLink equivalent). Their infra team prefers service endpoints over VPN.
- Storage: Medical records stored in GCS buckets with CMEK; transcripts currently stored for 30 days. For PoC they want logs forwarded and redacted versions stored 90 days.
- Latency profile: their frontend requires ~300–500ms perceived latency; they are OK if internal inference chain has extra steps (ASR -> summarize -> classify) as long as ASR is async and inference step stays under 300ms median.
- Tokenization quirks: many transcripts include shorthand and punctuation-free notes from older ASR model; we should test tokenizers for common misspellings and medical abbreviations (HTN, SOB, etc.).
- Model safety: need guardrails for hallucinations and generation of medical advice. Plan: deterministic classification + templated action outputs; limited free-text notes with explicit hallucination detection evals.

Known gotchas / risks:
- PHI export: if we use Hosted with dedicated capacity, confirm contract guarantees no model training/retention on customer text. They flagged legal language in SOWs.
- Edge cases with audio: short voice memos (3–7s) sometimes produce terse ASR outputs that confuse classifier; need dataset of short memos for eval.
- Fallbacks: clinical escalation path can't fail open. If inference fails, must default to human triage queue. Need SLA for fallback routing and metrics to surface.
- Token limits: some visit summaries hit ~2k tokens in aggregate (multi-message). Need to limit context window or chain-of-thought extraction to avoid model OOM.

Initial PoC plan (high level):
1) Week 0–1: Data access + security review. Get sample anonymized transcripts (10k rows) and a short event stream for 48 hours to model traffic cadence. Contact: Jamal (Brindle).
2) Week 1–2: Quick eval on 3 model candidates (tiny/med/large quantized flavors). Produce accuracy baseline for classification + hallucination rate on prompt set.
3) Week 2–3: Deploy Private PoC in their us-central1 VPC with small dedicated pool (2 GPUs) + KV caching enabled for common prefixes. Run 1 week of shadow traffic.
4) Week 3–4: Measure latency/cost, iterate on batching, enable prefix caching, and tune prompts. Produce go/no-go recommendation and SOW draft.

Action items (owner — due):
- Jamal (Brindle): provide GCP service account and sample dataset (due 2025-02-18).
- Marcus (Redwood): compile initial model candidate list + cost estimate (due 2025-02-16).
- Ava (Redwood CS): schedule compliance call with legal (Nina + Redwood legal) and send PAGs (due 2025-02-17).
- Sofia (Redwood): create sample prompt suite and basic eval harness in GH repo (link in linked_artifacts) (due 2025-02-19).

Open questions for next meeting:
- Brindle legal: acceptable SLOs for failover to human triage? Need explicit uptime and max delay guarantees.
- Which token masking/redaction rules must be applied before sending to Redwood? Are we allowed to receive raw PHI or only redacted derivatives?
- ASR plan: they currently run vendor ASR (internal pipeline). Will Redwood host ASR (probably no) or accept transcripts? Confirm expected transcript format and labels.

Meeting follow-ups:
- Setup compliance deep-dive (legal + security) next Tue 2025-02-18. Marcus to prep architecture diagram with VPC peering options.
- SE pairing session: shadow traffic run window proposal for 2025-03-03 — Marcus + Jamal.

Notes-to-self / rough ideas:
- Try Redwood Optimize suggestions for batching + quantization to hit their cost envelope; document tradeoffs for 7B q4 vs 13B q8.
- Build a quick hallucination detector using n-gram overlap + entailment check with a smaller classifier as gating.
- Consider route-based fallback: low-confidence -> run cheaper deterministic classifier then escalate to human if still ambiguous.

Raw snippets from call (verbatim):
- "We can't have the system telling patients 'go to ER' unless a clinician signs off." — Dr. Rao
- "Short audio memos are the worst — ASR chops words, then models get confused." — Jamal

Next meeting: 2025-02-18 compliance deep-dive (invite: Marcus, Ava, Jamal, Nina, Redwood legal).

End of scratchpad.

title: Contradictory Utterance Resolution — Labeler Cheat Notes
preview:
Purpose:
Quick personal cheat-sheet for resolving contradictory statements in labeling tasks (dialogue, user metadata, multi-field records). Meant as an aide for fast adjudication and creating examples for the labeling guide. Not canonical — propose changes to the shared guide after validating across samples.

Scope / common contexts seen in our datasets:
- Short multi-turn chats where the user later corrects themself ("No, I meant Tuesday not Wednesday").
- System/user metadata disagreements (profile says "NYC" but user message says "I'm in SF now").
- Multi-field documents where different fields state different entity values (shipping_address vs billing_address).
- Extractive QA pairs where source paragraphs contradict each other.
- OCR/ASR artifacts where recognition errors create contradictions.

Guiding principles (ordered):
1) Preserve original text: the label should reflect what actually appears, not the inferred truth, unless the task asks for canonicalization.
2) Prefer explicit self-corrections: if the speaker issues an explicit correction ("sorry, I meant" / "correction:"), take the corrected value as current.
3) Respect timestamps and chronology: later statements generally supersede earlier ones for user intent, unless older value is explicitly "confirmed" or declared immutable.
4) If contradiction is unresolved, label as "conflict:unresolved" and capture both candidates in auxiliary fields (if the schema supports it).
5) When in doubt, mark low confidence and flag for adjudication.

Quick label rules for common patterns:
A) User self-correction in same session:
- Example: U1: "I'll pick 3pm" / U2: "Actually, make that 4pm" -> label final_time = 4pm, resolution = "self-correction", confidence high.
- If both appear but task asks for "first mentioned time", choose 3pm and tag note="first-mention".

B) Contradictory metadata vs utterance:
- If profile field (older) conflicts with a current explicit utterance, prefer utterance for conversational intent tasks.
- For profile-update tasks, prefer profile unless user explicitly states to update it.

C) Multi-field business data (addresses, phone numbers):
- If billing != shipping, do not infer they are the same — label both.
- If there is a single canonical "contact" field elsewhere, follow canonical mapping rules (see JIRA-QL-89).

D) Asymmetric negation / double negation traps:
- "I don't live in San Francisco anymore" vs "I don't NOT live in San Francisco" (rare) — prefer explicit positive or rely on adjudicator. Tag as "negation-complex" and low confidence.

E) OCR/ASR suspicion:
- If contradiction seems like OCR noise (digit garble, weird punctuation), mark suspect_ocr=true and capture both OCR candidates. Use context (dates, numeric patterns) to pick most plausible but mark as inferred.

Label schema fields we commonly use (informal list):
- value (one-string)
- resolution_type (self-correction | timestamp-supersede | profile-override | unresolved | inferred-ocr | inferred-context)
- confidence (high | medium | low)
- notes (free text)
- alternatives (comma-separated short list if schema supports multi-values)

Examples (real-ish samples I encountered):
1) Chat: "I booked it for 6/22" -> later "actually 6/23" -> value=2024-06-23, resolution=self-correction, confidence=high, notes="later turn explicit"
2) Profile shows email alice@example.com, message says "use alice+promo@example.com" -> value=alice+promo@example.com, resolution=user-override, notes="explicit basket update"
3) Doc A says vendor = Acme Co., Doc B says Acme Corporation (no other info) -> canonicalize to "Acme Corporation" only if we have vendor normalization step; otherwise mark both and tag notes=normalization-needed

Edge heuristics I often default to (to speed labeling):
- If the contradiction is numeric and one candidate matches surrounding patterns (ZIP, phone format, date), choose that one and mark confidence medium.
- If candidates are equally plausible textual variants (abbr vs long form), pick the longer canonical form and add a normalization tag.
- For person names: prefer the full-name form unless downstream task explicitly wants username/handle.

Adjudication signals to surface for reviewers:
- resolution_type=unresolved or confidence=low
- presence of more than 2 conflicting candidates
- contradictions across different modalities (text vs image alt text vs OCR)
- any sign of policy-sensitive content (PII) where automated resolution would be risky

Quick checklist before submitting a label:
- Did I capture the explicit latest user correction? Y/N
- Is there any meta-source (profile, previous ticket) that should override? Y/N (explain)
- Did I mark confidence and resolution_type? Y/N
- If low confidence, add a short justification sentence in notes and flag for adjudication.

Open questions / things to test (for product/docs):
- Should default behavior for multi-turn chats be "last explicit" or "last mention unless superseded by explicit correction"? Need product decision.
- How to model implicit corrections ("No, Tuesday works better" vs "Change to Tuesday"): labeler consistency needed.
- Do we want a standard "alternatives" field in the labeling schema to capture both candidates? (helps downstream models but increases labeler effort)
- Can we add a quick UI affordance to tag "suspected OCR" / "suspected ASR" so these flow to a special queue?

Tasks / TODO (me / team):
- Sample 100 multi-turn contradiction cases and compute inter-annotator disagreement (Ethan, can you run the batch?).
- Draft small section for shared guide (1 page) summarizing these heuristics (Priya to write, due 2024-11-15).
- Create a short micro-quiz for labelers: 10 examples with expected label and rationale.

Random notes to self (messy):
- Keep 'preserve original text' principle visible in UI — prevents over-normalization.
- For billing vs shipping: sometimes business rule says default shipping=billing if missing; capture that rule explicitly in the guide.
- Link examples to JIRA-QL-89 for canonicalization discussion.

If you read this and have suggestions, ping me on #labeling or drop notes in the Confluence page linked above.

End of cheat notes.

- Did I capture the explicit latest user correction? Y/N
- Is there any meta-source (profile, previous ticket) that should override? Y/N (explain)
- Did I mark confidence and resolution_type? Y/N
- If low confidence, add a short justification sentence in notes and flag for adjudication.

Open questions / things to test (for product/docs):
- Should default behavior for multi-turn chats be "last explicit" or "last mention unless superseded by explicit correction"? Need product decision.
- How to model implicit corrections ("No, Tuesday works better" vs "Change to Tuesday"): labeler consistency needed.
- Do we want a standard "alternatives" field in the labeling schema to capture both candidates? (helps downstream models but increases labeler effort)
- Can we add a quick UI affordance to tag "suspected OCR" / "suspected ASR" so these flow to a special queue?

Tasks / TODO (me / team):
- Sample 100 multi-turn contradiction cases and compute inter-annotator disagreement (Ethan, can you run the batch?).
- Draft small section for shared guide (1 page) summarizing these heuristics (Priya to write, due 2024-11-15).
- Create a short micro-quiz for labelers: 10 examples with expected label and rationale.

Random notes to self (messy):
- Keep 'preserve original text' principle visible in UI — prevents over-normalization.
- For billing vs shipping: sometimes business rule says default shipping=billing if missing; capture that rule explicitly in the guide.
- Link examples to JIRA-QL-89 for canonicalization discussion.

If you read this and have suggestions, ping me on #labeling or drop notes in the Confluence page linked above.

End of cheat notes.

title: Auto-batcher heuristics debug
preview:
Scratchpad: auto-batcher behaviour investigation (misc notes + commands run)

Summary:
- Nightly regression: p50 token throughput dropped ~18% for chat endpoint between 2025-02-11 02:00 & 02:30 UTC. No model change. Traffic distribution shifted slightly (more short-turn prompts), but not enough to account for drop. Hypothesis: continuous-batcher heuristic misfired (thresholds or temporal decay) causing smaller batches + KV-cache thrash.

What I saw in metrics (quick):
- baseline (2025-02-11 01:50): p50 tokens/sec ~ 3200, p95 latency 120ms
- regression window (02:05–02:25): p50 tokens/sec ~ 2600, p95 latency 160ms
- runtime CPU on hosts jumped ~12% during window, queue length increased modestly
- observed cache miss rate for prefix cache jumped from 4% -> 21% (sampling from /metrics/kvcache/miss_rate)

Commands I ran (local):
- tail recent logs (pod): kubectl -n serving-runtime logs -f auto-batcher-7c9d5 | sed -n '1,200p'
- grabbed prometheus range: curl -g 'https://prometheus/api/v1/query_range?query=kvcache_miss_rate%7Benv%3D%22prod%22%7D&start=...&end=...&step=60s'
- sampled queue lengths: for pod in $(kubectl -n serving-runtime get po -l app=auto-batcher -o name); do kubectl exec -n serving-runtime $pod -- /bin/sh -c 'curl -s localhost:9000/metrics | grep queue_length'; done

Key log snippets (redacted):
- WARN heuristics.go:142 heuristic decision: candidate-window-empty, falling back to immediate flush
- DEBUG batching.go:78 emit batch(size=2, wait=12ms) 
- ERROR kvcache.go:57 failed fetch prefix key="conv:abc123" err=redis: nil

What I tried (experiments):
1) Force larger batch threshold via local override (dev-config): set AB_MAX_WAIT_MS=60 -> redeployed single replica; p50 recovered to ~3050 but p95 still elevated. Suggests batching wait helped but didn't fully fix.
2) Disabled prefix prefetch (kv-prefetch=false) -> resulted in reduced CPU but higher latency for cold conversations; overall throughput similar to baseline in short run.
3) Run synthetic replay against canary with captured traffic (replay tool): noticed batch sizes drop when sequence length distribution changed (more short queries), and heuristics capped at 4. Replaying with option --force-merge increased avg batch size and improved tokens/sec.

Hypotheses (ordered):
- Primary: Decay constants in autoscaling heuristic cause the batcher to interpret transient load as sustained, reducing aggregation window and producing smaller batches. This interacts badly with a shift to longer tail of short queries.
- Secondary: KV prefix cache TTL/eviction incorrectly set after recent config flip (PR #879). Eviction leads to frequent cache misses, meaning more compute per request and smaller effective batching opportunities (because compute paths block).
- Tertiary: Autoscaler mis-samples metrics (using CPU instead of queue length) during that window due to metric scrape lag, reacting too late and spinning up/down host counts in a way that fragments runtime pools.

Repro steps (short):
1) Start canary deployment with AB_MAX_WAIT_MS=20 (current prod value).
2) Run replay with traffic sampled from 02:00–02:30 on prod (replay-tool --speed 1x --preserve-timings).
3) Monitor /metrics for batch_size, p50 tokens/sec, kvcache_miss_rate.
4) Toggle AB_MAX_WAIT_MS=60 and re-run. Compare.

Temporary scripts I used (local notes):
- quick-sampler.sh: collects 30s of /metrics from each pod and writes to /tmp/sample-$$.txt
- replay-wrapper: wraps saved traces and forces sequence length perturbations to simulate more shorts

Open questions / next steps:
- Need owner confirmation on PR #879 changes to prefix cache TTLs. Marco to confirm.
- Is there any cross-team change to Redis cluster or eviction policy around 02:00 on 2025-02-11? Check infra incident logs.
- Add short-term mitigation: increase AB_MAX_WAIT_MS to 50 for next 6h during peak, monitor. Do a canary rollout.
- Long-term: add more robust heuristics (percentile-aware aggregation) and add circuit that reduces wait if kvcache_miss_rate > 15% to avoid creating small batches when compute dominates.

Notes to self: include sample logs and prometheus snippets under linked_artifacts once collected. Save replay traces to s3://redwood-debug/auto-batcher/2025-02-11/trace.tar.gz

Action items:
- [ ] Ask Marco to validate PR #879 config changes
- [ ] Run controlled canary with AB_MAX_WAIT_MS=50 tomorrow (assign Janelle)
- [ ] Draft rollback plan if increasing AB_MAX_WAIT_MS amplifies tail latency

End of scratchpad.

title: Passage: Transition Crib (on-call start notes)
preview:
Purpose:
This is a compact, practical crib for the start-of-shift transition. Not a polished runbook — quick notes, recent context, and the first-pass mitigation steps I actually use. Keep it short, update live during the takeover.

Start-of-shift checklist (10–15 min):
- Check PagerDuty: acknowledge any unassigned incidents and skim active alerts.
- Slack: open #infra-ops and #prod-incidents. Note pinned messages and any OOO coverage calls.
- Deploy window: verify there are no scheduled rollouts in the next 2 hours (Confluence -> Deploy Calendar).
- Capacity: glance at Console -> Dedicated pools -> GPU util. If >75% sustained, flag for scale.
- Token costs: spot any sudden spikes in per-route token usage in the last 30m.
- Smoke test: run minimal inference path against /health and basic generation sample for main models.

Current active alerts (snapshot at handoff):
- Route timeout spike: prod-route-a — elevated 5xx timeout rate for 18m. Probable cause: noisy long-context requests.
- KV cache eviction errors: sporadic OOMs on node runtime-12 (restarted once last night).
- Embedding queue backlog: transient 200+ queued tasks for embeddings-worker.

Immediate triage steps (order I use):
1) Route timeout spike:
   - Identify offending customer route: rwictl routes tail --route prod-route-a --errors
   - If request type = long-context generation, toggle route policy to fallback-model-shortly (temporary): rwictl routes patch prod-route-a --fallback=gen-lite-4b
   - If surge from single API key, throttle the key in Console and contact CS.
2) KV cache OOMs:
   - Check node memory + /var/log/runtime/kvcache.log for eviction traces.
   - If node flapping: cordon runtime-12 and drain: k8s cordon/drain then restart pod. Notify SRE rotation for possible hardware swap.
3) Embedding backlog:
   - Scale embeddings worker group by +2 if backlog > 100 for 10+ min (autoscale sometimes lags).
   - If sudden client spike, enable temporary batching window 40ms in Optimize -> batching profiles.

Workarounds I keep handy:
- If a model route keeps failing on long contexts: change model flavor to quantized-compat variant with lower mem (gen-lite-q8).
- Quick restart sequence for single misbehaving runtime pod: kubectl rollout restart deployment/runtime --namespace=inference --field-selector metadata.name=<pod> (use only for single pod)
- Quick rollback of a deploy: rwictl deploy rollback --service=<service> --to=previous --reason=\"hotfix-rollback-by-oncall\"

Escalation path (use judiciously):
- Oncall SRE (second): page @Ethan Park (PagerDuty) — escalation if partial degradation >30m or customer SLA risk.
- Infra lead: @Lina Gomez — page for cluster-wide storage, capacity or network issues.
- Product Ops / CS: use when customer-visible failures need communication templates. Template links in the pinned Slack message in #prod-incidents.

Known flakiness / ongoing items that might surface:
- runtime-12 memory leak: still under investigation (ticket JIRA-OPS-987). Node restarts temper it but it returns after ~8–12 hours under heavy KV usage.
- Batching tunables: some traffic saw increased latency with 80ms window; default now 40ms but watch for latency regressions on short-requests.

Open questions to capture for next shift:
- Is the large spike yesterday at 03:45 due to a nightly job or an external customer test? Trace ids: trace-20250210-0345-zz.
- Does the new quantization profile for gen-x-13b change output quality for the top 5 customers? We need a rollout A/B.

Notes-to-self (for handover):
- If you patch route fallback, add a note in the incident thread documenting reason and rollback criteria.
- When draining runtime-12, coordinate with SRE to avoid draining two nodes at once.
- Keep an eye on embedding backlog metric (metric id: emb.queue.length) — alarm threshold currently 120, but false positives observed during batch small-hour spikes.

Post-shift TODO (short list):
- Follow up with Lina about runtime-12 maintenance window and root cause hypothesis.
- Add a quick test case to CI that simulates long-context noisy requests to exercise route throttles.
- Draft short staff note for Deploy Calendar about temporary fallback rules for prod-route-a.

Quick logs grep patterns I use:
- kvcache errors: grep -i kvcache /var/log/runtime/* | tail -n 100
- route timeouts: journalctl -u runtime-router -o cat | grep \"timeout\" | tail -n 200

Final handover line (what I'd say out loud):
\"Engineers — nothing critical right now, running a noisy long-context spike on prod-route-a that I throttled to gen-lite. runtime-12 is unstable; I cordoned it earlier — follow Lina's plan for maintenance. If you see embedding backlog >200 for 15m, scale workers manually. I'm setting my status to away at 18:30; Ethan is second on call. Ping in-thread for any questions.\"

-- end crib --

(If this helps, copy into the real runbook page and then expand the escalation templates)

title: Cloud-init network glitch — node postinstall notes
preview:
TIMELINE
2025-02-12 09:12 PST — node registered in cloud console (gce-ctl) and started cloud-init
2025-02-12 09:18 PST — kubeadm join failed with 'timed out waiting for API server'
2025-02-12 09:20 PST — node appears Ready for ~2m then NotReady; intermittent

OBSERVED SIGNS
- node boots, cloud-init finishes, kubelet systemd unit active but Node status toggles Ready/NotReady.
- cni plugin (calico) pod stuck CrashLoopBackOff on new node.
- network interface eth0 gets 169.254.x.x APIPA address briefly before DHCP assigned correct 10.140.x.x.
- kubelet journal shows: "Failed to get node address: no suitable address found" and "Failed to set node annotations: Get https://10.0.0.1:6443/… dial tcp 10.0.0.1:6443: connect: connection refused"
- cloud-init net config shows race between dhclient and systemd-networkd

COMMANDS I RAN (raw outputs trimmed)
- ssh into VM and checked interfaces
  ip addr show eth0
  # output: inet 169.254.12.3/16 ...
  dmesg | grep -i dhcp
  # output: dhclient[826]: DHCPDISCOVER on eth0 to 255.255.255.255 port 67

- journalctl -u kubelet -b -o cat | tail -n 40
  "E0212 09:18:45.123456    1234 kubelet.go:2187] Failed to get kubeconfig: error creating certificate: signing request failed"
  "W0212 09:19:01.987654    1234 desired_state_of_world_populator.go:xxx] ... Failed to refresh volumes"

- kubeadm join output (on node)
  [preflight] Some preflight checks failed
  [preflight] WARNING: kubelet service is not active
  [discovery] Trying to connect to API Server "10.0.0.1:6443"
  [discovery] Failed to connect: dial tcp 10.0.0.1:6443: connect: connection refused

- kubectl (from control plane) during the window node appeared Ready
  kubectl get nodes -o wide
  NAME        STATUS     ROLES   AGE   VERSION   INTERNAL-IP   EXTERNAL-IP   OS-IMAGE
  node-23     Ready      <none>  3m    v1.28.0   10.140.0.23   ...

HYPOTHESES
1) Transient networking: cloud-init delays DHCP, kubelet starts too early and registers wrong address (169.254) -> apiserver unreachable, node re-registers later causing flapping.
2) Race between cloud-init and systemd-networkd leads to missing /etc/hosts or wrong resolv.conf during kubeadm bootstrap.
3) CNI assumes certain MTU/route that isn't established until postinstall scripts run -> calico pods fail, causing network readiness checks to mark node NotReady.
4) token/cert provisioning timing: kubelet fails to obtain certs when cloud-init restarts network, certificate signing requests get dropped.

QUICK FIXES I TRIED
- systemctl stop kubelet; wait until DHCP assigned correct IP; start kubelet -> node becomes stable (works as workaround)
- added "ExecStartPre" sleep in kubelet systemd override (temporary) to delay start by 30s -> reduced flapping
- bumped DHCP retry in cloud-init to be more aggressive -> partial
- disabled predictable-network-names in metadata -> no change

NEXT DIAGNOSTIC STEPS
- Gather full kubelet journal for entire boot sequence (journalctl -u kubelet -b > /tmp/kubelet-boot.log)
- Add systemd drop-in: /etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/10-wait-network.conf with ConditionPathExists=/run/network-online.target and Wants=network-online.target to force network-online; test reproducible
- Capture cloud-init logs: /var/log/cloud-init.log and /var/log/cloud-init-output.log for user-data ordering
- Recreate node with metadata flag to run cloud-init in debug mode
- Check CNI plugin logs (calico/node) for errors referencing IP autodetection

NOTES FOR A PERMANENT FIX
- We should move kubeadm/kubelet start to require network-online.target; relying on dhclient races is brittle in our image
- Consider using systemd-networkd-wait-online or cloud-init network configuration to block until IP assigned
- Add postinstall hook to verify that the node's primary internal IP is routable to the control-plane before starting kubelet
- Add probe that fails kubeadm join early if IP is link-local

POTENTIAL FOLLOW-UPS
- Add test in image pipeline that simulates DHCP race (boot vm without DHCP for first 10s)
- File an infra ticket: LINK -> https://linear.app/RED/ISSUE-2312

SNIPPETS (for quick paste)
# Wait for network then start kubelet
cat > /etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/50-wait-network.conf <<EOF
[Unit]
Wants=network-online.target
After=network-online.target
EOF
systemctl daemon-reload && systemctl restart kubelet

# Collect logs
journalctl -u kubelet -b --no-pager > /tmp/kubelet-journal.txt
journalctl -u systemd-networkd -b --no-pager > /tmp/networkd-journal.txt

---
Notes to self: test with calico IP autodetection set to cloud provider (gce) vs interface. If switching to cloud-provider mode, ensure metadata server reachable early.

END

- Capture cloud-init logs: /var/log/cloud-init.log and /var/log/cloud-init-output.log for user-data ordering
- Recreate node with metadata flag to run cloud-init in debug mode
- Check CNI plugin logs (calico/node) for errors referencing IP autodetection

NOTES FOR A PERMANENT FIX
- We should move kubeadm/kubelet start to require network-online.target; relying on dhclient races is brittle in our image
- Consider using systemd-networkd-wait-online or cloud-init network configuration to block until IP assigned
- Add postinstall hook to verify that the node's primary internal IP is routable to the control-plane before starting kubelet
- Add probe that fails kubeadm join early if IP is link-local

POTENTIAL FOLLOW-UPS
- Add test in image pipeline that simulates DHCP race (boot vm without DHCP for first 10s)
- File an infra ticket: LINK -> https://linear.app/RED/ISSUE-2312

SNIPPETS (for quick paste)
# Wait for network then start kubelet
cat > /etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/50-wait-network.conf <<EOF
[Unit]
Wants=network-online.target
After=network-online.target
EOF
systemctl daemon-reload && systemctl restart kubelet

# Collect logs
journalctl -u kubelet -b --no-pager > /tmp/kubelet-journal.txt
journalctl -u systemd-networkd -b --no-pager > /tmp/networkd-journal.txt

---
Notes to self: test with calico IP autodetection set to cloud provider (gce) vs interface. If switching to cloud-provider mode, ensure metadata server reachable early.

END

title: Caffeine-powered Bisect Playbook for Quick Regression Triage
preview:
Context:
- Late-stage dev: small perf/regression spike observed in hosted text-generation v2 route (low-prompt-length).
- Customer report + internal smoke tests: occasional incoherent completions (~0.6% -> 1.9% of sample). Need fast triage before escalation.

Objective (5–20 minute prioritization):
- Rapidly determine if regression is model, routing, or pre/post-processing.
- Produce small reproducible sample set + ad-hoc rubric to categorize failure mode.

Quick hypothesis list (ordered cheap->expensive):
1) Prompt tokenization change (tokenizer update or subtle encoding mismatch).
2) Quantization/precision path change (new kernel selection for short sequences).
3) Routing fallback to smaller model variant under burst.
4) Prompt truncation or prefix drop due to cache bug.
5) Data corruption in the request body at edge (rare but check).

Ad-hoc rubric (for quick labeling):
- Category A: Coherence collapse (output loses sentence-level meaning; hallucinations unrelated to input).
- Category B: Truncation / abrupt cutoff (ends mid-sentence, missing closing).
- Category C: Repetition loop (same phrase/token repeats).
- Category D: Safety / unexpected refusal (sudden policy refusal where none expected).
- Category E: Tokenization artifact (weird tokens/escape sequences present).
- Category F: OK (acceptable).

Sampling plan (cheap, reproducible):
- Pull 120 random requests from the last 48h where route == /v2/generate and token_count < 40. Use existing logging-sampler query: samples:recent-short-prompts-48h.
- Augment with 20 customer-reported examples (from ENGINF-4821).
- Run local reproduce harness (use --deterministic-seed) across three model variants: model-v2.3-fp16, model-v2.3-q4, model-v2.2-fp16.

Checklist for each sample (2–3 minute per batch of 10):
- Reproduce output locally from same model binary + same seed. (Y/N)
- If reproduces: inspect tokens at boundaries for truncation or repeated patterns.
- If not reproducing: check routing logs (timestamp alignment, region, fallback flag), edge proxies for body transforms.
- Record rubric label and short note.

Working examples (kept terse):
- prompt: "Summarize the following paragraph: The experiment showed a 2x improvement..."
  - production output: "It shows 2x improvement ..." -> OK
- prompt: "Write a short blurb about ecosystem services in urban planning."
  - production output: "<s> \u0000 \u0000 0xFF" -> Category E (tokenization artifact)
- prompt: "Explain how to reset a router."
  - production output: "I can't help with that." -> Category D (unexpected refusal)
- prompt: "Give 3 bullets about benefit of caching"
  - production output: repeats "Benefit: reduces cost" 6x -> Category C

Preliminary observations after first 60 samples:
- 8 samples labeled Category E (tokenization artifacts). All originate from same customer region (eu-west-1).
- 4 Category C (repetition) and 1 Category B (truncation).
- Reproduced tokenization artifacts locally when swapping to the q4 kernel path; fp16 path didn't show null bytes.

Immediate hypothesis refinement:
- Kernel selector may be misclassifying some short requests to q4 path that has a packaging bug causing stray null bytes in decode buffer for specific hardware. Explains region skew (different GPU SKU in EU pool).

Quick bisect steps to validate (fast):
1) Re-run 20 failing samples forcing model binary + kernel path to fp16 (bypass q4 route) in staging. If artifact disappears -> kernel path bug.
2) Reproduce one artifact with instrumentation to dump pre-decode bytes and tokenizer output. Compare to healthy run.
3) Check recent release notes: PR 2567 touched kernel selection heuristics for seq_len < 48.
4) If confirmed, create hotfix: pin short-requests to fp16 routing until kernel bug fixed.

Notes for communication to SRE/infra:
- Include sample ids, timestamps, region, and exact node ids where artifacts observed.
- Request temporary routing rule: region eu-west-1 short-requests -> force fp16. Include safe rollback.

Edge checks (non-blocking but important):
- Proxy / edge: Are any intermediate proxies altering Content-Encoding or injecting headers? Quick grep of edge logs shows no gzip anomaly.
- Tokenizer version mismatch: check wheel version pinned in image: tokenizer==1.14.2 both sides.

Next actions (today):
- [ ] Run forced-fp16 reproduce on 20 artifacts (Ravi)
- [ ] Add instrumentation dump for pre-decode bytes and tokenizer outputs (me)
- [ ] Draft temporary routing rule + rollback plan (Aisha)
- [ ] Open follow-up bug with kernel vendor team if confirmed (me)

Open questions / things to watch:
- Why do only some short requests route to q4? (load-based heuristic? weight pruning?)
- Are there any recent infra config flips around kernel selection? Look for terraform changes + recent deploys.
- Monitor rate of Category E—if it rises quickly, escalate to SEV.

Random notes to self (messy):
- Idea: add short-request feature flag to toggle kernel path per-customer for targeted mitigation.
- Consider adding lightweight token-level checksum in debug header for quick detection of corrupt payloads.

Bottom line (for initial sync):
- Early signs point to kernel selection -> q4 path causing occasional decode artifacts on a particular hardware pool. Fast mitigation: force fp16 for short prompts in affected region while we root-cause.

Timestamped audit trail: 2025-02-14 10:02Z - first 60-sample pass done.

-- J.B.

title: API key heartbeat / window probe notes
preview:
Context / quick summary:
- Observed recurring 401/429 bursts for a subset of long-lived API keys (mostly internal test clients + some partner integrations). Pattern: relatively low sustained QPS, then periodic clustered 429 spikes every ~60s for a small cohort of keys. Suspect interaction between a "heartbeat" client behavior (periodic keepalive or metrics ping) and sliding window quota enforcement.

Goal of this scratchpad:
- Capture commands I ran and the responses
- Hypotheses for root cause
- Minimal repro plan to validate locally/infra
- Short-term mitigations to reduce customer impact

Raw errors / snippets seen in logs:
- Example 1 (proxy logs)
  2025-02-07T17:12:03Z [proxy] key=acct-test-17 code=429 msg="rate limit exceeded" limit_window=600 window_bucket_start=2025-02-07T17:11:00Z

- Example 2 (app/API response body returned to client)
  {
    "error": "Requests exhausted for this minute",
    "type": "rate_limit_exceeded",
    "retry_after": 2
  }

- Example 3 (401 variant) — intermittent token validation failures during burst windows:
  2025-02-08T09:04:12Z [auth] key=acct-internal-3 result=401 detail="token-expired-or-unrecognized"
  BUT token TTL in KMS shows valid until 2025-02-10 -> so likely a downstream auth cache eviction or sync race instead of real TTL expiry.

Commands I ran (local / from staging)
- curl reproducer (simple 1-rps baseline, then add heartbeat):
  curl -i -H "Authorization: Bearer $API_KEY" https://staging.api.redwood-inference.com/v1/generate

- quick throughput probe script (bash):
  for i in $(seq 1 120); do 
    curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code} %T
" -H "Authorization: Bearer $API_KEY" \ 
      "https://staging.api.redwood-inference.com/v1/embeddings" &
    sleep 0.5
  done

- More targeted: reproduce heartbeat overlay (50ms heartbeat concurrent with 1r/s background)
  # spawn background steady worker
  python3 scripts/steady_client.py --qps 1 --duration 120 --key $API_KEY &
  # in another shell spawn heartbeat pulses every 60s that do small metadata calls
  while true; do curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $API_KEY" \ 
    "https://staging.api.redwood-inference.com/internal/heartbeat" >/dev/null; sleep 60; done

- Observed consequence: when heartbeat coincided with internal quota bucket boundary (see timeline below) -> short burst of 429s for the key until next bucket tick.

Monitoring / introspection queries used
- Query rate-window counters in redis: (pseudo)
  redis-cli HGETRATE window:acct-test-17 2025-02-07T17:11
  returned 98 (requests) when we expected ~20

- DB query to find bucket alignments (Postgres):
  SELECT bucket_start, count(*) FROM request_logs WHERE api_key='acct-test-17' AND bucket_start > now() - interval '10 minutes' GROUP BY bucket_start ORDER BY bucket_start;
  -> shows spikes aligned to :00 of each minute and also consistent +1 offset windows at :59 in some hosts.

Hypotheses (ranked):
1) Sliding-window collision / heartbeat alignment: some clients perform a periodic tiny-payload "heartbeat" exactly on a 60s cadence. If server's rate-windowing aligns to minute boundaries, heartbeat causes short-lived bucket overflow when combined with regular traffic from that key.
2) Inconsistent clock / clock skew between front-proxies and quota store nodes causes requests to be attributed to adjacent buckets -> double counting in two windows for the same logical request.
3) Auth-cache eviction + concurrent revalidation: auth service temporarily returns 401 while revalidating token; client retries immediately -> bumping the rate counters and converting 401s to 429 cascades.
4) Shared key cohorts hitting global per-key-per-region limit because we accidentally folded partner test tokens into same cohort (config bug).

Why hypothesis 1 is attractive:
- Pattern periodicity ~60s, matches many heartbeats we've historically seen (metrics agent, internal probe, third-party SDKs).
- Repro on staging by adding a 60s heartbeat consistently recreated the failure.

Evidence to collect next / experiments to run:
- Instrument one key to log precise timestamp (ms) and window attribution (which bucket). Add verbose logging: "attributed_to_window_start": "2025-02-07T17:11:00.000Z".
- Run staggered-heartbeat test: run N clients with heartbeats jittered uniformly across 60s and compare 429 incidence vs synchronized heartbeat.
- Clock sync test: intentionally change delta on one ingress host by +500ms to see if bucket assignment shifts causing duplicates.
- Replay captured requests into a synthetic quota-proxy (unit test) that uses same window algorithm to verify collision behavior.

Quick local repro plan (minimal):
- Setup: staging quota-proxy + redis (exact config). Use single API key and start two processes:
  - steady_client.py at 1 rps (normal workload)
  - heartbeat script that posts to /internal/heartbeat exactly every 60s (no jitter)
- Expected: after ~2-3 minutes heartbeat aligns with bucket boundary -> short burst 429s
- Variation: add jitter (random 0..10s) to heartbeat -> should smooth/avoid 429s if hypothesis 1 is true

Short-term mitigations (what to try in prod quickly):
- Add server-side jitter to bucket attribution (random offset for each key up to +-500ms) to reduce synchronized collisions (low risk). Roll as an A/B under config flag.
- Return helpful Retry-After headers and soft-429 code path for heartbeat endpoints (so clients back off with jitter instead of tight retries).
- Contact top affected partners and ask them to add jitter to heartbeat; ship SDK patch in 24h to add randomized heartbeat jitter and exponential backoff.
- Quick config change: bump per-key soft-limit by +20% for keys flagged as internal test clients to reduce impact while we root-cause.

Longer-term fixes to pursue:
- Replace minute-aligned sliding window with decaying leaky-bucket / token-bucket implementation per key (more tolerant of synchronized pulses).
- Improve auth cache refresh to avoid 401->retry loops: while token is being validated, return a 202 (or 'auth-in-progress') so client doesn't immediately retry against quota.
- Add telemetry: per-request attributed_window_ms and per-key heartbeat fingerprinting (detect exact heartbeat cadence and auto-suggest jitter).

Notes-to-self / TODOs:
- + Add more logging fields: upstream_host, local_time_ms, window_start_ms.
- + Build a small repro harness in go that can arbitrarily jitter and run at scale (1000 keys) to test herd effects.
- + Run the jittered-heartbeat experiment in staging with real redis config and metric collection.
- + Draft customer message template for partners that are hit. Keep it technical and give mitigation steps (add jitter + backoff).

Timeline snippet from today (for incident ticket):
- 09:00 - alert fired for increased 429 for acct-test cohort
- 09:08 - reproduced in staging with heartbeat overlay (Ethan pair-debugged)
- 10:20 - verified redis counters show multi-window attribution spikes
- 11:05 - attempted local fix (server-side jitter flag) behind feature flag, tested in staging (no regressions yet)

Open questions / unknowns:
- Are there other periodic agents (CDNs, upstream health checks) that could be hitting the same cadence? Need to scan partner integration logs for exact 60s pings.
- How many keys are synthetic/test keys that should be quarantined into a separate lower-priority cohort?
- Is there any interaction with Dedicated instances where customer-level quotas differ? Need to check dedicated quota overlay logic.

References / past tickets to review:
- INFR-0934: previous minute-boundary rate limit investigation (May 2024)
- PR #187 in rate-limiter: initial sliding-window code (has comments about boundary behavior)

If I don't return to this tomorrow: steps to continue for whoever picks up:
- run jittered vs aligned heartbeat A/B test in staging for 30m with real redis store
- collect per-key window attribution logs (sample 1%) and grep for repeated exact-second timestamps
- escalate to infra if we observe cross-host bucket assignment mismatches (clock skew)

-- end of notes --

title: Clearwater insurance — claims routing assistant scratchpad
preview:
Call notes (2025-02-10) — kickoff with Clearwater claims team:
- attendees: Mike Rivers (Clearwater VP Ops), Priya Nair (Claims PM), Sean (infra lead), Ava Chen (Redwood SE), Marcus (deployment engineer)
- ask: lightweight claims-routing assistant that reads claim metadata + attachments, suggests route (fraud, medical, auto, escalation). Must be ~= deterministic with audit trail.
- data constraints: PHI in attachments for medical claims; Clearwater requires no persistent storage of raw attachments in hosted environment. KMS for keys (customer uses AWS KMS), SOC2 + HIPAA reference.
- throughput expectation: peak 400 req/min during morning batch window, typical 80 req/min. Latency SLO: 95th < 400ms for route decision.
- routing logic: initial MVP uses metadata + short extracted text; attachments processed by OCR pipeline outside Redwood. Redwood receives structured extraction JSON and returns route + top-3 rationale snippets.

Technical gaps / gotchas observed:
- attachment extracts vary in length; some extracts up to 8k chars -> token budget concerns. Need truncation strategy and prefix caching for repeated claimant histories.
- PHI policy: Clearwater insists on no KV cache containing raw PHI. We can enable prefix/KV caching but must strip PHI fields. Need a sanitizer step upstream or a transform hook on Redwood private connector.
- Model determinism: they want consistent decisions for same payload. Need to propose model variant + temperature=0 and pinned model version. Also provide eval harness with 200 canonical examples.
- Fallback behavior: if model times out or exceeds SLO, must fallback to a simple rule-based router. Need canary for rollout.
- Audit trail: each decision must store tokens-of-interest (non-PHI) and model version + request id. Storage retention = 7 years per compliance. We must map this to Dedicated/private storage plans.

Redwood-specific implementation ideas:
- Use Redwood Private (VPC) for initial pilot to satisfy PHI/no-hosted-storage constraint. Connectors: S3 presigned + Redwood private ingestion.
- Configure request transform hook (lambda-style) to strip PHI fields and to normalize claimant IDs before caching. Document sample transform pseudocode.
- Batching: suggest micro-batching at 50ms window to combine concurrent small requests during peak to reduce unit cost, but enforce per-request latency SLO.
- Model selection: recommend a 7B open model quantized + Redwood-verified profile for low-latency routing, pinned to exact commit sha. Provide fallback to CPU-hosted rule engine in Dedicated.

Open questions to confirm:
- exact fields considered PHI (they said attachments + ssn + dob; confirm if claim notes free-text considered PHI). Priya to share data spec.
- retention policy clarity: 7 years for audit vs shorter for caching. Do they want caches disabled entirely?
- expected integration pattern for OCR/attachment extraction: will Clearwater push extracts to our ingest endpoint or will we poll S3?

Action items / next steps:
- Priya: share sample redacted extract payload (by 2025-02-13).
- Marcus: prototype transform hook that strips phone/ssn/dob keys and replaces claimant_id with hash; share example by 2025-02-14.
- Ava: draft architecture sketch with Private deployment + KMS flow + audit-store options; include cost/back-of-envelope for Dedicated vs Private by 2025-02-17.
- Lena (Customer Success): schedule follow-up to review eval dataset and run 200 canonical cases (w/labels) — aim for week of 2025-02-24.
- Engineering: confirm whether 95th < 400ms achievable with 7B quantized on their preferred region GPUs (Marcus to loop infra).

Quick prototype notes (things to try):
- try prefix-caching only on claimant-history keys (non-PHI) -> keep cache TTL short (1 hr)
- test truncation + summarization: run a light summarizer in pre-stage to compress extracts > 2k chars into bullet points before sending to model
- implement deterministic decoding: greedy/beam with seed, temperature 0, forced-stop tokens

Risks / mitigation:
- Compliance pushback if any PHI leaves their VPC -> mitig: Private deploy + E2E audit + signed BAAs
- Large extraction -> model cost blowouts -> mitig: summarizer + per-request token budget + reject policy for > 16k tokens (return rule-based route)
- Unexpected spikes -> mitig: burst capacity plan in Dedicated + circuit-breaker to fallback to rules

Notes-to-self:
- reference Redwood docs: "private-deploy-kms-integration" + "batching-best-practices" pages (link later)
- capture sample payloads in a separate sheet for eval harness (JIRA-CLM-198 ticket).

Conversation snippets (for quoting in follow-up):
- Mike R: "we can't risk PHI escaping, even for caching — if you can show an isolated VPC flow we'll be comfortable"
- Priya: "we need high explainability — top-3 reasons with text spans"
- Sean: "our infra prefers S3 push, not polling — want event-driven ingest"

Next meeting: 2025-02-18 09:00 PT (follow-up technical deep dive) — agenda: transform hook demo, sample payload review, eval dataset plan.
