title: Citrine Warehouse inference intake & field digest
preview:
Meeting: Citrine Warehouse kickoff — 2025-02-06 (field site, onsite with ops lead)
Attendees: Pilar Gomez (Citrine IT), Rob Hsu (Ops), Mia Tan (data engineering), Ava Chen (Redwood SE), Diego Morales (Redwood infra)

Quick summary:
- Citrine wants a small-footprint inference layer to run SKU classification + packing-recommendation assistant at 50–200 concurrent device sessions across 3 warehouses. Latency target: 95th <= 150ms for short completions ( < 64 tokens).
- Primary constraint: intermittent connectivity to cloud (satellite fallback); prefer Private deployment (VPC + optional air-gapped batch sync).
- Security: strict PII policy, on-prem logs only, KMS-managed keys required, SOC2 + ISO audit in 6 months.
- Budget: initial pilot target spend cap $12k/month for reserved capacity + ops support.

Raw field notes / gotchas noticed on site:
- Floor wifi has high jitter; we recommended separate management VLAN; ops said VLAN changes require procurement lead (delay ~3 weeks).
- Many barcode scanners push data via local MQTT broker; integration will need a lightweight gateway translating MQTT->HTTP to Redwood API.
- Edge devices are running Jetson Xavier NX (8GB) and a couple older TX2s — memory/quantization tradeoffs matter.
- Team currently using a cached heuristics model on devices; they'll want shadow testing (Redwood routing -> local model) before cutover.

Suggested technical approach (initial pilot):
1) Private deployment, single VPC region adjacent to site, control-plane hosted (Redwood) with on-prem inference worker cluster (k8s) in customer datacenter.
2) Use a trimmed model family: quantized 4-bit LLM variant for packing-recommendation, and a smaller embedding model for SKU similarity. Recommend Q4_0 or GPTQ equivalent; run perf tests to confirm memory fit on Xavier NX.
3) Enable prefix/KV caching for repeated SKU prompts to push token latency down. Cache layer colocated with gateway.
4) Routing policy: local-first (edge workers) -> dedicated private pool -> hosted fallback. Fallback only for non-sensitive telemetry.
5) Autoscaling: keep 2 hot workers per warehouse, burst pool for shipping surge windows (nightly batch windows).

Performance assumptions to validate (action: perf test checklist):
- QPS target: 200 concurrent sessions with avg prompt size ~30 tokens.
- Batch windows: typical inter-arrival times spike at 150ms during packing rush. Need batching threshold tuned to preserve 95th latency.
- Memory: 8GB constraint on Xavier NX; test model loading + KV overhead (we may need to offload KV service to local microserver).

Security / compliance notes:
- KMS integration: customer insists on HSM-backed keys via their Vault (HashiCorp). Confirm Redwood Private supports external KMS or bring-your-own-HSM for model encryption at rest.
- Audit logs: they want per-request audit with request id and prompt hash, retention 2 years. Plan: pipe logs to local syslog collector, redact PII at gateway.

Integration checklist (rough priorities):
- [ ] Local MQTT->HTTP gateway prototype (owner: Citrine devs + Redwood SE (Ava) pairing) — due 2025-02-21
- [ ] Perf runs with Q4_0 on Xavier NX (owner: Diego) — begin 2025-02-11, report 2025-02-14
- [ ] KMS / Vault POC (owner: Nina) — confirm support by 2025-02-18
- [ ] Cache placement decision: in-gateway vs dedicated microservice (owner: Samir) — determine by 2025-02-17
- [ ] Shadow traffic plan and acceptance criteria doc (owner: Ava) — draft by 2025-02-13

Open questions (need answers before pilot kickoff):
1) Can Citrine provide a small rack and power for 2 Xavier NX devices in each warehouse for initial pilot? (Rob to confirm procurement timeline)
2) What is the expected tokenization style for SKU descriptions (many use 6–10 token alphas) — will affect prompt costs.
3) Is there an existing local metrics ingestion pipeline we can hook into for per-request latency tracing?
4) Which endpoints are considered "sensitive" and must never leave on-prem? Need explicit list.

Notes-to-self / friction points to raise with product/infra teams:
- Need a lightweight edge worker build that supports Xavier NX cross-compiled binaries and a small RAM mode (disable heavy tracing). Ticket idea: EDGE-BUILD-384.
- Consider exposing a simple batching-parameter override in the Console per-deployment (ops requested fast tuning without redeploy).
- Add explicit guidance in Private onboarding checklist about Vault HSM integration (customer expected friction).

Communication plan with Citrine:
- Weekly sync on Fridays for first 4 weeks. Quick Slack channel with Ops and SEs for rapid escalations.
- Share a short acceptance criteria doc for shadow testing (metrics + qualitative accuracy checks).

Meeting follow-ups (immediate):
- Send perf-run VM images + runbook to Rob and Mia (Ava) — by EOD 2025-02-07
- Kick off JIRA INF-4821 for Xavier quant perf; assign Diego.
- Draft Shadow test acceptance criteria and sample prompts (Ava + Rob) — share by 2025-02-13

Random snippets / scribbles from site that might matter later:
- "packing assistant should prefer older-stock first" -> add as default business rule in prompt template.
- They mentioned a weekly "freeze" period (Sunday 00:00–04:00) where deployments must not occur. Add to rollout policy.
- Rob: "If the model is slow we will fallback to human triage instead of queued responses" — plan fallback UX accordingly.

End-of-file quick summary: Pilot is plausible but hinges on (a) Xavier memory fit with quantized model, (b) KMS/HSM integration timeline, and (c) gateway stability in jittery wifi. First perf run (Diego) and KMS confirmation (Nina) are blockers.

---
Raw scratch lines (for later cleanup):
- prompt sample for FIFO packing (rough): 
  "system: You are a warehouse packing assistant. Prioritize older stock, minimize weight. Output JSON: {item_id, pick_order, reason}
  user: items=[...]"

- keep rollout: canary at 5% sessions -> 25% -> 100% with automatic roll-back on latency regression >10%

(End notes)

title: Orchard Capital — Integration & POC notes (scratchpad)
preview:
Quick context / snapshot:
- Customer: Orchard Capital (mid-sized asset manager, ~120 employees). Focus: portfolio analytics + regulatory reporting automation.
- Primary contact: Javier Ramos (Head of Data Platform) + Iris Cho (Director, Ops). Security owner: Leena Patel.
- They insist on strong data residency and audit trails; leaning to Private deployment (VPC) or on-prem appliance for first phase.

Call notes (2025-01-21, 60m):
- Start: quick intros. Javier: wants a POC to extract insights from quarterly reports and answer compliance queries in natural language (time-to-first-answer target: <600ms median).
- Iris: concerned about hallucinations — wants confidence scoring + provenance links back to source docs.
- Leena: must have KMS integration (Azure Key Vault) and audit logs forwarded to Splunk. No external egress for raw data.
- Evan demoed hosted chat flow + embeddings pipeline. Showed embedding-based retrieval + citation snippet. Good reaction to streaming responses.
- Priya walked through Dedicated vs Private tradeoffs: Dedicated could meet latency but doesn't satisfy their no-egress requirement. Private (VPC w/ customer-managed NAT or on-prem) looks necessary.

Technical requirements (explicit / implied):
- Private deployment (VPC or on-prem) with optional air-gapped mode.
- Integration with Azure Key Vault for envelope keys; HSM optional later.
- Splunk-compatible audit log forwarder; also S3-compatible object store in their VPC for document corpus.
- Model constraints: prefer an open model we can quantize (license check first). Avoid models that require internet calls.
- Throughput estimate (POC): 50–100 queries/day interactive; burst up to 100 qps during internal testing. Token distribution: most queries short (50–150 tokens), a subset long context retrieval (2k–8k).
- SLOs: median p50 latency target <600ms for simple Qs, p95 <1200ms. Cost target for POC: < $1k/month for infra (proof-level).

Architectural notes / suggestions (scratchpad):
- Use retrieval-augmented generation with embedding store in their VPC S3 + vector DB (we recommend FAISS/annoy inside our private runtime or PGvector if they prefer managed DB).
- Enable prefix/KV caching for repeated queries to reduce cost and improve p50 latency.
- Suggest starting with 8-bit quant model variant + Redwood Optimize recommendations later.
- Telemetry: instrument token-level traces and include linkable snippet pointers to source docs (must persist doc ids).

Gotchas & risks (callout):
- Data egress policy: Orchard explicitly forbids sending raw documents outside their network. Hosted API eliminated unless we can guarantee no egress. Need clear network diagram for security review.
- KMS/HSM: some of their compliance reviewers may require FIPS/HSM integration — check with Leena before committing to a keyflow.
- Model hallucination: Iris wants provenance for every assertion; RAG citations must include source offsets. Might need custom evals to prove reliability.
- Throughput vs latency: their burst requirement could force larger instance sizes; need to size for worst-case test day.
- Legal/licensing: if we propose any LLaMA-derived model, ensure license compatibility with finance usage.

Open questions (assign and chase):
- Javier: exact internal SLA / availability expectations for POC (Action: Javier to confirm target uptime for POC environment) — due 2025-01-28.
- Leena: can Orchard provide a test VPC with egress controls for our install, or do they require fully on-prem? (Action: Maya) — due 2025-01-24.
- Legal: confirm acceptable model licenses for downstream regulated use (Action: Maya -> Legal) — due 2025-01-30.
- Infra: which vector DB do they prefer? (Action: Evan to propose options + perf notes) — due 2025-01-27.
- Telemetry: confirm retention period for audit logs & log format (Action: Priya) — due 2025-01-25.

Acceptance criteria for POC (proposal draft):
- POC demonstrates: 1) answer quality >= 80% on a small eval set (their curated prompts), 2) provable citations with doc id + offset returned for each answer, 3) median latency <600ms for short queries, 4) all data remains inside Orchard network.
- Deliverable: a runbook for deployment + a short video demo + a small evaluation report (precision/recall of returned facts).

Immediate next steps (short term checklist):
- [ ] Send follow-up email with proposed POC scope and timeline (Maya) — target 2025-01-23.
- [ ] Produce a network diagram for Private deployment options (VPC vs on-prem) and share with Leena (Evan) — target 2025-01-24.
- [ ] Draft an eval prompt set & rubric and ask Iris for 20 canonical queries (Priya) — target 2025-01-26.
- [ ] Quick security checklist: KMS, log retention, data residency, encryption-at-rest/in-transit — create doc to drop into Confluence (Maya) — target 2025-01-25.
- [ ] Internal: cost estimate for a small private cluster (4x A10 or equivalent) + vector store + 30-day run (Evan) — target 2025-01-29.

Notes-to-self / meeting prep for next call:
- Bring example of citation format we can guarantee (JSON snippet).
- Have two model options: one smaller quantized model for latency, one larger for quality; show tradeoffs.
- Prepare short list of legal/license red flags for models.

Meeting cadence suggestion:
- Weekly 30-min sync during POC (Tues 10am PT) + ad-hoc security deep-dive with Leena.

Misc (random):
- Iris liked the idea of a "confidence band" overlay on answers (0.0–1.0) + link to supporting paragraphs. Could be a nice UI differentiator for them.
- Javier asked if we can pre-index their quarterly pdfs overnight and keep delta updates incremental — yes, suggest nightly ingestion job.

End of scratchpad.

ACTION LOG (short):
- Maya: follow-up email (2025-01-23) — pending
- Evan: network diagram + infra cost (2025-01-24 / 2025-01-29) — pending
- Priya: eval prompt set template (2025-01-26) — pending
- Legal: model license check (2025-01-30) — pending

-- Maya A.

- [ ] Internal: cost estimate for a small private cluster (4x A10 or equivalent) + vector store + 30-day run (Evan) — target 2025-01-29.

- Have two model options: one smaller quantized model for latency, one larger for quality; show tradeoffs.
- Prepare short list of legal/license red flags for models.

Meeting cadence suggestion:

- Evan: network diagram + infra cost (2025-01-24 / 2025-01-29) — pending

title: Ridgewood Retail — onramp notes: prompt delivery gotchas & ops
preview:
Context:
Ridgewood Retail (mid-market omnichannel retailer) wants an in-store + web assistant that answers product availability, returns policy, and guided troubleshooting for electronics accessories. Primary ask: low-latency product Q&A with an offline fallthrough for kiosk mode. They’re sensitive to PII and customer order IDs — legal wants a private deployment or strict VPC/KMS controls.

High-level constraints / goals (customer stated):
- P95 latency target: <= 200ms for short Q&A (single-turn), P99 < 600ms for multi-step dialogs.
- Sustained peak traffic: 150–250 requests/sec (region: us-west). Weekend spikes up to 400rps for promotions.
- Cost target: keep per-conversation token cost under $0.08 on average (we need to propose model + quantization mix).
- Data control: VPC peering + KMS; no customer data stored in shared buckets.
- Rollout: start in web + api, then add in-store kiosks (air-gapped kiosk mode later).

Immediate technical notes from calls (raw):
- Product descriptions are scraped HTML (lots of markup + sizing tables). These blow token counts because we feed full descriptions. Need chunking + canonicalization before embedding or retrieval.
- Customers refer to SKUs and order numbers; must redact or hash identifiers before sending to hosted inference (or use Private).
- Their search infra uses Elasticsearch; they want to reuse it for retrieval but are open to a vector store for semantic re-ranking.
- They asked about on-device/offline fallback for kiosks — need an on-prem inference image that can operate without control plane connectivity.

Gotchas / pitfalls noted so far:
1) Inflation of token counts from raw HTML + product spec tables -> expensive to embed + serve. Action: strip HTML, flatten tables to short attribute lists, and truncate/clip long descriptions; store full text in cold storage and only include shorter context in prompt.
2) Embedding model mismatch: if we use smaller embeddings (lower dims) for cost, re-ranking quality drops. Need to propose 2-tier embedding strategy: dense 1536 for likely queries, cheaper 512 for breadth.
3) Cold KV cache on first requests for popular SKUs -> high token cost and higher latency until cache warms. Suggest warming policy for top 1k SKUs before promotion events.
4) Rate-limiting on customer side: their CDN occasionally throttles burst traffic; tie Redwood routing to region and provide burst buffer for Dedicated pool.
5) Short prompt hallucinations when retrieval contains conflicting specs (two versions of product text). Add provenance in the prompt and instruct model to prefer latest verified doc.
6) On-prem hardware variance: they have mixed NVIDIA A10s + older T4 hosts. Need quantization profiles per hardware.

Concrete configuration suggestions (Redwood features mapped):
- Deployment: Redwood Private (VPC) for initial rollout; use Dedicated pool for web traffic (predictable committed capacity) and a separate Private on-prem image for kiosks (air-gapped).
- Routing: latency-first routing for web, cost-aware fallback to a smaller model for background re-ranks. Configure automatic fallback chain: rtx-a6000-quantized -> rtx-a4000-ptq -> hosted-small-llm.
- Caching/batching: enable prefix KV caching for session continuity; set max batch latency = 8ms, batch size cap = 32 for generation routes to keep p95 latency target.
- Model/quantization: recommend 1) primary: 13B quantized 4-bit (cost/latency balance) for generation, 2) cheap reranker: 3B QLoRA or hosted smaller model for non-critical queries.
- Embeddings: create two index types: fast-512 index for initial recall, dense-1536 index for top-k re-rank. Store embedding metadata with source date to support provenance-based prompts.
- Rollouts: canary at 5% of traffic for two days; monitor quality metrics (false positive rate) and latency. Use Redwood rollouts: A/B with automatic rollback on error% > 0.5% or latency degrade > 25% vs baseline.

Prompting guidance (examples to iterate with Ridgewood):
- System: "You are a customer support assistant for Ridgewood Retail. Only answer from the provided product facts and policy snippet. If info is missing, ask one clarifying question. Never fabricate order info."
- Retrieval-augmented prompt skeleton:
  - [SYSTEM]
  - [PROVENANCE BLOCK: top-3 sources with date and doc-id]
  - [USER QUERY]
  - [INSTRUCTIONS: if multiple conflicting specs, prefer doc with most recent 'verified' tag; explicitly list assumptions]

- Example prompt fragment (short):
  "User: 'Is the Verge Charger compatible with Verge-Lite phones? I have model V-L2.'
   Sources:
   - SKU-445 product-page (2025-01-10) - specs table: compatibility: Verge-Lite (V-L1, V-L2).
   - FAQ entry (2024-12-02) - note: \"Verge-Lite V-L3 planned\"
   [Assistant instructions] Prefer product-page; answer concisely and include link to returns policy."

Data handling / compliance considerations:
- Redact order IDs and customer email before sending to any non-private endpoint. Use deterministic hashing for traceability if they need mapping later (store mapping within their VPC).
- KMS: recommend KMS integration for private deployment; confirm support for their HSM vendor (Thales) — action item.

Open questions for next call (to confirm with customer):
- Which SKU set is "source of truth"? (ERP vs commerce CMS). We need to know canonical upstream to avoid conflicting docs.
- Acceptance criteria for hallucination/regression: they asked for a sample quality threshold but did not define measurable metric. Propose: exact-match on policy snippets 95% across test set.
- Kiosk offline cadence: how often will kiosks sync content? Hourly or daily? This impacts indexing strategy and cold-start warmers.
- Hardware specifics for on-prem: confirm CUDA versions + driver levels across their fleet.

Action items (owner + ETA):
- Diana: prototype prompt + retrieval template using their sample SKU dump (deliverable: PR with example queries + metrics) — ETA: 2025-01-22.
- Omar: verify Thales HSM support and document KMS config options for Private deployment — ETA: 2025-01-20.
- Priya: create embedding cost/quality matrix (512 vs 1536 dims) with offline eval on their sample queries — ETA: 2025-01-24.
- Ethan: propose Dedicated capacity sizing for 250rps baseline + burst headroom and draft a committed usage quote — ETA: 2025-01-28.

Notes for internal follow-up:
- Pre-warm plan for promotions: need schedule from Ridgewood marketing for next quarter promo window — coordinate with Customer Success.
- Consider a hybrid retrieval approach: keep short canonical product snippets in fast key-value cache for immediate answers and full-text retrieval only for deep troubleshooting.
- Add a check in onboarding to canonicalize product tables -> attribute lists. This will be a recurring ask with retail customers.

Quick rough risks:
- If we go hosted-only to save time, must guarantee redaction pipeline or they will not accept.
- Mixed GPU fleet on-prem complicates quantization and introduces potential perf regressions.

End raw notes. This is a living scratchpad — update after next sync (2025-02-03 scheduled).

title: Optimistic Tool Invocation API — personal sketch
preview:
Quick personal sketch for an optimistic tool-invocation API pattern.

Context / motivation:
- Want a lightweight pattern where the model can request external tool calls (search, DB lookup, code-runner, calculator) but the caller (client or platform) doesn't have to block synchronous LLM response until the tool finishes.
- Useful when: tool latency is variable and we prefer a fast optimistic reply + later patch/update; or client UX can stream a provisional answer and then incorporate factual corrections when the tool result arrives.
- Goal: surface an API that supports: optimistic (speculative) invocation, robust reconciliation, idempotent updates, auditing, cancellation, and simple developer ergonomics.

Constraints / design goals:
- Minimal server-side state for the hosted path; negotiation-friendly for private deployments.
- Default safe: optimistic mode must have explicit opt-in by prompt flag or model-supplied token.
- Security: tools may touch PII/credentials. Auth & scoping need to be explicit in the invocation envelope.
- Cost signals: allow caller to choose speculative strategies to trade cost vs latency.

User story sketches:
1) Chat assistant asks to "look up customer invoice #1234" -> returns provisional reply: "I think invoice was for $X" + tool-invocation id. When tool result arrives, client receives a patch event to patch the chat and mark authoritative data.
2) Assistant invokes code-runner for a quick data transform; client opts to show provisional output, but later receives test results and a new code snippet that fixed a bug.

Proposed API shape (HTTP/REST + streaming hooks):
- POST /v1/optimistic_invocations
  payload: 
  {
    "request_id": "string (client-generated, optional)",
    "prompt_context": "(text) optional - minimal model context snapshot",
    "tool_hint": "search.kb.customer_invoices",
    "tool_inputs": "{...} (opaque json string - validated by platform)",
    "speculative": "true|false",
    "priority": "low|normal|high",
    "max_wait_ms": "int (how long to wait before returning provisional)",
    "callback_url": "optional webhook where results/patches are POSTed",
    "stream_mode": "provisional|none|blocking",
    "fallback_policy": "return_best_guess|defer_to_noop|call_alternate_tool",
    "auth_scope": "scoped-token-id or reference",
    "ttl_sec": "liveness of invocation state on server"
  }

- Response (fast):
  {
    "invocation_id": "uuid",
    "status": "accepted|started|completed|failed|cancelled",
    "provisional_response": "optional text/object - what LLM already returned (if any)",
    "estimated_completion_ms": "int (best-effort)",
    "patch_webhook_registered": "true|false"
  }

- Asynchronous result/patch POST to callback_url:
  {
    "invocation_id": "uuid",
    "final_status": "completed|failed|timed_out",
    "result_payload": "opaque json string (tool-specific)",
    "patches": ["ld+json-patch blobs or custom diff entries"],
    "signed_provenance": "optional (for audit)"
  }

Event stream/websocket variant:
- Open /v1/optimistic_invocations/{invocation_id}/events -> server pushes: provisional, progress, final, patch, provenance, metrics.
- Event envelope fields: type, ts, body, seq. Must be idempotent and resumable (cursor).

Speculative integration modes (implementation notes):
- Speculative=true: server will start tool call and return provisional response immediately (i.e., not waiting for final). Model can generate text that references a placeholder like [[TOOL_RESULT:invocation_id]].
- Blocking/stream_mode=blocking: wait for tool result before acknowledging 200; platform must enforce max_wait_ms to prevent stalls.
- Progressive: server streams partial tool output as it arrives so client can incrementally patch.

Idempotency & dedupe:
- Clients should supply request_id; server dedupes within TTL window. Invocation_id stable for lifecycle.
- Patch messages must contain a monotonic patch_version to apply in order; clients should ignore stale patch_version numbers.

Error handling and reconciliation strategies:
- If final result contradicts provisional: publish a patch that contains a diff and a reconciliation hint (e.g., "replace sentence 2, mark assertion as corrected").
- Provide an audit token (signed) so clients can display "verified" vs "speculative" and link to tool provenance.
- If tool fails, fallback_policy governs: e.g., replace placeholder with "couldn't verify" or call alternate tool.

Security considerations:
- Auth_scope: each invocation must bind to a scoped token with minimal privileges. Avoid letting model craft raw credentials.
- Sanitization: tool_inputs should be validated, rate-limited, and checked for data exfiltration patterns.
- Provenance: sign final_payload with platform key so downstream consumers can verify server-produced outputs.

Resilience patterns / SRE notes:
- Keep per-invocation ephemeral state small. TTL cleanup. Use durable cursor/streaming only when callback_url not provided.
- Expose metrics: invocation_latency_p50/p90/p99, speculative_ratio, provisional-to-final-correction-rate.
- Alert if correction rate > threshold (application-level quality regression detector).

ASCII sequence diagram (rough):
Client -> Redwood API: POST optimistic_invocation(speculative=true, max_wait=200)
Redwood API -> Model runtime: accept, embed placeholder token -> returns provisional_text
Redwood API -> Client: 202 + {invocation_id, provisional_text}
Redwood API -> Tool backend: start tool call (async)
Tool backend -> Redwood API: final_result
Redwood API -> Client (webhook or stream): patch(final_result + signed_provenance)
Client: apply patch -> mark authoritative / show correction banner

Open questions / TODOs:
- How to express "confidence" in provisional_text? numeric score vs heuristics?
- Do we allow tool-driven follow-ups (tool triggers additional invocations) or restrict to single-hop? (risk: loops)
- Authorization UX: how to let users grant per-tool consent without UX friction in hosted product?
- Patch format: ld+json-patch is nice but too low-level for chat messages; maybe define a small "chat-patch" schema (replace/insert/highlight).

Next steps for this note:
- Sketch minimal prototype: server-less flow where a client simulates optimistic_by_echoing placeholder, and a small worker posts patch after delay.
- Wire up a demo: search tool + chat UI showing provisional + authoritative patch. Add telemetry to measure correction frequency.
- Draft a Confluence page with decision matrix for fallback_policy and patch schema.

Notes to self (random):
- Keep the default conservative: speculative=false unless model suggests and client opts in.
- Consider a TTL-based visibility: provisional staleness > X mins auto-hide to avoid confusion.
- We should record cost attribution: speculative requests that later get confirmed vs speculative that lead to extra calls.

Example minimal chat-patch schema (informal):
{
  "type": "chat_patch",
  "invocation_id": "uuid",
  "patch_version": 2,
  "ops": [
    {"op": "replace", "target": {"message_index": 3, "char_range": [12, 45]}, "with": "Corrected text..."},
    {"op": "annotate", "target": {"message_index": 3}, "meta": {"confidence": 0.98, "provenance": "search:kb:invoice-1234"}}
  ]
}

Final: this is a personal scratchpad. Keep iterative, share with Aisha/Mateo before exposing to product.

-- Riley

title: Cache tunneling & cold-start runbook (scratch)
preview:
Purpose:
Quick personal runbook and scratchpad for exploring a lightweight "cache tunneling" strategy to reduce cold-start tail latency when a shared KV cache is cold across tenants. Not formal yet — scribbles, assumptions, and next steps.

Background / hypothesis:
- Problem: when a model instance loses its KV cache (warmup evicted or new allocation), p95/p99 on the first ~20 requests spikes hugely (often 4–8x), hurting interactive experiences.
- Idea: proactively inject compact prefix priming across low-cost background requests (tunnels) that are batched and scheduled during low-util windows. These priming requests are cheap, synthetic, and target popular prefix keys to accelerate real traffic warmup.

Experiment setup (run 2025-01-16):
- Model: open-llama-13b-8k (quantized FP16-ish stack) on a single A100-equivalent instance.
- Workload generator: local script (./tools/arrival-sim) producing Zipf-ish user text arrivals; two modes: 'baseline' (no priming) and 'tunnel' (priming background traffic at 5% of ingress qps).
- Keys: selected top-200 prefixes from production prompt histogram (anonymized).
- Metrics captured: p50/p95/p99 latencies (server-side), first-token time, tokens/sec, KV cache hit-rate tracked via debug counters, and cost delta (proxy estimate tokens * GPU-time).

Raw results (median of 5 runs each):
- Baseline (no priming): QPS 60 steady, first-30 requests after cold allocate:
  p50=48ms, p95=380ms, p99=940ms, KV-hit-rate initial=2% -> ramps to 63% after 60s
- Tunnel (5% background priming): QPS 60 steady, first-30 requests after cold allocate:
  p50=46ms, p95=210ms, p99=430ms, KV-hit-rate initial=28% -> ramps to 72% after 60s
- Observations: p95 reduced ~45%, p99 ~55% compared to baseline for the initial window. Overall token throughput unchanged. Estimated incremental cost ~+1.8% due to priming tokens.

Notes / observations:
- Priming pattern matters: spreading the priming across many distinct prefixes reduces single-key hotspotting and avoids causing larger cache churn.
- Background priming increases early KV hit-rate but introduces small additional jitter; worst-case extra tail events appear when priming bursts coincide with real heavy arrivals — need smoothing.
- Synth tokens are cheaper if we keep them short (1–3 tokens) and avoid expensive attention lengths.
- For multi-tenant fleet, tunneling should be soft-isolated: prefer tenant-local prefixes where available; cross-tenant priming risks polluting privacy-sensitive caches.

Open questions / TODOs:
- What is the minimal effective priming budget? Try 0.5%, 1%, 2%, 5% in next sweep.
- Evaluate on dedicated vs shared hosts (dedicated likely lower risk of pollution).
- Add tracing hooks to attribute which priming key produced a cache hit for which real request (causality).
- Consider adaptive gating: only run tunnels if instance has been idle > X sec and recent cache hit-rate < Y.
- Validate on longer-decoder workloads (16k seq) — priming tokens may be more/less effective.

Quick hypotheses for broader rollout:
- For interactive tiers we can accept a small cost bump (<=3%) to cut p99 cold-start by >40% — good ROI.
- For latency-sensitive endpoints, integrate tunneling into rollout policy as optional pre-warm phase after allocation.

Scratch code & commands used:
- ./tools/arrival-sim --mode baseline --qps 60 --duration 300 --seed 42
- ./tools/arrival-sim --mode tunnel --qps 60 --tunnel-qps 3 --prefix-file artifacts/top200-prefixes.txt
- Quick plot: python tools/plot_latency.py results/run-20250116-*.csv --percentiles 50 95 99

Notes to self:
- Capture heap/kv-size metrics next run; maybe priming helps but increases memory pressure.
- Run a focused 24h simulation to check long-term cache churn behavior.
- Sync with Maya about privacy constraints on cross-tenant priming (INF-4721).

Conclusions (interim):
- Cache tunneling looks promising as a low-cost mitigation for cold-start tails. Significant initial p95/p99 wins with small token overhead. More sweeps needed to tune budget and safety for multi-tenant fleets.

Last lines: rough, personal. Will convert to a proper runbook + experiment PR if the next set of runs are repeatable.

title: Abbrev expansion & casemap: scratchpad
preview:
Context / why I care:
This is a personal scratchpad for handling abbreviation expansion and case-mapping edge cases in the user-text corpus. We get systematic errors downstream in the NER and intent models when abbreviations or mixed-case tokens are treated inconsistently between training labels, tokenizers, and postprocessing. Goal is to propose a small set of deterministic heuristics we can (a) run as a preprocessing pass for labeling, and (b) optionally apply at inference output postprocessing.

Current pain points observed (examples):
- 'u.s.' vs 'US' vs 'U.S.A' in location/entity fields -> sometimes labeled as GPE, sometimes O. Tokenizer splits 'U.S.' into ['U', '.', 'S', '.'] which breaks span alignment.
- 'Acme, Inc' vs 'Acme Inc.' vs 'ACME INC' -> company names normalized inconsistently, hurts dedupe and model recall.
- Measurements: '5kg' vs '5 kg' vs '5-kg' -> unit attached to token changes embedding and label boundaries.
- Title abbreviations: 'Dr.', 'Prof', 'mgr' -> case + punctuation cause label drift for PERSON titles.
- Email local parts with camelCase (johnDoe@example.com) -> casemap decisions change uniqueness heuristics.

Small principles to consider:
- Preserve label-aligned character offsets when possible; prefer heuristics that do not shift labeled spans by inserting characters.
- Prefer idempotent transforms (apply twice -> same).
- Keep a configurable "labeling preprocessing" toggle so labeler UI can show preprocessed text vs original for context.
- Distinguish between canonicalization for downstream dedupe (aggressive) and for model inputs (conservative).

Heuristic candidates (ordered by conservatism):
1) Token-preserving punctuation normalization:
   - Replace repeated periods inside tokens: 'U..S' -> 'U.S'
   - Strip trailing period on known honorifics only if they are standalone tokens: 'Dr.' -> 'Dr' but do not change 'a.m.' or domain tokens.
   - Regex: (?i)\\b(Dr|Mr|Mrs|Ms|Prof)\\.(?=\\s|$) -> remove period.

2) Abbreviation expansion with whitelist (label-aware):
   - Maintain small mapping table (editable CSV) for high-impact abbreviations seen in labeled set: e.g. 'dept' -> 'department', 'svc' -> 'service', 'st' -> 'street' (but see ambiguity with 'st' as state).
   - Expansion applied only if token matches exact case-insensitive token and the expansion does not change length of preceding tokens (to minimize offset drift). Optionally record pair mapping for review.

3) Casefold policies (3 modes):
   - preserve: don't touch casing (default for training to keep original signal)
   - lowercase-only: naive lowercasing for dedupe/embedding index (external, not used for label alignment)
   - smart-casemap: lower-case ASCII tokens unless token contains internal capitalization (camelCase) or is all-caps and length <=4 (likely an acronym) -> keep all-caps for acronyms.

4) Unit detachment for numeric tokens:
   - Insert space between number and unit when unit is in short whitelist (kg, lb, km, mi, ml) but only when token is strictly number+unit (regex: ^(\\d+(?:[\\.,]\\d+)?)(kg|g|lb|lbs|km|mi|ml)$).
   - Record original char offsets in a sidecar map so labeling tools can show original.

Edgecase checklist / unresolved decisions:
- 'st' ambiguous (street vs state abbreviation). Proposal: expand to 'street' only when there is a numeric preceding token (\\d+\\s+st -> '1st' vs '1 st' is messy). For US states, prefer 2-letter uppercase mapping (e.g., 'CA'->'California') only in address contexts. Need more labeled examples.
- Period-in-Abbr vs Ellipses: 'U.S.' vs '...' -> must avoid collapsing ellipses into single dot sequence that breaks semantics.
- Email local-part casemap: should we lowercase full emails for matching? For now, lowercase domain only, keep local part case-preserving but normalizing when it is clearly vanity casing (multiple capitals).
- Company suffixes: ambiguous whether to strip 'Inc', 'LLC' for NER. For canonicalization/dedupe yes; for raw label training no unless label says company spans exclude suffix.

Quick sample mappings (for CSV canonicalization table):
- dept -> department
- svc -> service
- hrs -> hours
- wk -> week
- mgr -> manager
- acct -> account

Labeler UI notes (how this could fit into labeling workflow):
- Add a toggle: "show normalized text" which overlays transformations but preserves original offsets in hover tooltip.
- When a labeler accepts/edits a span after normalization, save both normalized span and original span to the annotation metadata (for downstream regression analysis).
- Add a quick-review queue where transformed tokens that map to a canonical form for the first time are batched for human approval (small daily microtasks).

Small experiments to run (data queries):
- Query: all tokens in labeled set matching ^[A-Za-z]{1,4}\\.$ (likely honorifics) -> sample 200 and see label consistency.
- Query: frequency of trailing punctuation on ORG spans (e.g., 'Inc', 'Inc.', 'Inc,') -> help decide stripping policy.
- Query: cases where tokenizer split acronyms into separate tokens (e.g., 'U.S.' -> ['U', '.', 'S', '.']) and measure label span overlap.

Implementation notes for data-tools PR (#213):
- Add a preprocessor module normalizers/abbrev_and_case.py with unit tests for idempotency and offset map generation.
- Provide a CLI flag --preserve-offsets to output JSONL with original indices and normalized_text.
- Output sample diff file for first-run so labelers can approve bulk changes before committing.

Open questions for team (ping Ethan + Leah):
- Do labeling guidelines want expansions applied in-editor or only as a separate canonicalization step? (Impact: complexity of labeler UX)
- Which abbreviations deserve automatic expansion vs just labeling hints? Need to produce a priority list.

TODO (next steps):
- Pull 500 random ORG, PERSON, LOC spans where token contains punctuation and create review spreadsheet (Ethan to run query).
- Draft small mapping CSV of 50 most common abbrev candidates (Maya to start).
- Prototype normalization pass in data-tools and run against 10k unlabeled examples to estimate delta in token counts and span shifts.

Notes to self: keep transformations reversible where possible (store sidecar mapping). When in doubt, err on the side of NOT changing training text unless we can show label-benefit.

Example noisy snippets (copy for quick paste into review):
- 'Visited U.S. office, met Dr. A. Patel from Acme, Inc.'
- 'order #12345 shipped 5kg package to 123 main st.'
- 'Email John.Doe@Example.com to reach the mgr.'
- 'Acme Inc., LLC'

End of scratchpad. Will iterate after we get the queries back.

title: Avalanche release simulation and tuning notebook
preview:
Context:
This is my lightweight scratchpad for the 'avalanche' style ramp experiments — stochastic, multi-vector traffic ramps where we intentionally vary amplitude and client cohorts to stress fallback and routing logic. Not a final playbook; mostly notes, quick sims, and checklist items to iterate with SRE and SEs.

Goals (what I'm trying to answer):
- How fast can we safely move X% of traffic to model-v2 while keeping p95 token latency within the 10% budget and keeping error-rate delta < 0.2%?
- Which warmup shapes (linear, exponential, random jitter) best expose regressions early without creating false positives?
- Is our automatic fallback (tiered variant fallback) stable when regional capacity hits soft limits?

Quick TL;DR plan idea:
- Preflight: synthetic load + smoke tests against canary cluster for 45m (perf + correctness).
- Stochastic ramp: 0 -> 1% -> 3% -> 7% -> 15% -> 30% with per-step jitter +/- 20% and a randomized hold time (15–45 min).
- Failure heuristics: abort on p95 increase > 12% OR request-error-rate increase > 0.25% (immediate) OR model output-quality regression flagged by sample scorer.
- Postmortem triggers: any automated rollback OR manual cancel + more than 2 rollback triggers in 24h.

Parameters I used in quick sims (local simulator, toy traffic):
- baseline qps: 600 rps global (mostly small tokens, seq avg 64).
- client cohorts: web (60%), mobile (30%), api-batch (10%) — batch has bursty seq=512 occasionally.
- cold-kv-hit: 12% initial miss rate without pre-warm
- batching window: 8ms continuous batching profile
- fallback probability at 75% load: 18% (measured)

Interpretation notes from sim runs:
- Exponential ramps (fast early growth) detected quality regressions earlier, but caused more oscillation in autoscaler decisions.
- Randomized hold times introduced better signal separation for flaky edge cases — lower false-positive rollbacks in 10 runs vs fixed holds.
- Warmup using KV-cache preload (small prefix coverage) reduced early p95 spikes by ~9% in sim. Worth operationalizing as a preflight step (see checklist).

Observability checks to run during each stage (copy into alert dashboard):
- latency_p95_tokens{model=~"model-v2|model-v1"}
- request_error_rate{route="generation"}
- fallback_fraction{route}
- kv_cache_miss_ratio{model}
- tokens_served_per_second{region}
- sample-scoring-alert{sample_set=canary-smoke}

PromQL snippets (quick paste):
- p95 (per model): histogram_quantile(0.95, sum(rate(request_duration_bucket[5m])) by (le, model))
- error rate: sum(rate(http_requests_total{code!~"2.."}[5m])) / sum(rate(http_requests_total[5m]))
- fallback fraction: sum(rate(fallback_calls_total[5m])) / sum(rate(requests_total[5m]))

Checklist: preflight -> in-ramp -> post-ramp
- Preflight (ops + product signoff):
  - [ ] Smoke gen: 3 canonical prompts through model-v2 and model-v1, diff scorer < threshold
  - [ ] KV cache preload script run once per region (small prefixes)
  - [ ] Healthchecks green for 10m (cpu/gpu/mem stable, no OOMs)
  - [ ] Canary tracing enabled and sampling=100% for trace debug window
  - [ ] Quick legal/customer check for any dataset-specific flags
  - [ ] Notify on-call + product slack channel + post brief on #releases

- In-ramp:
  - [ ] Start stage 1 (1%) and annotate timeline in release doc
  - [ ] After 10m baseline, verify p95 delta < 6% and ERR delta < 0.1%
  - [ ] If performance borderline (p95 6–12%), hold + run synthetic cache preload then continue
  - [ ] If ERR delta > 0.1% or quality alerts fire, pause and run sample-set scoring vs canary cases
  - [ ] Keep client-cohort steering enabled (use anchor cohort web users for first 7 stages)

- Post-ramp sanity:
  - [ ] Run 1000 synthetic variant queries for regression fuzzing
  - [ ] Confirm metrics stable for 60m after reaching target fraction
  - [ ] Update rollout config: pin model version in route if approved, otherwise pinback to model-v1
  - [ ] Log rollout in release tracker and schedule 24h health-check review

Pinning strategy notes (short):
- Feature-flag toggles vs route-level pin: prefer route-level pin when we require deterministic behavior for client cohorts (SDK flip slower).
- Pinning metadata to include: model-id, runtime-image-hash, quantization-profile, creation-timestamp, owning-PR.
- Quick command to pin (ops): run `rctl route pin --route <route> --model model-v1@sha256:<hash> --reason "post-ramp stable"` (ops-only).

Rollback heuristics (playbook-ish):
- Immediate rollback triggers: p95 increase > 20%, errors > 0.5% absolute, OOMs or crashed replicas > 3 in 10m
- Soft rollback: reduce cohort by 50% and hold 30m if p95 10–20%
- Auto-rollforward: if pinned route remains stable for 24h with no quality alerts and less than 0.05% err delta, remove pin and allow full traffic.

Open questions / TODOs (for follow-up):
- Should we standardize on randomized hold times across all release flows? SRE sees benefits but product wants predictability.
- Validate sample-scorer sensitivity: current thresholds tuned for hallucination detection; might be too noisy for small cohorts. Quick A/B to run with lower threshold.
- Integrate KV-cache preload into release CI pipeline or keep as manual script? Ops prefers automated, but need safe credentials handling.
- Need a simple script to convert rollout timeline into Slack threaded updates automatically. Copy-paste is error-prone.

Simulation quick log (toy run id: sim-2024-11-05-12):
- stage 1 (1%): p95 delta 2.1%, err delta 0.02% -> continue
- stage 2 (3%): p95 delta 5.6%, err delta 0.04% -> hold 20m for extra cache preload
- stage 3 (7%): p95 delta 11.8%, err delta 0.12% -> soft-reduction by 30% then resume slower ramp (jittered)
- stage 4 (15%): p95 delta 9.2% (after soft reduction), err delta 0.05% -> resume

Notes to self (operational):
- When running these for real, always keep 'panic-button' runbook link in a pinned Slack message.
- Make a minimal 'rollback' CLI alias that reduces traffic and pins in one shot (rctl rollout reduce --pct 0 --pin model-v1@hash).
- Coordinate windows with Dedicated customers in region us-west-2 between 02:00–06:00 local to reduce noisy traffic.

End notes:
This file is for iterating with Priya and Ethan. Next step: take the toy sim parameters above and run 20 stochastic sims with full trace capture to tune thresholds. Then propose a compact policy doc for product signoff (one pager).

-- Maya B.

- kv_cache_miss_ratio{model}
- tokens_served_per_second{region}
- sample-scoring-alert{sample_set=canary-smoke}

PromQL snippets (quick paste):
- p95 (per model): histogram_quantile(0.95, sum(rate(request_duration_bucket[5m])) by (le, model))
- error rate: sum(rate(http_requests_total{code!~"2.."}[5m])) / sum(rate(http_requests_total[5m]))
- fallback fraction: sum(rate(fallback_calls_total[5m])) / sum(rate(requests_total[5m]))

Checklist: preflight -> in-ramp -> post-ramp
- Preflight (ops + product signoff):
  - [ ] Smoke gen: 3 canonical prompts through model-v2 and model-v1, diff scorer < threshold
  - [ ] KV cache preload script run once per region (small prefixes)
  - [ ] Healthchecks green for 10m (cpu/gpu/mem stable, no OOMs)
  - [ ] Canary tracing enabled and sampling=100% for trace debug window
  - [ ] Quick legal/customer check for any dataset-specific flags
  - [ ] Notify on-call + product slack channel + post brief on #releases

- In-ramp:
  - [ ] Start stage 1 (1%) and annotate timeline in release doc
  - [ ] After 10m baseline, verify p95 delta < 6% and ERR delta < 0.1%
  - [ ] If performance borderline (p95 6–12%), hold + run synthetic cache preload then continue
  - [ ] If ERR delta > 0.1% or quality alerts fire, pause and run sample-set scoring vs canary cases
  - [ ] Keep client-cohort steering enabled (use anchor cohort web users for first 7 stages)

- Post-ramp sanity:
  - [ ] Run 1000 synthetic variant queries for regression fuzzing
  - [ ] Confirm metrics stable for 60m after reaching target fraction
  - [ ] Update rollout config: pin model version in route if approved, otherwise pinback to model-v1
  - [ ] Log rollout in release tracker and schedule 24h health-check review

Pinning strategy notes (short):
- Feature-flag toggles vs route-level pin: prefer route-level pin when we require deterministic behavior for client cohorts (SDK flip slower).
- Pinning metadata to include: model-id, runtime-image-hash, quantization-profile, creation-timestamp, owning-PR.
- Quick command to pin (ops): run `rctl route pin --route <route> --model model-v1@sha256:<hash> --reason "post-ramp stable"` (ops-only).

Rollback heuristics (playbook-ish):
- Immediate rollback triggers: p95 increase > 20%, errors > 0.5% absolute, OOMs or crashed replicas > 3 in 10m
- Soft rollback: reduce cohort by 50% and hold 30m if p95 10–20%
- Auto-rollforward: if pinned route remains stable for 24h with no quality alerts and less than 0.05% err delta, remove pin and allow full traffic.

Open questions / TODOs (for follow-up):
- Should we standardize on randomized hold times across all release flows? SRE sees benefits but product wants predictability.
- Validate sample-scorer sensitivity: current thresholds tuned for hallucination detection; might be too noisy for small cohorts. Quick A/B to run with lower threshold.
- Integrate KV-cache preload into release CI pipeline or keep as manual script? Ops prefers automated, but need safe credentials handling.
- Need a simple script to convert rollout timeline into Slack threaded updates automatically. Copy-paste is error-prone.

Simulation quick log (toy run id: sim-2024-11-05-12):
- stage 1 (1%): p95 delta 2.1%, err delta 0.02% -> continue
- stage 2 (3%): p95 delta 5.6%, err delta 0.04% -> hold 20m for extra cache preload
- stage 3 (7%): p95 delta 11.8%, err delta 0.12% -> soft-reduction by 30% then resume slower ramp (jittered)
- stage 4 (15%): p95 delta 9.2% (after soft reduction), err delta 0.05% -> resume

Notes to self (operational):
- When running these for real, always keep 'panic-button' runbook link in a pinned Slack message.
- Make a minimal 'rollback' CLI alias that reduces traffic and pins in one shot (rctl rollout reduce --pct 0 --pin model-v1@hash).
- Coordinate windows with Dedicated customers in region us-west-2 between 02:00–06:00 local to reduce noisy traffic.

End notes:
This file is for iterating with Priya and Ethan. Next step: take the toy sim parameters above and run 20 stochastic sims with full trace capture to tune thresholds. Then propose a compact policy doc for product signoff (one pager).

-- Maya B.

title: Cobalt Labs oncall capture - integration and risk notes
preview:
Context: Quick oncall sync + kickoff with Cobalt Labs (retail analytics SaaS) post-PoC. They onboarded sandbox tokens and saw some unexpected timeouts under burst loads. Primary ask: make hosted inference reliable at ~600 req/min with 100ms tail latency target for short-chat prompts (avg 250 tokens) and provide clear plan for PII handling and request logging redaction.

Participants:
- Riya B. (me) - Redwood SE
- Ethan P. - Redwood SRE (oncall)
- Maya G. - Customer success / Cobalt contact
- Jordan L. - Cobalt engineering lead
- Derrick O. - Cobalt security

High-level notes (raw):
- Symptoms: periodic 5–15s spikes ~1-2x/day during catalog refresh windows. Looks correlated with Cobalt's batch pre-processing hitting bursty external data pipelines.
- Sandbox creds: scoped key used, limited to small model (redwood-compact-v1); they will provision a dedicated key for staging next week.
- Throughput targets: they want steady 600 req/min, occasional 2k/min bursts for 5–10m. Current hosted-account autoscaling didn't bring up enough capacity fast enough in their region (EU-west).
- Latency SLO: 95th < 300ms acceptable for now, long-term aim 95th < 100ms for typical short prompts.
- Tokenization pattern: most prompts are short (<40 tokens) but they attach metadata blobs that sometimes push requests to 250–350 tokens. This increases KV cache churn.
- PII: customer data includes hashed user IDs + occasional raw email strings in metadata. Security asked for explicit instructions for redaction middleware and KMS key rotation cadence.

Concrete requirements (customer-stated):
1) Staging deployment in eu-west region with dedicated capacity or guaranteed warm pool.
2) Failover plan to alternate region with <2s additional latency when capacity constrained.
3) Redaction/transform pipeline example (sidecar or proxy) that removes emails and replaces them with tokens before logging.
4) Audit logs at request-level for 90 days (but redacted).
5) Throughput testing plan + synthetic workload runner from Cobalt's network to reproduce catalog-refresh spikes.

Gotchas / potential risks:
- Request size variance: metadata blobs often include base64 images inlined in early tests — must ensure client-side guidance to avoid embedding large blobs.
- KV cache thrash: model variant selection and quantization choices could raise latency unpredictably when token lengths jump. Need guidance in Optimize to set cacheable prefixes.
- EU compliance: they require that logs containing PII never leave eu-west; our hosted audit pipeline needs to ensure region-bound retention or route to their private storage via Private deployment.
- Billing surprise: burst pricing could blow past their forecast if autoscale happens without pre-warmed reserved capacity. Recommend Dedicated reservation for baseline.
- Observability gaps: Cobalt wants per-request token breakdown and token-level cost in their dashboard; current console can show token summaries but not per-request breakout without instrumental changes.

Immediate actions (who/when):
- Jordan (Cobalt) to create a staging API key and enable synthetic runner by 2025-01-14. (Blocking: get egress IPs to whitelist).
- Ethan (SRE) to run quick capacity test in eu-west using their runner, capture autoscale timeline and failure modes by 2025-01-16.
- Riya to draft redaction middleware example (node + python snippets) and a one-page integration checklist by 2025-01-15.
- Derrick to send specific compliance requirements (data residency + legal language) by 2025-01-17 so we can validate hosted retention policy.
- Product: open follow-up ticket to explore per-request token breakdown exposure in console (GH-PR-4523) — assign to obs team, due 2025-01-24.

Notes-to-self / SE tips to include in draft checklist:
- Recommend setting session tokens and client-side prompt trimming: transform pipeline should drop non-essential metadata before send.
- Use model variants with KV-cache-friendly configs for repeated catalog prompts; group similar prompts into same warm pool.
- For bursts, propose a hybrid approach: reserved baseline (Dedicated small instance) + temporary burst via hosted with explicit cost-guard (budget caps + alerts).
- Provide a short script to compute expected token cost per run given sample prompt set — helps their finance run forecast.

Open questions (unresolved):
- Can Cobalt guarantee they can move metadata blobs off request path (e.g., upload to CDN and reference URI)? Jordan suggested yes but needs dev work estimate.
- Does their legal team accept our hosted redaction + regional retention or do they insist on Private deployment? Derrick will confirm.
- Exact burst profile: 2k/min for 5–10m was anecdotal; need synthetic run to validate.

Follow-ups for next call (tentative 2025-01-20):
- Walk through staged synthetic run results and autoscale timeline.
- Approve recommended architecture (Reserved baseline + burst via hosted) and cost estimate.
- Review redaction middleware patch and E2E demo.

Quick links / snippets I should paste into invite and follow-up email:
- Autoscale telemetry to capture: timestamp, desired_capacity, actual_running_workers, queue_depth, tail_latency_p95, error_rate
- Redaction example (short): s/email_regex/\"[REDACTED_EMAIL]\"/g and then hash user ids with HMAC-SHA256 before sending.

Misc: keep this file light and copy to shared drive under solutions-engineering if we get alignment. For now, keep as personal user notes + action list.

-- end capture --

title: Annotator mental models and fallback triggers
preview:
Notes-to-self: running through how annotators actually think when they see messy inputs. Goal: capture common mental shortcuts + explicit fallback triggers so we can codify into small decision rules and a short adjudication checklist. Useful for new hires + quick adjudication on ambiguous samples.

High-level observations:
- Annotators lean heavily on perceived intent rather than literal tokens when unsure (eg: 'Sent from my iPhone' -> usually signature).
- When text is noisy (OCR or chat logs), annotators prefer to preserve coherent semantic spans and ignore artefactual punctuation.
- People default to: if something reads like a social sign-off (thanks, cheers, best) + a name-like token -> label as SIGNATURE. If there is an email/phone under it -> reinforce signature.
- Quotes/replies: many annotate inline replies as NOT signature even if they contain a sign-off, because it's contextual.

Quick heuristics (practical, low-effort rules to start):
1) Signature strong signal: last 4 lines contain 1–3 tokens that look like a name OR contain 'sent from', 'on my', 'mobile', 'cell' OR contain contact patterns (phone/email/link). Mark as SIGNATURE.
2) Reply block detection: if starts with 'On <date>,' or '>' or contains 'wrote:', treat as QUOTED REPLY context; do not label sign-off inside quote as message-level signature.
3) Bot banners and autogenerated footers: regex patterns (unsubscribe, privacy, mailed-by, do-not-reply) -> AUTO_FOOTER tag and do not mix with signature.
4) Short salutations alone (eg: 'Thanks!') at the end of a message: ambiguous. If there is capitalized token after (Name) -> SIGNATURE, else TONE/EXCLAMATION.
5) Multi-line compound signatures: if there is a line with job title keywords (engineer, product, manager, director) beneath a name-looking line, mark entire block as SIGNATURE_BLOCK.

Edge examples (annotator-intuition notes):
- "Thanks
John" -> most label SIGNATURE; but if preceding text is 'Thanks for the doc. John' (no line break) it's ambiguous. Prefer: prefer SIGNATURE only if newline separated.
- "Sent from my iPhone
On Nov 3, Jane wrote:" -> first line = mobile-footer, next is quote marker; don't conflate.
- Email chains with inline corrections: '—
Best,
Support Team' inside reply are often left unlabeled because annotators see this as intra-thread noise. We should decide whether thread-level labels are allowed.

Hypotheses to test (A/B to validate with small sample):
- H1: A simple last-n-lines heuristic (n=4) gets >85% annotator agreement on signatures for non-quoted messages.
- H2: For OCRed docs, punctuation/noise threshold (>=10 non-alpha chars in last 50 chars) correlates with labeler uncertainty — flag for human adjudication.
- H3: If a contact pattern exists (email/phone) anywhere in the last 6 lines, annotators mark as contact info even if not clearly a sign-off.

Ambiguities to surface in labeling guide (things to write into short 1-pager):
- When to label a line as SALUTATION vs SIGNATURE vs TONE. Create flow: is it at end? Does it include name/contact? Inside quote? If quote -> do not mark as top-level signature.
- Compound footers: advertising vs functional footers vs legal disclaimers. We should annotate as FOOTER_{AD|LEGAL|AUTO}. Team can decide during adjudication whether to collapse them.

Adjudication checklist (1–2 minute):
- Is block at end of message? If no, likely NOT signature.
- Does block contain 1–2 name-like tokens or contact patterns? If yes -> SIGNATURE.
- Is block part of a quoted/reply block? If yes -> ignore for top-level.
- Does block contain job title keywords? If yes -> include title in signature span.
- If OCR noise or short lines: escalate to adjudicator with reason code (OCR_NOISE / AMBIGUOUS_SHORT).

Sample labeled snippets (for quick copying into sample set):
1) "Hi team,
Please review the PR.

Thanks,
Maya Patel
Product Manager" -> SIGNATURE_BLOCK: 'Thanks,
Maya Patel
Product Manager'
2) "Hi,
See attached." -> NO_SIGNATURE (salutation at top)
3) "On Wed, Dec 1, John Doe wrote:
> Thanks
> John" -> QUOTE_BLOCK contains 'Thanks
John' -> do NOT label as message-level SIGNATURE.
4) "Thanks!
—
support@acme.co" -> CONTACT_LINE -> mark contact as CONTACT (not name) and mark preceding 'Thanks!' as TONE only.
5) "Sent from my iPhone" -> MOBILE_FOOTER -> not included in signature span by default, but tag as FOOTER_MOBILE for downstream filters.

Open questions / TODOs (priority ordering):
- Q1: Do we want distinct 'signature' vs 'footer' classes? My instinct: yes (footers are machine-generated and should be removable without losing author info).
- Q2: Train a tiny binary classifier for "is-signature-block" using these heuristics + n-gram features vs. purely rule-based. Start with 2k training samples from past email exports.
- Q3: Add explicit examples for non-Latin scripts (names in Cyrillic / accented characters) — annotator guidance needed.

Operational notes (how to use this doc):
- This is a living scratchpad. After eng/labeler PRs settle, migrate concise rules into the official labeling playbook on Confluence (confluence:labeling-guides/annotator-fallbacks).
- For adjudication sprints: export top 500 high-uncertainty items using the "uncertainty_reason" flag, run through the checklist above.

Random annotator comments captured during sampling sessions:
- "I only look at the last 3 lines unless it's clearly a signature earlier."
- "If I see a phone number I assume signature/contact and stop scanning."
- "Quotes drive me nuts — wish there was a better quote-stripper prestep."

Bottom line: encode a conservative set of rules that errs on the side of NOT changing semantic content of messages (i.e., avoid removing anything that could be part of the message body). Use a separate FOOTER tag that downstream systems can drop if they want to.

Next steps (this week):
- Pull 500 examples with current labeler flags: SIGNATURE*, FOOTER_*, QUOTE.
- Build small adjudication rubric doc (1 page).
- Spike: train rule-based precision test for last-4-lines heuristic and report precision/recall.

Notes end. Keep short examples in the next doc for actual annotator quick reference (1 single sheet).

-- Priya

title: Tidal runbook — launch edits & handoffs
preview:
Daily notes (tidal runbook style) — quick capture, not polished.
Date: 2024-12-02
Owner: Avery Chen (PM)
Context: final pass on hosted API v2 launch PRD edits, coordinate handoffs with infra and SRE, confirm Dedicated capacity gating items.

Morning snapshot:
- Brain dump: small, focused set of PRD changes left (schema examples, telemetry contract, rollout policy table). Need signoff from Rosa on telemetry by EOD.
- Infra flagged: instance sizing note — 4x p4d vs 2x a100 for burst path. Cost model spreadsheet updated (see links).
- UX wants 1:1 example for streaming fallback; doc has only 1:many. Add short snippet.

Quick to-dos (priority order)
1) Telemetry schema: finalize event names + sampling policy — OWNER: Rosa Martinez — due 2024-12-02 (TODO: ask about retaining debug-level traces for 48h).
2) PRD section "Rollout & Fallbacks": rewrite paragraph to clarify canary % and automatic fallback order — OWNER: Avery — due 2024-12-02 (status: drafting).
3) Add streaming fallback example (code) in examples appendix — OWNER: Ethan Park — due 2024-12-03.
4) Confirm KMS/HSM integration note with security — OWNER: Maya Singh — due 2024-12-04.
5) Final README for eng-oncall (one-page) — OWNER: Avery — due 2024-12-05.

PRD edits (notes for final pass)
- Summary: condense feature bullets to three actionable outcomes (latency SLO, cost target, observable events).
- Telemetry: rename "request.trace_id" -> "request.span_id"? Need alignment with observability team. Action: add TODO flag and link to Confluence.
- Performance section: add table mapping sequence length percentile to kernel selection recommendation (quick chart). Use numbers from infra perf run #2024-11-28.
- Rollout policy: explicit canary windows (30m, 1h, 6h) + automatic rollback thresholds (5% error rate or 2x baseline latency). Add flow diagram stub.
- Security: short paragraph on customer key separation for Private deployments; include pointer to enterprise contract appendix.

Stakeholder follow-ups (email/Slack draft snippets)
- To: @rosa.m (telemetry) — "Rosa — quick ask: can you confirm event names + retention? If not today, ping with constraints so I can mark scope. Thx."
- To: @ethan.p (examples) — "Ethan — adding streaming fallback snippet to appendix; can you drop a minimal JS + Python pair by EOD? I can wire up comments."
- To: @infra-oncall — "Heads-up: we might change burst sizing; will post updated cost table + perf regressions. Please watch PROD-752."

Launch checklist (prelaunch -> launch -> immediate post-launch)
Prelaunch (green box = done, orange = needs attention)
- [x] PRD final draft in Confluence (link above)
- [~] Telemetry schema signoff (pending)
- [~] Perf run stability checks (infra) — flagged: burst instance choice (follow up with Ethan/Infra)
- [ ] One-pager for CS + sales (short bullets + limits)
- [x] Legal/finance billing note added to contract appendix

Launch (day-of) quick actions

- Open question: do we enable prefix caching by default for hosted API v2? Pros/cons listed in PRD, need final call.

- When editing examples, keep them <20 lines. Devs hate 200-line snippets.
- Add explicit "what to check" list for oncall: 1) error surge, 2) cache miss rate, 3) unexpected model fallback.
- Remind sales to not promise "zero-downtime" for initial week.

Next steps (today)

- ping Rosa in morning (Avery)
- drop streaming snippet ask in PR comment (Ethan)
- update cost spreadsheet with burst delta (link in Confluence)

- 12:01 PM — Tiny wording change: replace "fallback model" with "fallback variant" across doc.

title: CallPack credit arbitrage notes
preview:
Purpose:
Quick brain-dump on a small "callpack" construct that can be used as a compact credit unit for interactive apps. Focus: reduce friction for tiny/embedded apps, make burst pricing predictable, and close some arbitrage currently visible in sessionized credit schemes.

Context / motivation:
- Many micro-usage customers hate per-token math. They want 'calls' or 'messages' that are easy to explain to non-technical PMs and finance.
- Existing credit schemes (tokens-per-credit) open arbitrage when customers craft very long prompts or extremely long completions. Also cache/prefix behavior complicates unit economics.
- Goal: design a unit (callpack) that maps to an expected compute cost envelope, supports prefix caching discounts, and is still intuitive.

High-level idea:
- 1 callpack ~= budgeted compute for 1 interactive turn with mild completion (eg 512-1024 total tokens traveling through runtime).
- Make the callpack tiered by SLO (latency-sensitive vs throughput-optimized). Lower-latency callpacks cost more credits.
- Provide prefix-cache multiplier: if request hits cached KV/prefix fully, apply 0.25x callpack cost. If partial hit, 0.6x. Encourages reuse but avoids full free lunches.

Assumptions (back-of-envelope):
- Baseline model (open-medium-ish): cost per token served (compute) ~ $0.000006 / token (i.e., 0.6c per 100 tokens) — placeholder, we’ll replace with measured profile.
- Average interactive turn: prompt 300 tokens + completion 700 tokens = 1,000 tokens total.
- GPU throughput pricing amortized + infra overhead -> target gross cost per 1k tokens = $0.006 (rounded).
- UX target price to customers for small plans = $0.02 per callpack (target margin + marketing).

Mapping:
- Callpack A (latency SLO <= 200ms): covers up to 1k tokens, priced at 1 credit -> internal cost ~$0.006, price $0.02 -> gross margin ~70% (before CAC/overhead).
- Callpack B (economy, queued): covers up to 2k tokens, priced at 1.6 credits -> internal cost ~$0.012, price $0.032 -> margin ~62.5%.
- Credit pack bundles for startups: 1,000 credits = discounted to effective $0.015 / credit. (volume commit)

Edge cases & anti-arbitrage rules (proto):
- If single request exceeds the covered tokens, charge overflow at per-1k-token fractional rate (e.g. 0.9 callpacks per additional 1k). No absolute free long completions.
- If average session uses >N turns with tiny tokens (<50 each), apply minimum-charge rule (eg 0.25 callpack per turn) to avoid hammering with tiny requests.
- Prefix-cache discount requires server-side verification of cache hit; partial hits use heuristic based on KV size.

Examples (numerical):
- Customer buys 100 credits at $0.02 each = $2. They use 250 small requests with 80 tokens each: naive per-turn would cost 0.08 callpacks * 250 ~ 20 callpacks -> 20 credits -> $0.40 used. Good. If they instead spawn 10k requests of 8 tokens (micro pings), min-charge of 0.25/turn results in 2,500 credits - protects us.
- Burst scenario: client wants 10 simultaneous low-latency calls that each will be 1k tokens. If on-demand, 10 credits consumed; if dedicated pool (reserved), discount applies.

Unit econ checks (rough):
- Assuming 100M callpacks/year sold -> revenue at $0.02 = $2M. Compute cost (20% of revenue after infra amort?) This needs product-model fitting.

Open design questions / To test:
- Measure per-model true cost per token across popular shapes (1k vs 4k context windows change kernel behavior).
- Decide sensible thresholds for min-charge and overflow multipliers (simulate with real telemetry).
- How to display to end-users: "Calls remaining" vs "Estimated tokens". Simpler UX tends to win.
- Billing reconciliation: show token equivalence per charge in billing UI for transparency.

Notes to follow-up:
- Pull telemetry for typical interactive sessions (median/90/99 token counts) across small customers.
- Run a 2-week A/B where new micro-app customers get callpack meter vs tokens meter to observe activation and churn.
- Sketch API ergonomics: server-side header X-Callpack-Hit to signal cached hits used for lowered charge (security considerations).

Random thoughts / heuristics:
- Consider 'first X calls cheap' onboarding booster: first 20 callpacks at 0.5x to allow exploration.
- For embed customers (button flows), present rate in $/1000 calls to be more relatable.
- Add a 'burst buffer' add-on customers can enable to absorb spikes; priced as monthly fixed + per-burst-call fee.

Unresolved caveats:
- Embeddings + reranking flows mess with callpack definition (single call may spawn N embedding calls). Need to decide whether embedding ops drain callpacks or have separate credit type.
- Multi-tenant cache cross-usage implications for prefix-discounts and security (should not leak).

Next steps (short):
- Grab telemetry (Diego) + run per-model cost calc (SRE/infra) -> replace numbers.
- Draft example billing page copy (I can sketch) and run small user test with 5 early customers.

-- maya

title: canary-rollout-scratchpad-jl
preview:
Quick objective:
- Personal scratchpad for the next hosted API canary (target service: text-generation /v1/generate). Primary goals: validate perf improvements in 1.3 kernel selection, ensure no output regressions on our regression prompt set, and confirm fallback behavior for quantized variants.

High-level plan (sketch):
1) Prep stage (today - dev + infra):
   - Pin candidate model bundle: redwood-text-1.3.0 (kernel:opt-v2). Note: there is a quantized variant redwood-text-1.3.0-q8a that we should keep as warm fallback.
   - Create canary route: /v1/generate?route=canary-rls-2024-11-? (config in routes repo).
   - Add canary routing policy in routing-config: 2% of traffic -> canary for first 4h, then 5% for next 12h, then 10% if stable.
   - Create temporary canary service group on dedicated pool dpool-canary-3 (limits: 8xA10G).

2) Smoke + warmup (hour 0-1):
   - Warm KV caches with warmup script (scripts/canary/warm_kv.sh).
   - Run 50 synthetic requests at distribution bin sizes (avg seq=256, 95p seq=1024). Check latency p50/p95 within target.

3) Observability/metrics to watch (continuous):
   - Latency: p50/p95/p99 request latency (generator-side), tail latency on GPU kernels.
   - Errors: 5xx rate per-minute (should be ~0), decoding errors / truncation warnings.
   - Quality: perplexity proxy + diff-rate against baseline on eval set (eval-suite/canary/inputs.csv).
   - Cost: tokens/sec and GPU utilization (should not exceed warm pool capacity).
   - User-facing regressions: failure to match intent for top-20 prompts in regression set.

4) Traffic ramp plan (automated but manual approvals after checkpoints):
   - 00:00 - 02:00: 2% traffic. Run smoke checks every 15m.
   - If no anomalies for 4h: 5% for 12h with hourly checks.
   - After 16h stable: 10% for 24h and run A/B quality evaluation (A=baseline v1.2.2, B=canary).
   - If quality delta < 1.5% (metric = BLEU/F1-ish proxy used in eval harness) and latency targets met -> start phased promote.

5) Rollback criteria (automatic triggers + manual):
   - Automatic rollback if 5xx rate > 0.2% sustained over 5m.
   - Automatic abort if p95 latency exceeds baseline p95 + 45% for >10m.
   - Manual rollback if quality regression > 3% on the regression set or high customer complaints flagged in #releases.
   - Post-rollback steps: revert route, flush canary caches, collect full traces and core dumps, open INF ticket with logs attached.

6) Post canary:
   - If promoted: coordinate with infra to create new stable bundle tag redwood-text-1.3.0-stable and update catalog.
   - Note pinning: customers who pin to redwood-text-1.2.* will be unaffected, add PR to docs: "how to pin to major.minor.patch" (docs repo).

Notes on version pinning (personal):
- We need clearer docs for semantic versioning of model bundles. Current behavior: major.minor.patch corresponds to model weights + kernel selection + optional quantization tag. Customers can request exact bundle tags via API header X-Model-Bundle.
- Action: propose adding "bundle metadata" endpoint that returns bundle id + supported fallback ids. Add to INF-987.

Checklist (quick):
- [ ] Confirm route config PR merged (infra/infra-routing#432).
- [ ] Create canary slack thread in #releases with pinned checklist.
- [ ] Run eval harness baseline vs canary (eval/smoke-run-<date>.csv).
- [ ] Notify SRE on-call (pager duty: eng-sre rota).
- [ ] Ensure canary logs have enhanced sampling (set log-level debug for canary routes).
- [ ] Prepare rollback playbook (short commands below).

Rollback shell snippets (notes):
- Revert route: curl -X POST "$ROUTER_API/route/revert" -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" -d '{"route":"canary-rls-2024-11"}'
- Drain canary pool: infractl pool drain dpool-canary-3 --timeout 300s
- Flush caches: kubectl exec -n inference canary-cache -- /app/flush_kv --prefix=canary

Open questions / TODOs (my inbox):
- How to surface per-customer pinning in the console? (Product asks for a per-endpoint UI).
- Can we have automatic fallbacks that are less aggressive (e.g., degradations to q8a only after repeated failures)?
- Need to reconcile "kernel selection" vs "bundle id" naming across docs (confusing to support teams).
- Should the canary include embeddings endpoints? Probably not in this week’s run.

Quick reference links (local):
- eval harness: /home/jordan/canary/eval-harness/run_canary.py
- warmup script: /home/jordan/scripts/warmup_kv.sh
- route config PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/routes/pull/118

Final personal note: This doc is a working scratchpad. I'll move a cleaned playbook to shared_drives/engineering/release-engineering/canary-playbooks once the run is stable. For now — keep it minimal, run checks, and capture snapshots at 00:00, 04:00, 16:00 checkpoints.

title: Coffee-break interview riff — Iman Harper (runtime)
preview:
Context: quick internal scratch after 40m loop with Iman (senior runtime engineer candidate). Took notes on a run between meetings — not polished. Goal: capture raw impressions, potential probing follow-ups, and a near-term recommendation for next-step.

Quick take:
- Overall: technically strong, pragmatic, but borderline on systems depth for a senior runtime role. Good pair energy, asks precise follow-ups.
- Hire? leaning towards 'on-deck' not immediate yes. Would like one deeper systems/design follow-up to validate kernel/latency reasoning.

Positive signals (concrete):
- Hands-on: walked through a recent patch they shipped (quantized attention caching fix) with commit-level detail — mentioned tradeoffs between contiguous vs scatter KV layout and memory fragmentation. Concrete numbers: reported 12–18% p99 improvement after tuning batch alignment.
- Benchmark fluency: comfortable reading perf output (nvprof-like tables), could explain why memory-bound vs compute-bound for 4k context on A100 80GB.
- Pragmatic fallbacks: favors graceful degradation (smaller KV cache + early-stop heuristics) rather than exotic async scheduling early in project.
- Team signals: asked about on-call practices, SLI definitions, can riff on rollback strategies. Shows production empathy.

Potential concerns / red flags:
- Architecture breadth: when pushed on multi-tenant routing (cost-aware vs latency-first policy) they proposed a naive priority queue per-tenant rather than discussing scalable sharding or backpressure propagation — suggests less experience at scale. Might struggle to design cross-tenant routing that coordinates cache sharing.
- Sparse on comp-sci fundamentals: slipped on lock-free data structure correctness proof; used heuristic reasoning rather than sketching memory ordering. Could be OK if pair with senior infra lead, but flag if hiring for independent design owners.
- Quantization depth: knows quant-aware ops and per-channel quant but not the nuanced GPU kernel tradeoffs for INT8 on Ampere V3. If we need kernel dev, follow-up required.

Behavioral / team fit:
- Communicates clearly, calm under light pressure, good at drawing quick diagrams on whiteboard.
- Values code ownership and documentation; explicitly called out incident blameless postmortem practice as a must.
- Cultural: decent collaborative bias; asked about mentorship expectations. Seems motivated to ship and coach.

Notes from coding/design probes (raw):
- Prompt: gave an example workload where many short chats (<=64 tokens) dominate but tail has occasional 8k generations. Proposed dual-path serving: micro-batched low-latency pool + background long-run pool with async merge. Pros: simple, maps to capacity tiers. Cons: didn't discuss cache coherency across pools (we pressed — reply was to invalidate at request boundary; may be expensive).
- KV cache sketch: suggested per-shard LRU with approximate size targets. Didn't cover cold-start penalty estimation; when asked about prefilling heuristics, suggested simple warmup from sample prefixes but couldn't quantify overhead.
- Failure modes: noted OOM risk during long tails and suggested circuit-breaker throttles. Good pragmatic thinking, but more concrete SLO math would help.

Questions to follow-up in a short debrief call (if we move forward):
1) Ask them to sketch a design for unified routing that handles cost-sensitive vs latency-sensitive tenants, include how cache is sharded/shared and how fallbacks are signaled. Watch for sharding rationale and backpressure propagation.
2) Small kernel question: ask them to explain how they would implement a fused attention kernel that preserves zero-copy KV access for variable batch shapes. Looking for memory layout choices + concurrency model.
3) Provide a tiny benchmark table and ask them to interpret (we have sample nvprof output from their take-home). See if they detect the likely bottleneck (memcpy vs matmul) and propose mitigations.

Comp / logistics signals:
- Asked about compensation band early; seems flexible but prefers equity/remote hybrid. Open to relocation? prefers West Coast time overlap.
- Notice period: 4 weeks.

Recommendation (short):
- Do a targeted follow-up with a senior infra engineer (30–45m) focusing on routing + cache consistency and a microkernel reasoning check (15m). If they can articulate sharding/backpressure and a reasonable kernel layout, convert to on-site loop. If not, probably fits better at senior IC++ role with mentorship, not as an autonomous runtime owner.

Action items (for me):
- Share these notes with Diego + Ava, schedule 30m follow-up next week.
- Send candidate the reading link we discussed (short doc on backpressure propagation patterns) to seed conversation.
- Flag: follow-up question set and share before call so interview can be a focused probe.

Random scribbles / things I want to remember:
- Liked their phrasing: 'measure assumptions quickly' — good signal for pragmatic engineers.
- Keep an eye on memory-ordering discomfort during low-level kernel discussions. Might be a training gap not fatal.
- They mentioned experience with 'open inference stacks' — ask to list specific repos/tools next call.

End of raw notes.

(Leah) — quick, messy; intended for internal use only.
